## 第 2 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 Abstract: This amendment defines standardized modifications to both the IEEE Std 802.11 physi-
2 cal layers (PHY) and the Medium Access Control Layer (MAC) that enable at least one mode of
3
operation capable of supporting a maximum throughput of at least 30 Gbit/s, as measured at the
4
MAC data service access point (SAP), with carrier frequency operation between 1 and 7.250 GHz
5
6 while ensuring backward compatibility and coexistence with legacy IEEE Std 802.11 compliant
7 devices operating in the 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz bands. This amendment defines at least one
8 mode of operation capable of improved worst case latency and jitter.
9
10
Keywords: EHT, extremely high throughput, jitter, latency, MAC, medium access control, PHY,
11
12 physical layer, wireless local area network, WLAN
13
14
15
16
17

## 第 3 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 Introduction
2
3
4 This introduction is not part of IEEE P802.11be /D3.0, January 2023, IEEE Standard for Information technology—
5 Telecommunications and information exchange between systems—Local and metropolitan area network—Spe-
6 cific requirements—Part 11: Wireless LAN Medium Access Control (MAC) and Physical Layer (PHY) Specifica-
7 tions—Amendment 8: Enhancements for extremely high throughput (EHT).
8
9
10
11
12 This amendment defines standardized modifications to both the IEEE Std 802.11 physical layers (PHY) and
13 the Medium Access Control Layer (MAC) that enable at least one mode of operation capable of supporting
14 a maximum throughput of at least 30 Gbit/s, as measured at the MAC data service access point (SAP), with
15
carrier frequency operation between 1 and 7.250 GHz while ensuring backward compatibility and
16
coexistence with legacy IEEE Std 802.11 compliant devices operating in the 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz
17
18 bands. This amendment defines at least one mode of operation capable of improved worst case latency and
19 jitter.
20
21
Laws and regulations
22
23
24 Users of these documents should consult all applicable laws and regulations. Compliance with the
25
provisions of this standard does not imply compliance to any applicable regulatory requirements.
26
Implementers of the standard are responsible for observing or referring to the applicable regulatory
27
28 requirements. IEEE does not, by the publication of its standards, intend to urge action that is not in
29 compliance with applicable laws, and these documents may not be construed as doing so.
30
31
32 Copyrights
33
34
35 This document is copyrighted by the IEEE. It is made available for a wide variety of both public and private
36 uses. These include both use, by reference, in laws and regulations, and use in private self-regulation,
37 standardization, and the promotion of engineering practices and methods. By making this document
38
available for use and adoption by public authorities and private users, the IEEE does not waive any rights in
39
copyright to this document.
40
41
42
43 Updating of IEEE documents
44
45
Users of IEEE standards should be aware that these documents may be superseded at any time by the
46
47 issuance of new editions or may be amended from time to time through the issuance of amendments,
48 corrigenda, or errata. An official IEEE document at any point in time consists of the current edition of the
49 document together with any amendments, corrigenda, or errata then in effect. In order to determine whether
50
a given document is the current edition and whether it has been amended through the issuance
51
of amendments, corrigenda, or errata, visit the IEEE Standards Association website at http://
52
53 ieeexplore.ieee.org/xpl/standards.jsp, or contact the IEEE at the address listed previously.
54
55
For more information about the IEEE Standards Association or the IEEE standards development process,
56
visit the IEEE-SA website at http://standards.ieee.org.
57
58
59
Errata
60
61
62
Errata, if any, for this and all other standards can be accessed at the following URL: http://
63
standards.ieee.org/findstds/errata/index.html. Users are encouraged to check this URL for errata
64
65 periodically.

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 Patents
2
3
Attention is called to the possibility that implementation of this standard may require use of subject matter
4
covered by patent rights. By publication of this standard, no position is taken by the IEEE with respect to the
5
6 existence or validity of any patent rights in connection therewith. If a patent holder or patent applicant has
7 filed a statement of assurance via an Accepted Letter of Assurance, then the statement is listed on the IEEE-
8 SA Website at http://standards.ieee.org/about/sasb/patcom/patents.html. Letters of Assurance may indicate
9
whether the Submitter is willing or unwilling to grant licenses under patent rights without compensation or
10
under reasonable rates, with reasonable terms and conditions that are demonstrably free of any unfair dis-
11
12 crimination to applicants desiring to obtain such licenses.
13
14 Essential Patent Claims may exist for which a Letter of Assurance has not been received. The IEEE is not
15
responsible for identifying Essential Patent Claims for which a license may be required, for conducting
16
inquiries into the legal validity or scope of Patents Claims, or determining whether any licensing terms or
17
18 conditions provided in connection with submission of a Letter of Assurance, if any, or in any licensing
19 agreements are reasonable or non-discriminatory. Users of this standard are expressly advised that determi-
20 nation of the validity of any patent rights, and the risk of infringement of such rights, is entirely their own
21
responsibility. Further information may be obtained from the IEEE Standards Association.

31
32
33
34 NOTE—The editing instructions contained in this amendment define how to merge the material contained therein into
35 the existing base standard and its amendments to form the comprehensive standard.
36
37 The editing instructions are shown in bold italic. Four editing instructions are used: change, delete, insert, and replace.
38 Change is used to make corrections in existing text or tables. The editing instruction specifies the location of the change
39 and describes what is being changed by using strikethrough (to remove old material) and underscore (to add new mate-
40 rial). Delete removes existing material. Insert adds new material without disturbing the existing material. Insertions may
41 require renumbering. If so, renumbering instructions are given in the editing instruction. Replace is used to make
42 changes in figures or equations by removing the existing figure or equation and replacing it with a new one. Editorial
43 instructions, change markings and this NOTE will not be carried over into future editions because the changes will be
44 incorporated into the base standard.
45
46
47
Editorial Notes
48
49
50 Editor’s Note: Editorial Notes in the body of the standard appear like this. They will be removed before
51
publication. They may highlight some issue that the editor has had to address during the implementation
52
of a change. Where there may be any technical impact from an editing issue, the editor will raise a tech-
53
54 nical letter ballot comment. There is no need for voters to comment on such issues unless they have a spe-
55 cific resolution they wish to present.
56
57
Editor’s Note: Headings with empty content or Headings preceding editing instructions that modify the
58
contents of the referenced subclause are there to provide context to the reader of this document, they have
59
60 no other significance.
61
62
Editor’s Note: The default IEEE-SA style for tables is to "float". This means that they be repositioned
63
later, usually at the head of the next page, to avoid splitting the table and reduce the amount of blank
64
65 space. The table can appear to move out of the subclause it is referenced first from, and can even split a
Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved. 47
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

## 第 48 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 paragraph. This is the intended IEEE-SA behavior, please do not report it as a defect in the draft. In
2 many cases, additional line feeds have been inserted to force tables to follow text, rather than float beyond
3
sequential text. The additional line feeds will be removed before publication, please do not report them as
4
a defect in the text.
5
6
7 Editor’s Note: Line numbering is only approximate. This is a limitation of the FrameMaker tool.
8 Whitespace between paragraphs is part of the IEEE-SA style, as defined in their templates. The combina-
9
tion of these two facts leads to the appearance of blank lines in the draft between every paragraph. Please
10
do not report this as an editorial defect as it is the unavoidable behavior.
11
12
13
14 Tags:
15
16 Tags are placed in this draft near changes to identify the source of the change. Changes resulting from incor-
17 poration of an approved proposal are shown like this: (#<number>), where <number> identifies the submis-
18 sion/revision that introduced that change.
19
20 These tags will be hidden in versions of the draft sent out to letter ballot - i.e., they are present only to assist
21 the editorial review panel in checking that changes have been properly applied.
22
23 Tags are shown close to the point of change. When a whole subclause is new, the heading is tagged.
24
Otherwise, when a whole paragraph is new, the paragraph is tagged. Otherwise tags are placed after a section
25
26 of changes within a paragraph or at the end of the paragraph if the changes are substantial.
27
New tables are tagged in the table caption (if there is one), or in the introductory paragraph. Otherwise, new
28
rows in existing tables are tagged only in the first column, to avoid distraction. Otherwise, a modified cell is
29
30 tagged.
31
Finally, any other changes made by the editor (e.g., for grammar, language, style & consistency with other
32
comment resolutions, baseline, etc.) are tagged (#Ed).
33

1 1. Overview
2

4 1.4 Word Usage
5
6
Insert the following paragraph at the end of the subclause:
7
8
9 Reference in this standard to “STA” means a “STA” that is not affiliated with an MLD unless specified oth-
10 erwise. Reference to “AP” means an “AP” that is not affiliated with an MLD unless specified otherwise.
11
When referring to MLD management, the “SME” is the entity that manages the MLD. A peer MAC entity
12
can be within a STA that is not affiliated with an MLD or an MLD depending on the context. A PeerSTA-
13
14 Address can be the MAC address of a STA that is not affiliated with an MLD or an MLD MAC address
15 depending on the context.
16
17

21
22
23 min(x, y) is the lower of the two values x and y.
24
25
max(x, y) is the higher of the two values x and y.

## 第 53 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 3. Definitions, acronyms, and abbreviations
2
3
4
3.1 Definitions
5
6
7 Change the following definitions:
8
9
10 association: The service used to establish a mapping between an access point (AP) or personal basic service
11 set (PBSS) control point (PCP), and a station (STA) and enable STA invocation of the distribution system
12
services (DSSs) or to establish a mapping between an access point (AP) multi-link device (MLD) and a non-
13
AP MLD and enable non-AP MLD invocation of the DSSs.
14
15
16 authentication: The service used to establish the identity of one station (STA) as a member of the set of
17
STAs authorized to associate with another STA or to establish the identify of one multi-link device (MLD)
18
as a member of the set of MLDs authorized to associate with another MLD.
19
20
21 authenticator address (AA): The medium access control (MAC) address of the IEEE 802.1X Authentica-
22
tor’s STA or the multi-link device (MLD) MAC address of the IEEE 802.1X Authenticator’s MLD.
23
24
25 basic service set (BSS) transition: ChangeMovement of an association by a station (STA) or non-AP
26
multi-link device (MLD) from one BSS or AP MLD to another BSS or AP MLD in the same extended ser-
27
vice set (ESS) that might involve changing operating mode from STA to MLD, or vice versa. See 4.5.3.2
28
29 (Mobility types).
30
31
fast basic service set (BSS) transition: Change of association by a station (STA) that is from one BSS in
32
one extended service set (ESS) to another BSS in the same ESS and that minimizes the amount of time that
33
34 the data connectivity is lost between the STA and the distribution system (DS).A BSS transition that mini-
35 mizes the amount of time that data connectivity to the distribution system (DS) is lost.
36
37
multicast-group address: A medium access control (MAC) address associated by higher level convention
38
39 with a group of logically related stations (STAs) or non-access point (non-AP) multi-link devices (MLDs).
40
41
service set transition: A station (STA) movement from one basic service set (BSS) to another BSS, i.e.,A
42
change of association that is either a basic service set (BSS) transition or an extended service set (ESS) tran-
43
44 sition.
45
46
Supplicant address (SPA): The medium access control (MAC) address of the IEEE 802.1X Supplicant’s
47
STA or the multi-link device (MLD) MAC address of the IEEE 802.1X Supplicant’s MLD.
48
49
50
51 3.2 Definitions specific to IEEE 802.11
52
53
54 Change the following definitions:
55
56
20 MHz mask physical layer (PHY) protocol data unit (PPDU): One of the following PPDUs:
57
58 a) A Clause 17 (Orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) PHY specification) PPDU trans-
59
mitted using the 20 MHz transmit spectral mask defined in Clause 17 (Orthogonal frequency divi-
60
sion multiplexing (OFDM) PHY specification).
61
62
b) A Clause 18 (Extended Rate PHY (ERP) specification) orthogonal frequency division multiplexing
63
(OFDM) PPDU transmitted using the transmit spectral mask defined in Clause 18 (Extended Rate
64
65 PHY (ERP) specification).
Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved. 53
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

## 第 54 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 c) A high throughput (HT) PPDU with the TXVECTOR parameter CH_BANDWIDTH equal to
2 HT_CBW20 and the CH_OFFSET parameter equal to CH_OFF_20 transmitted using the 20 MHz
3
transmit spectral mask defined in Clause 19 (High Throughput (HT) PHY specification).
4
5 d) A very high throughput (VHT) PPDU with the TXVECTOR parameter CH_BANDWIDTH equal to
6
CBW20 transmitted using the 20 MHz transmit spectral mask defined in Clause 21 (Very High
7
Throughput (VHT) PHY specification).
8
9
e) A Clause 17 (Orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) PHY specification) PPDU trans-
10
mitted by a VHT STA using the 20 MHz transmit spectral mask defined in Clause 21 (Very High
11
12 Throughput (VHT) PHY specification).
13
f) An HT PPDU with the TXVECTOR parameter CH_BANDWIDTH equal to HT_CBW20 and the
14
CH_OFFSET parameter equal to CH_OFF_20 transmitted by a VHT STA using the 20 MHz trans-
15
16 mit spectral mask defined in Clause 21 (Very High Throughput (VHT) PHY specification).
17
g) A high efficiency (HE) PPDU with TXVECTOR parameter CH_BANDWIDTH equal to CBW20
18

1 enhanced distributed channel access (EDCA) transmission opportunity (TXOP). It is the IEEE medium

access control (MAC) individual address of the transmitting STA but with the Individual/Group bit set to 1.

5 multi-user (MU) physical layer (PHY) protocol data unit (PPDU): A PPDU that carries one or more
6 PHY service data units (PSDUs) for one or more stations (STAs) using the downlink multi-user multiple
7 input, multiple output (DL-MU-MIMO) technique, orthogonal frequency division multiple access (DL
8

18 ment.

20

22 a Reduced Neighbor Report element or Basic Multi-Link element, describing a reported AP.

24
resource unit (RU): A group of 26, 52, 106, 242, 484, 996, or 2(cid:117)996, or 4(cid:117)996 subcarriers as an allocation
25
of subcarriers for transmission.

27
28 service period (SP): A period of time during which one or more downlink individually addressed frames
29 are transmitted to a quality-of-service (QoS) station (STA) and/or one or more (portions of) transmission
30
opportunities (TXOPs) are granted or allocated to the same STA. SPs are either scheduled or unscheduled.

33 GCR-SP) active at any time.

63
256-QAM, 1024-QAM, 4096-QAM) and forward error correction (FEC) coding rate (1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/6) and
64
65 that is used in an EHT PHY protocol data unit (PPDU).

41 multi-link probe request: A Probe Request frame that is transmitted by a station (STA) affiliated with a

request and response).

50
in 35.3.4.2 (Use of multi-link probe request and response).

52
53 multi-radio non-access point (non-AP) multi-link device (MLD): A non-AP MLD that supports recep-
54 tion or transmission of frames on more than one link at a time.

56
multiple resource unit (MRU): A group of subcarriers that consist of multiple RUs of 26-tone RU, 52-tone
57
RU, 106-tone RU, 242-tone RU, 484-tone RU, 996-tone RU, and 2(cid:117)996-tone RU.

60 nonsimultaneous transmit and receive (NSTR) link pair: A pair of links corresponding to stations
61 (STAs) affiliated with a multi-link device (MLD) for which the receiver requirements specified in Clause 36
62
(Extremely high throughput (EHT) PHY specification) are not met on one of the links when a STA affiliated
63
with the MLD is transmitting on the other link. Each link of such a pair is a member of the an NSTR link
64
65 pair.

61
62
setup link: Between the access point (AP) multi-link device (MLD) and the associated non-AP MLD, a link
63
that is requested by the non-AP MLD in the (Re)Association Request frame and is accepted by the AP MLD
64
65 in the (Re)Association Response frame (see 35.3.5 (Multi-link (re)setup)).

1 simultaneous authentication of equals (SAE) entity: An entity that is a station (STA) or a multi-link device
2 (MLD) that participates in SAE authentication (see 12.4 (Authentication using a password)).
3
4
simultaneous transmit and receive (STR) link pair: A pair of links that is not a nonsimultaneous transmit
5
6 and receive (NSTR) link pair.
7
8 single radio non-access point (non-AP) multi-link device (MLD): A non-AP MLD that supports opera-
9
tion on more than one link but receives or transmits frames only on one link at a time.
10
11
12 enhanced multi-link single radio (EMLSR) operation: A mode of operation that allows a non-access
13 point (non-AP) multi-link device (MLD) with multiple receive chains to listen on a set of enabled links
14 when the corresponding stations (STAs) affiliated with the non-AP MLD are in awake state for an initial
15
Control frame sent by an AP affiliated with an AP MLD in a non-high-throughput (non-HT) (duplicate)
16
physical layer (PHY) protocol data unit (PPDU) with one spatial stream, followed by frame exchanges on
17
18 the link on which the initial Control frame was received.

9 4.3.5.2 Extended service set (ESS): the large coverage network

12 Change the first paragraph as follows:
13
14
The DS and infrastructure BSSs allow IEEE Std 802.11 to create a wireless network of arbitrary size and
15
complexity. IEEE Std 802.11 refers to this type of network as the ESS. An ESS is the union of the infrastruc-
16
17 ture BSSs with the same SSID connected by a single DS. All BSSs in an ESS have the same SSID. All BSSs
18 created by APs affiliated with an AP MLD have the same SSID and belong to the same ESS. The ESS does
19 not include the DS.

24 4.3.16a Extremely high throughput (EHT) STA
25
26
The IEEE 802.11 EHT STA operates in frequency bands between 1 GHz and 7.250 GHz.
27
28
29 In the 5 GHz and 6 GHz bands, the following apply:
30
— An EHT STA is also an HE STA
31

36

37

39
— Support for 160 MHz operating channel width is optional in a non-AP EHT STA in the 6 GHz band
40

42

1 4.3.21 Wireless network management

4.3.21.2 BSS max idle period management
4
5
6 Change as follows:
7
8
For non-MLO, BSS max idle period management enables an AP to indicate a time period during which the
9
AP does not disassociate a STA due to nonreceipt of frames from the STA. This supports improved STA
10
11 power saving and AP resource management. MLD max idle period management is described in 4.3.21.2a
12 (MLD max idle period management).
13
14
Insert the following new subclause after subclause 4.3.21.2 (BSS max idle period management):
15
16
17 4.3.21.2a MLD max idle period management
18
19
For MLO, MLD max idle period management service enables an AP MLD to indicate a time period during
20
21 which the AP MLD does not disassociate a non-AP MLD due to nonreceipt of frames from the non-AP
22 MLD on at least one of the setup links. This supports improved power saving at the non-AP MLD and
23 resource management at the AP MLD.
24
25
4.3.21.23 WNM sleep mode
26
27
28 Change as follows:
29
30
WNM sleep mode is an extended power save mode for non-AP STAs in which a non-AP STA or all STAs
31
32 affiliated with a non-AP MLD need not listen for every DTIM Beacon frame, and need not perform GTK/
33 IGTK/BIGTK updates. For non-MLO, WNM sleep mode enables a non-AP STA to signal to an AP that it
34 might sleep for a specified length of time. For MLO, WNM sleep mode enables a non-AP STA affiliated
35
with the non-AP MLD to signal to an AP affiliated with the AP MLD that all the STAs affiliated with the
36
non-AP MLD might sleep for a specified length of time. This enables a non-AP STA or a non-AP MLD to
37
38 reduce power consumption and remain associated while the non-AP STA or the non-AP MLD has no traffic
39 to send to or receive from the AP or AP MLD.
40

42 4.5 Overview of the services

44
4.5.3 Connectivity-related services
45
46
47 4.5.3.1 General
48
49
Change the first paragraph as follows:
50
51
52 The primary purpose of a MAC sublayer is to transfer MSDUs between MAC sublayer entities. The
53
information required for the distribution system service to operate is provided by the association services.
54
Before an MSDU can be handled by the distribution system service a STA or an MLD is “associated.”

57 4.5.3.2 Mobility types
58
59
Change the first paragraph as follows:
60
61
62 The three transition types of significance to this standard that describe the mobility of STAs or MLDs within
63 a network are as follows:
64
65 a) No-transition: In this type, two subclasses that are usually indistinguishable are identified:
Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved. 65

## 第 66 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 1) Static—no motion.
2
2) Local movement—movement within the PHY range of the communicating STAs, i.e.,
3
4 movement within a basic service area (BSA).
5
b) BSS-transition: This type is defined for a STA or an MLD as follows:
6
7 • aA STA movement from one BSS in one ESS to another BSS within the same ESS.
8 • A non-AP MLD movement from one AP MLD in one ESS, where each non-AP STA affiliated
9
with the non-AP MLD is within one BSS and different non-AP STAs affiliated with the non-AP
10
MLD are within different BSSs, to another AP MLD within the same ESS, where each non-AP
11
12 STA affiliated with the non-AP MLD is within another BSS and different non-AP STAs affili-
13 ated with the non-AP MLD are within different BSSs.
14 • A non-AP MLD movement from one AP MLD in one ESS, where each non-AP STA affiliated
15
with the non-AP MLD is within one BSS and different non-AP STAs affiliated with the non-AP
16
MLD are within different BSSs, to another BSS within the same ESS and becoming a non-AP
17
18 STA, where the MLD MAC address of the non-AP MLD is the same as the MAC address of the
19 non-AP STA.
20
• A non-AP STA movement from one BSS in one ESS to an AP MLD within the same ESS and
21
becoming a non-AP MLD, where each non-AP STA affiliated with the non-AP MLD is within
22
23 another BSS, different non-AP STAs affiliated with the non-AP MLD are within different BSSs
24 and the MAC address of the non-AP STA is the same as the MLD MAC address of the non-AP
25 MLD.
26
A fast BSS transition is a BSS transition that establishes the state necessary for data connectivity
27
28 before the reassociation rather than after the reassociation.
29
c) ESS-transition: This type is defined as STA movement from a BSS in one ESS to a BSS in a
30
different ESS. This case is supported only in the sense that the STA might move. Maintenance of
31
32 upper-layer connections cannot be guaranteed by IEEE Std 802.11; in fact, disruption of service is
33 likely to occur.
34
35
Move the following third paragraph as the first paragraph of this subclause:
36
37
38 The different association services support the different categories of mobility.
39
40
4.5.3.3 Association
41
42
43 Change the first three paragraphs as follows:
44
45
To deliver an MSDU within an ESS via the DS, the DS needs to know which AP or AP MLD within the ESS
46
to deliver the MSDU, so that the MSDU might ultimately be delivered to the addressed IEEE 802.11 non-
47
48 AP STA or non-AP MLD. This information is provided to the DS by the concept of association. Association
49 is necessary, but not sufficient, to support BSS-transition mobility. Association is sufficient to support no-
50 transition mobility. Association is one of the services in the DSS.
51
52
Before a non-AP STA or a non-AP MLD is allowed to senddeliver an MSDU via an AP or an AP MLD,
53
54 respectively, it first becomes associated with the AP or the AP MLD, respectively.
55
56
For a non-GLK STA that is not affiliated with an MLD, the act of becoming associated with an AP invokes
57
the association service, which provides the STA to AP mapping to the DS. For a non-AP MLD, the act of
58
59 becoming associated with an AP MLD invokes the association service (see 11.3 (STA
60 authenticationAuthentication and association)), which provides the non-AP MLD to AP MLD mapping to
61 the DS. How the information provided by the association service is stored and managed within the DS is not
62
specified by this standard.
63
64
65

## 第 67 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 Change the fifth paragraph as follows:
2
3
Within a robust security network (RSN), association is handled differently. In an RSNA, the IEEE 802.1X
4
5 Port determines when to allow data traffic across an IEEE 802.11 link between two STAs or multiple IEEE
6 802.11 links between two MLDs. A single IEEE 802.1X Port maps to one association, and each association
7 maps to an IEEE 802.1X Port. An IEEE 802.1X Port consists of an IEEE 802.1X Controlled Port and an
8
IEEE 802.1X Uncontrolled Port. The IEEE 802.1X Controlled Port is blocked from passing general data
9
traffic between two STAs or between two MLDs until an IEEE 802.1X authentication procedure completes
10
11 successfully over the IEEE 802.1X Uncontrolled Port. Once the AKM completes successfully, data
12 protection is enabled to prevent unauthorized access, and the IEEE 802.1X Controlled Port unblocks to
13 allow protected data traffic. IEEE 802.1X Supplicants and Authenticators exchange protocol information via
14
the IEEE 802.1X Uncontrolled Port. It is expected that most other protocol exchanges use the IEEE 802.1X
15
Controlled Ports. However, a given protocol might need to bypass the authorization function and make use
16
17 of the IEEE 802.1X Uncontrolled Port.
18
19 Change the seventh, eighth, and ninth paragraphs as follows:
20
21
22 At any given instant, a non-AP STA is associated with no more than one AP, and a non-AP MLD is
23 associated with no more than one AP MLD. This allows the DS to determine a unique answer to the
24 questions, “Which AP is serving non-AP STA X?” and “Which AP MLD is serving non-AP MLD X?” Once
25
an association is completed between a non-AP STA and an AP, a non-AP STA can make full use of a DS
26
(via the AP) to communicate. Similarly, once an association is completed between a non-AP MLD and an
27
28 AP MLD, a non-AP MLD can make full use of a DS (via the AP MLD) to communicate. Association
29 between a non-AP STA and an AP is always initiated by the non-AP STA, not the AP. Association between
30 a non-AP MLD and an AP MLD is always initiated by the non-AP MLD, not the AP MLD.
31
32
33 An AP or an AP MLD might be associated with many non-AP STAs or non-AP MLDs, respectively, at the
34 same time.
35
36
A non-AP STA or a non-AP MLD learns what APs or AP MLDs, respectively, are present and what opera-
37
38 tional capabilities are available from each of those APs or AP MLDs and APs affiliated with each AP MLD,
39 respectively, and then invokes the association service to establish an association with an AP or an AP MLD,
40 respectively. A FILS STA is able to discover, authenticate and associate with the AP with a reduced number
41
of frame transmissions. For details of how a STA learns about what APs are present, see 11.1.4 (Acquiring
42
synchronization, scanning).
43
44

Association is sufficient for no-transition MSDU delivery between IEEE 802.11 STAs or MLDs. Additional
51
functionality is needed to support BSS-transition mobility. The additional required functionality is provided
52
53 by the reassociation service. Reassociation is one of the services in the DSS.

59

61 association)) of a non-AP STA with an AP from onethe AP to the same AP or another AP or.
62
— a current association (see 4.5.3.3 (Association) and 11.3 (STA authenticationAuthentication and
63
association)) of a non-AP MLD with an AP MLD from the AP MLD to the same AP MLD or
64
65 another AP MLD or

1 — a current association of a non-AP STA with an AP to an association of a non-AP MLD with an AP
2 MLD, where the MAC address of the non-AP STA is the same as the MLD MAC address of the non-
3
AP MLD or
4

7
AP STA.
8
9
10 In an ESS with a DS, the reassociation service informs the DS of the current mapping between AP and non-
11
AP STA or between AP MLD and non-AP MLD as the STA moves from BSS to BSS within the ESS. For a
12
general link in an IEEE 802.1Q network, the reassociation service informs higher layer services how the link
13
14 is reconfigured, commonly, with which BSS the GLK non-AP STA is a member of. The higher layer
15 services will then destroy, disable, or maintain the existing Internal Sublayer Service SAPs, create or enable
16 new Internal Sublayer Service SAPs, inform the GLK convergence function of the reconfigured general link
17
mapping of the Internal Sublayer Service SAPs, and inform the network routing protocol of the updated
18
general link. The GLK AP and GLK non-AP STA each then establish or maintain a
19
20 service_access_point_identifier for the reconfigured general link, for their respective MS SAPs.
21 Reassociation also enables changing association attributes of an established association while the non-AP
22 STA or non-AP MLD remains associated with the same AP or the same AP MLD, respectively.
23
Reassociation is always initiated by the non-AP STA or the non-AP MLD.
24
25
26 Change the last paragraph as follows:
27
28
29 Only the fast BSS transition facility can move an RSNA during reassociation. Therefore, if FT is not used,
30 the old RSNA is deleted and a new RSNA is constructed.

4.5.3.5 Disassociation
33
34
35
Change the second paragraph as follows:
36
37
38 For a non-GLK STA that is not affiliated with an MLD, the act of becoming disassociated invokes the
39
disassociation service, which voids any existing non-AP STA to AP mapping known to the DS, for the
40
disassociating non-AP STA. For a non-AP MLD, the act of becoming disassociated invokes the
41
42 disassociation service, which voids any existing non-AP MLD to AP MLD mapping known to the DS, for
43 the disassociating non-AP MLD (see 35.3.5.3 (Multi-link tear down procedure)).
44
45
Change the fourth, fifth, and sixth paragraphs as follows:
46
47
48
The disassociation service can be invoked by either party in an association between a non-AP STA and an
49
AP (non-AP STA or APsee 4.5.3.3 (Association)) or an association between a non-AP MLD and an AP
50
51 MLD). Disassociation is a notification, not a request. Disassociation cannot be refused by the receiving STA
52 or the receiving MLD except when management frame protection is negotiated and the message integrity
53 check fails.
54
55
56 An AP or an AP MLD can disassociate non-AP STAs or non-AP MLDs, respectively, to enable the AP or
57 the AP MLD to be removed from a network for service or for other reasons.
58
59
60 STAs or MLDs attempt to disassociate when they leave a network. However, the MAC protocol does not
61 depend on STAs or MLDs invoking the disassociation service. (MAC management is designed to
62 accommodate loss of communication with an associated STA or an associated MLD.)
63

## 第 69 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 4.5.6 Traffic differentiation and QoS support
2
3
Insert the following new subclause at the end of subclause 4.5.6:
4
5
6 4.5.6.3 Support for predictable latency
7
8
Traffic originating from many real time applications has stringent requirements in terms of latency and its
9
10 jitter along with certain reliability constraints. Such traffic is referred to as latency sensitive traffic. Latency
11 sensitive traffic requires packets to be delivered with predictable latency in terms of both its average and the
12 worst case values over the wireless link. Such traffic typically shows periodic pattern with burst arrival of
13
packets in each interval.
14
15
16 This standard defines mechanism(s) such as R-TWT (35.8 (Restricted TWT (R-TWT))) to enable the BSS to
17 use enhanced medium access protection and resource reservation to provide predictable latency with higher
18 reliability for latency sensitive traffic over the wireless link.
19
20
21 Insert the following new subclause at the end of subclause 4.5 (Overview of the services):
22
23
4.5.13 EPCS priority access
24
25
26 Existing national security and emergency preparedness communication services1 in multiple countries pro-
27 vide priority for voice and data exchanges on public networks. EPCS priority access is intended to provide
28 capabilities to support such priority services on IEEE 802.11-based networks2.
29
30
EPCS priority access provides prioritized access to system resources for authorized devices to increase their
31
32 probability of successful communication during periods of network congestion. Priority access involves
33 treating the EPCS traffic with a higher priority, as described in 35.16.3 (EPCS priority access procedure), in
34 obtaining channel access and in allocation of network resources. The service is only available to designated,
35
authorized devices that normally represent a small fraction of the overall number of devices operating in the
36
area.
37
38
39 An AP MLD that has EPCS priority access activated advertises this capability in Beacon and Probe
40 Response frames. The AP MLD authorizes a non-AP MLD to use EPCS priority access based on locally
41
available information (which can be obtained a priori from an NS/EP service provider) or using online
42
information obtained through a service provider’s authorization infrastructure, which might be accessed via
43
44 an SSPN interface (see 11.22.5 (Interworking procedures: interaction with SSPN)). The AP MLD might
45 cache authorization information locally to enable subsequent verification and use it to confirm authority
46 during (re)association with a non-AP MLD.
47
48
49 An AP MLD or a non-AP MLD invokes EPCS priority access on-demand when instructed to do so by a
50 higher layer function, such as an authorized user or a managed service provider that detects the need for pri-
51 ority. The process for detecting the need for EPCS priority access by the higher layer function is outside the
52
scope of this standard.
53
54
55 A non-AP MLD enables EPCS priority access by sending an EPCS Priority Access Enable Request frame
56 (see 9.6.35.5 (EPCS Priority Access Enable Request frame format)) to an associated AP MLD that adver-
57 tises the capability. A non-AP MLD can send the request on any available enabled link between the non-AP
58
MLD and the AP MLD. The AP MLD authorizes the non-AP MLD using either locally stored verification
59
60
61 1For example, EPCS in the United States is called National Security and Emergency Preparedness (NS/EP) service that includes the
62 Government Emergency Telecommunications Service and the Wireless Priority Service and run on commercial wireline and cellular
63 networks, respectively.
64 2Priority access capabilities to support these services in other types of networks are defined in appropriate international standards, (e.g.,
65 (the Multimedia Priority Service (MPS) was defined in 3GPP for use in wireless cellular networks).
Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved. 69
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 70 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 information or information obtained from an EPCS service provider via other means, such as the SSPN
2 interface (see 11.22.5 (Interworking procedures: interaction with SSPN)) and sends an EPCS Priority
3
Access Enable Response frame (see 9.6.35.6 (EPCS Priority Access Enable Response frame format)) to the
4
non-AP MLD. If the non-AP MLD is authorized, EPCS priority access will be enabled on all links the non-
5
6 AP MLD has set up with the AP MLD. Alternatively, the AP MLD can enable EPCS priority access by
7 sending an unsolicited EPCS Priority Access Enable Request frame (see 9.6.35.5 (EPCS Priority Access
8 Enable Request frame format)) to a non-AP MLD and the non-AP MLD confirms the request by sending an
9
EPCS Priority Access Enable Response frame. An AP MLD can send the request on any available enabled
10
link between the AP MLD and non-AP MLD and EPCS priority access will be enabled on all links within
11
12 the MLD.
13
14
While the EPCS priority access is enabled, all traffic to and from the non-AP MLD is treated with a higher
15
priority, as described in 35.16.3 (EPCS priority access procedure). EPCS priority access is applied individu-
16
17 ally for each link within an MLD. Either the AP MLD or the non-AP MLD can tear down EPCS priority
18 access by transmitting an EPCS Priority Access Teardown frame (see 9.6.35.7 (EPCS Priority Access Tear-
19 down frame details)).
20
21
22
4.9 Reference model
23
24
25 4.9.4 Reference model for multi-band operation
26
27
Change the second paragraph as follows:
28
29
30 A multi-band capable device can manage operation over more than one frequency band/channel using trans-
31
parent FST and nontransparent FST. The operation across the different frequency bands/channels using
32
transparent FST and nontransparent FST can be simultaneous or nonsimultaneous.
33
34
35 Insert the following new subclause at the end of subclause 4.9 (Reference model):
36
37
4.9.6 Reference model for multi-link operation (MLO)
38
39
40 MLO defines a set of procedures allowing communication over multiple links between MLDs. An MLD
41
manages such communication over multiple links. Communication across links using different frequency
42
bands or channels can occur simultaneously or not depending on the capabilities of both the AP MLD and
43
44 the non-AP MLD (see 35.3.16.3 (Simultaneous transmit and receive (STR) operation) and 35.3.16.4
45 (Nonsimultaneous transmit and receive (NSTR) operation)).
46
47
The MLO procedures (see 35.3 (Multi-link operation)) allow a pair of MLDs to discover, synchronize,
48
49 (de)authenticate, (re)associate, disassociate, and manage links and other resources with each other on any
50 common bands or channels that are supported by both MLDs.
51
52
Each MLD has a single MAC-SAP. Each AP affiliated with an AP MLD has a MAC address different from
53
54 any other AP affiliated with the AP MLD, and each non-AP STA affiliated with a non-AP MLD has a MAC
55 address different from any other non-AP STA affiliated with the non-AP MLD.
56
57
An example of an AP MLD with two affiliated APs (Link 1 and Link 2) is shown in Figure 4-30a (Example
58
59 MLD and the affiliated STA communication system). The figure shows an AP MLD with MLD MAC
60 address M and the MLD lower MAC sublayers of two affiliated APs (AP1 with MAC address w and AP2
61 with MAC address x). The AP MLD is associated with a non-AP MLD with MLD MAC address P and the
62
MLD lower MAC sublayers of two affiliated STAs (STA1 with MAC address y and STA2 with MAC
63
address z) are shown. Link 1 is established between AP1 and STA1 and link 2 is established between AP2
64
65 and STA2. In general, the MAC address of an MLD and the MAC addresses of the STAs affiliated with the
70 Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved.

1 MLD are all different (e.g., M, P, w, x, y, and z have different values). However, the architecture supports an
2 implementation where M could equal either w or x, and where P could equal y or z.

26 (i.e., an AP1 affiliated with an AP MLD or
27 a STA1 affiliated with a non-AP MLD (e.g., in 2.4 GHz))
28
Figure 4-30b—Reference model for an MLD for two links
29
30
31
An MLD supports multiple MAC functions, coordinated by an SME.
32
33
34 The SME maintains the authentication and association states. The Authenticator and the MAC-SAP of the
35 AP MLD are identified by the same AP MLD MAC address. The Supplicant and the MAC-SAP of the non-
36
AP MLD are identified by the same non-AP MLD MAC address.
37
38
39 The SME is responsible for coordinating the MLD and each of the affiliated STAs through the MLME, and
40 to maintain an RSNA key management entity and IEEE 802.1X Authenticator or Supplicant in each upper
41 MAC sublayer component, for MLO.
42
43
The MAC Sublayer is further divided into an MLD upper MAC sublayer and an MLD lower MAC sublayer.
44
45 The MLD upper MAC sublayer performs functionalities that are common across all links, and the MLD
46 lower MAC sublayer (shared with an AP or non-AP STA affiliated with the MLD) performs functionalities
47 that are local to each link. Some of the functionalities require joint processing of both the MLD upper MAC
48
sublayer and the MLD lower MAC sublayer.
49
50
51 An AP MLD always operates in cooperation with one or more affiliated APs, one for each link. The MLD
52 lower MAC sublayer components implement link specific functions that operate independently of the lower
53 MAC in other affiliated APs. Use of these MLD lower MAC functions is shared by the AP MLD’s upper
54
MAC sublayer, and the affiliated AP’s upper MAC sublayer (see Figure 4-30c (High level architecture for
55
AP MLD with affiliated APs)). Some behaviors of MLO require the use of one or more affiliated APs’ upper
56
57 MAC components. In particular, the affiliated AP MLD upper MAC sublayer components support group
58 addressed traffic, )and any group or individually addressed traffic to or from any (non-MLO) non-AP STAs.
59
60
61
62
63
64
65

The non-AP MLD reference model includes the MLD upper MAC sublayer and MLD lower MAC sublayers
50
51 (one for each link). The single upper MAC within a non-AP MLD can operate at any given time in either

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 5. MAC service definition
2
3
4 5.1 Overview of MAC services
5
6
5.1.5 MAC data service architecture
7
8
9 5.1.5.1 General
10
11 Insert the following paragraphs at the end of this subclause:
12
13
14 For MLO, one or more links are used for communication between the AP MLD and non-AP MLD after
15 MLD (re)setup as described in 35.3.5 (Multi-link (re)setup). The MAC data plane architecture with n links
16 (i.e., processes that involve transport of all or part of an MSDU) is shown in Figure 5-2a (MAC data plane
17
architecture (MLO) for unicast data frames)).
18
19
20 During transmission, an MSDU from the MAC SAP goes through the processes shown in the left hand side
21 of Figure 5-2a (MAC data plane architecture (MLO) for unicast data frames), then through the TID-to-link
22 mapping process (see 35.3.7.1 (TID-to-link mapping)) that forwards the MPDUs down to one of the MLD
23
lower MAC sublayers and then to the corresponding PHY SAP.
24
25
26 NOTE 1—TID-to-link mapping negotiation between peer MLDs is an optional feature.
27
28 During reception, MPDUs originating from different PHY SAPs first go through an MLD lower MAC
29
sublayer, followed by a merging process (first-in first-out), and then go through the rest of the processes in
30
the right-hand side of Figure 5-2a (MAC data plane architecture (MLO) for unicast data frames). Then, one
31
32 or more MSDUs are delivered to the LLC sublayer via the MAC SAP or, via the DSAF to the DS. The IEEE
33 802.1X Controlled/Uncontrolled Ports discard any received MSDUs if the Controlled Port is not enabled
34 and if the MSDU does not represent an IEEE 802.1X frame.
35
36
37 NOTE 2—Many of the processes shown in Figure 5-2a (MAC data plane architecture (MLO) for unicast data frames)
38 also apply to MLD-level MMPDU flows for the MAC control plane architecture, and the processes shown at the MLD
39 lower MAC sublayer also apply to Control and Extension frames.
40
41 When MLO is being used, the same security association (PTKSA) is used to encrypt the unicast MPDUs and
42 MMPDUs prior to transmission on any of the links. The same security association (PTKSA) is used to
43
decrypt the unicast MPDUs and MMPDUs received on any of the links.
44
45
For an AP MLD to support group addressed transmissions and also non-MLO peer STA associations,
46
47 Figure 5-2a (MAC data plane architecture (MLO) for unicast data frames) is combined with n affiliated APs,
48 within a structure as shown in Figure 4-30c (High level architecture for AP MLD with affiliated APs). The
49 affiliated APs’ upper MAC sublayer components are the same as those for the AP MLD, but handle group
50
addressed security associations (GTK, IGTK, and BIGTK), and handle traffic to and from associated non-
51
AP STAs (not operating in MLO) with single link security associations for pairwise transient keys (PTKs).
52
53 The overall structure is as shown in Figure 5-2b (MAC data plane architecture for AP MLD and affiliated
54 APs).

50 2TID-to-link mapping controls which link an MPDU can be transmitted.
51
52
Figure 5-2b—MAC data plane architecture for AP MLD and affiliated APs
53
54
55
56
An additional function is added for data MPDU reception to distribute the MPDUs to the appropriate MLD
57
upper MAC sublayer based on the type of association with the peer, which is tracked per TA. MPDUs
58
59 received over any link from a non-AP STA affiliated with a non-AP MLD are delivered to the AP MLD
60 upper MAC, and other MPDUs are delivered to the affiliated AP upper MAC for that link.
61
62
Group addressed MSDUs at the DS are not transmitted directly by affiliated APs. Instead, the AP MLD
63
receives group addressed MSDUs and assigns a sequence number prior to distributing the group addressed
64
65 frames to the affiliated APs for transmission. The AP MLD and affiliated APs then coordinate to buffer (if
Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved. 77
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.

## 第 78 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 appropriate), assign packet numbers, and encrypt the resulting MPDU in the individual affiliated APs’
2
stacks. The GTK of an affiliated AP is used to encrypt the group addressed MPDUs and MMPDUs prior to
3
transmission on the link managed by that affiliated AP. The GTK of the corresponding affiliated STA is used
4
5 to decrypt the group addressed MPDUs and MMPDUs received on a link. Group addressed MMPDUs
6 generated within the AP MLD upper MAC sublayer are transferred to the intended affiliated APs for
7 transmission.
8
9
The MLD upper MAC sublayer functions include:
10
11 — Authentication, association, and reassociation (between an AP MLD and a non-AP MLD)
12
— Security association (e.g., PMKSA, PTKSA) and distribution of GTK/IGTK/BIGTK
13
14 — SN/PN assignment for frames to be encrypted by PTK for unicast frames
15
— SN assignment for group addressed MSDUs
16
17 — Power save buffering of individually addressed frames (only on AP MLD)
18
— Encryption/decryption using PTK for unicast frames
19
20 — Selection of the MLD lower MAC sublayer for transmission (TID-to-link mapping (see 35.3.7.1
21 (TID-to-link mapping)))
22
23 — Merging reception of MPDUs from two or more links
24 — Reordering of packets to ensure in-order delivery per each Block Ack session
25
26 — Block Ack scoreboarding for individually addressed frames (in collaboration with the MLD lower
27 MAC sublayer). Optionally, the MLD upper MAC sublayer delivers successful status records of
28 MPDUs and/or scoreboard context control information on Block Ack scoreboarding at one of the
29
setup links to other setup links
30
31 — MLD level management information exchange/indication via the MLD lower MAC sublayer
32
— Coordination of distribution and management of EDCA parameters across the MLD lower MAC
33
sublayers of the links
34
35
36 The affiliated (non-MLO) upper MAC sublayer functions (only on AP) include:
37
— Non-MLO peer operations, above the MLD lower MAC sublayer
38
39 — Maintenance of link specific GTK/IGTK/BIGTK (between an AP affiliated with the AP MLD and a
40 non-AP STA affiliated with the non-AP MLD)
41
— Link specific encryption/decryption/integrity protection and PN assignment using GTK/IGTK/
42
43 BIGTK (between an AP affiliated with the AP MLD and a non-AP STA affiliated with the non-AP
44 MLD)
45
— Link-specific management info exchange/indication (e.g., Beacon)
46
47 — Power save buffering of group addressed frames
48
49
The MLD lower MAC sublayer functions include:
50
51 — Link specific control information exchange/indication (e.g., RTS/CTS, acknowledgements, NDP,
52 etc.)
53
— Power save state and mode
54
55 — MAC address filtering for frame reception
56
— Block Ack scoreboarding for individually addressed frames (in collaboration with the MLD upper
57
58 MAC sublayer). Optionally, the MLD lower MAC sublayer receives the successful status records of
59 the MPDUs and/or scoreboard context control information of the Block Ack agreement from the
60 MLD upper MAC sublayer)
61
62 NOTE 3—The above functionality partitioning is meant for modeling the functionalities of each MAC sublayer and is
63 not meant for describing the MAC sublayer for which the actual implementation of each function should reside.
64
65

## 第 79 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 NOTE 4—The Block Ack scoreboarding maintenance might be either at the MLD upper MAC sublayer or the MLD
2 lower MAC sublayer, or both. The maintenance might involve collaboration between the MLD upper MAC sublayer and
3
MLD lower MAC sublayer.
4
5
When MLO is being used, the “Block Ack Scoreboarding” block in the MLD upper MAC sublayer manages
6
7 the scoreboard context control and may manage the reception status in the setup links where the Block Ack
8 session is established between the two MLDs. The “Block Ack Scoreboarding” block in the MLD lower
9 MAC sublayer manages at least the Block Ack status of the MPDUs (of this Block Ack session) that are
10
received on this link. The MLD lower MAC sublayer may convey Block Ack status of the MPDUs received
11
on another link if it obtained such info from the other link. The “Block Ack Scoreboarding” block in the
12
13 affiliated AP upper MAC sublayer manages the Block Ack status of the successfully received MPDUs over
14 corresponding non-MLO links. In an implementation, this function may be distributed into the MLD lower
15 MAC sublayer.

architecture):

21 5.1.5.10 Non-AP MLD role
22
23
The MAC data plane architecture of a non-AP MLD as shown in Figure 5-2a (MAC data plane architecture
24
(MLO) for unicast data frames) is completed by replacing the role-specific behavior block with that shown
25
26 in Figure 5-11 (Role-specific behavior block for a non-AP MLD). The function of this block in a non-AP
27 MLD is to perform destination address filtering as described in 10.2.8 (MAC data service).
28
29
30 NOTE—In implementations, the DA address filtering function may be done “lower in the stack.” It is shown in the role-
31 specific behavior block location for simplicity, and any implementation choice needs to provide equivalent behavior.

5.1.5.11 AP MLD role
44
45
46 In an AP MLD, the MAC data plane architecture as shown in Figure 5-2a (MAC data plane architecture
47 (MLO) for unicast data frames) and Figure 5-2b (MAC data plane architecture for AP MLD and affiliated
48 APs) includes Distribution System (DS) access in its role-specific behavior block, as shown in Figure 5-12
49
(Role-specific behavior block for a AP MLD). This block provides access to the DS for associated non-AP
50
MLDs as described in 4.5.2.1 (Distribution).
51
52
53
NOTE—This behavior block indicates that there is no access through the controlled port to or from the local higher lay-
54
ers (e.g., the LLC sublayer) at an AP MLD. Any such access is logically achieved in the architecture via transition of the
55
DS and Portal to an integrated LAN. In actual implementations, this is likely to be optimized, and Data frames appear to
56
be delivered directly to one or more local LLC sublayer entities on the same physical device as the AP MLD. Such opti-

20 6.3.33.5 MLME-RESOURCE-REQUEST-LOCAL.request
21
22
6.3.33.5.1 Function
23
24
25 Change as follows:
26
27
28 This primitive is used to enact the over-the-DS FT resource request protocol for a specified peer MAC
29 entity. The over-the-DS FT resource request protocol is performed by communication between the STAFTO
30 and the SME of the target APFTR, bypassing the MAC of the target APFTR. This MLME function is used
31
to allow the MAC of the target APFTR to process the resource requests.
32
33

51
is making the resource
52
request.

54
Descriptor(s) elements (Resource information that are being requested.
55
container (RIC))

30

## 第 124 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 6.3.134 Link disable
2
3
4 6.3.134.1 Introduction
5
6
This mechanism supports the process of advertising that a link on which an AP affiliated with an AP MLD is
7
8 operating is disabled for all associated non-AP MLDs that have an affiliated non-AP STA operating on that
9 link. An affiliated AP, while operating on a disabled link, does not transmit or receive any frames.
10
11
12 6.3.134.2 MLME-BSS-LINK-DISABLE.request
13
14
6.3.134.2.1 Function
15
16
17
This primitive requests the AP MLD to temporarily cease the operation of the BSS corresponding to the
18
affiliated AP operating on a link.
19
20
21 6.3.134.2.2 Semantics of the service primitive
22
23
24 The primitive parameters are as follows:
25
MLME-BSS-LINK-DISABLE.request(
26
27 BSSID,
28
29 DisableTimer,
30
ExpectedDuration,
31
32 DisassociateNonMLDSTAs
33
)
34
35
36 Name Type Valid range Description
37
38 BSSID MAC address Any valid individual The BSSSID of the AP operating on the
39 MAC address link to be disabled.
40 DisableTimer Integer 0–65 535 Specifies the number of TUs until the link
41 on which the AP is operating becomes dis-
42 abled.
43
ExpectedDuration Integer 0–16 777 215 Indicates the expected duration in TUs for
44
which the requested disablement is
45
expected to be effective.
46
DisassociateNon- Boolean true, false Specifies whether associated STAs not affil-
47
MLDSTAs iated with any MLDs need to disassociated.
48
49
50
6.3.134.2.3 When generated
51
52
53 This primitive is generated by the SME when it decides to disable a link on which an affiliated AP is operat-
54
ing.
55
56
57 6.3.134.2.4 Effect of receipt
58
59
60 The primitive starts the affiliated AP link disablement process in 35.3.7.3.2 (Affiliated AP link disable-
61 ment). All services provided by the AP to an infrastructure BSS, including Beacon and Probe Response
62
frame transmissions and access to the DS, are stopped during the disablement. If the DisassociateNonMLD-
63
STAs parameter is true, then all the associated STAs not affiliated with an MLD in an infrastructure BSS are
64
65 disassociated before the occurrence of the disablement.

This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

45 ing before the link was disabled.

operation on the link that becomes enabled and to indicate the enabling of the link.

## 第 130 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0 January 2023
1 7. DS SAP specification
2
3
4
7.1 Introduction
5
6
7 Change the contents of this subclause, including Figure 7-1 (DS architecture), as follows:
8
9
The DS SAP is the interface between the DS SAP service users and the DS SAP service provider. The DS
10
SAP service users are the connected APs, mesh gates, and the portal, and AP MLDs. The DS SAP service
11
12 provider is the DS. Figure 7-1 (DS architecture) shows the location of the DS in the IEEE 802.11
13 architecture. The DS SAP is indicated in this Figurefigure by the lines connecting the DS to its service users.
14 In Figure 7-1 (DS architecture), the DS has fourmultiple users, two APs, a mesh gate, and a portal, and an
15
AP MLD, so the DS is shown passing behind the MAC/PHYs of the STAs.
16
17
18
19 MAC MAC DSAF DSAF DSAF Portal DSAF MUMS
20 MAC MAC Mesh MAC MUMS
MAC
21 MLMS MLMS MLMS MLMS
PHY PHY PHY PHY DS PHY PHY PHY PHY PHY PHY
22
23
24 802.11(cid:3)Medium 802.11(cid:3)Medium 802.11(cid:3)Medium Non(cid:882)802.11(cid:3)Medium
802.11(cid:3)Media
25
26 Non(cid:882)AP(cid:3)STAs AP1 AP2 Mesh(cid:3)Gate Portal AP(cid:3)MLD non(cid:882)AP(cid:3)MLD
27
28 MUMS(cid:3)=(cid:3)MLD(cid:3)Upper(cid:3)MAC(cid:3)SubLayer
(cid:882)(cid:3)DS(cid:3)SAP MLMS(cid:3)=(cid:3)MLD(cid:3)Lower(cid:3)MAC(cid:3)SubLayer
29
30 Figure 7-1—DS architecture
31
32
33
The DS SAP interface specification describes the primitives required to get MAC service tuples in and out
34
35 of the DS and
36
— update the DS’s mapping of STAs to APs or to mesh gates.,
37
38 — update the DS’s mapping of non-AP MLDs to AP MLDs.
39
40 Describing the DS itself or the functions thereof is out of scope of this annexstandard.
41
42
The DS SAP actions are as follows:
43
44 a) Accept MSDUs (as part of MAC service tuples) from APs, mesh gates, and the portal, and AP
45
MLDs.
46
47 b) Deliver MSDUs (as part of MAC service tuples) to APs, mesh gates, or the portal, or the AP MLDs.
48
c) Accept STA-to-AP mapping updates from the APs.
49
50 d) Accept STA-to-mesh gate mapping updates from the mesh gates.
51
e) Accept non-AP-MLD-to-AP-MLD mapping updates from the AP MLDs.
52
53 NOTE—For MLDs, the source address or destination address parameters of the MAC service tuples (see
54 5.2.3.2 (Semantics of the service primitive)) are set to the MLD MAC address of the non-AP MLD, which is the identity
55 of the non-AP MLD known by the DS.
56
57 When the DS delivers the MAC service tuples to an AP, the AP then determines when and how to deliver
58 the MAC service tuples to the AP’s MAC (via the MAC SAP). When the DS delivers the MAC service
59
tuples to a mesh gate, the mesh gate then determines when and how to deliver the MAC service tuples to the
60
mesh gate’s MAC (via the MAC SAP). When the DS delivers the MAC service tuples to an AP MLD
61
62 through DSAF, the AP MLD then determines when and how to deliver the MAC service tuples to the AP
63 MLD’s MLD upper MAC sublayer (via the MAC SAP).
64
65

1 In the case of an AP MLD and its affiliated AP(s) connected to the DS, there are individual DS SAPs for
2
each affiliated AP and one for the AP MLD, as shown in Figure 7-2 (Example DS access for an AP MLD
3
with two affiliated APs). The AP MLD will provide a mapping to its associated non-AP MLDs, by their
4
5 MLD MAC addresses. The affiliated AP(s) will each provide a mapping to their associated non-MLO non-
6 AP STAs, by their MAC addresses. Thus, the non-AP devices form distinct sets of MAC addresses, and the
7 DS can deliver any service tuples with a one-to-one mapping of destination address to DS SAP.

1 9.2.4.5.6 Queue Size subfield
2

Change the first paragraph as follows:
4
5
6 The Queue Size subfield is an 8-bit field that indicates the amount of buffered traffic for a given TC or TS at
7
the non-AP non-HE STA sending the frame that contains this subfield and the amount of buffered traffic for
8
a given TC or TS at the non-AP HE STA for transmission to the HE STA identified by the receiver address
9
10 of the frame that contains this subfield. The Queue Size subfield is present in QoS Data frames with bit 4 of

12 field set to 1 sent by a non-AP HE STA. The AP might use information contained in the Queue Size subfield
13
to determine the TXOP duration assigned to the STA or to determine the UL resources assigned to the non-
14
AP HE STA (see 26.5.2 (UL MU operation) and 35.5.2 (EHT UL MU operation)).

7 Change the second paragraph as follows:
8
9
The STA selects between single and multiple protection when it transmits the first frame of a TXOP. All
10
11 subsequent frames transmitted by the STA in the same TXOP use the same class of duration settings. A STA
12 always uses multiple protection in a TXOP that includes:
13
— Frames that have the RDG/More PPDU subfield equal to 1
14
15 — PSMP frames
16
17 — VHT/HE NDP Announcement frames, Beamforming Report Poll frames or BFRP Trigger frames
18
— S1G Beacon frames
19
20 — Frames transmitted by an S1G STA with the TXVECTOR parameter RESPONSE INDICATION
21 equal to Long Response
22
— MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame

25 Change the item a) 2) of the fourth paragraph as follows (not all lines shown):
26
27
The Duration/ID field is set as follows:
28
29 a) Single protection settings.
30
31 1) In an RTS frame that is not part of a dual clear-to-send (CTS) exchange and is not part of a
32 BDT exchange, the Duration/ID field is set to the estimated time, in microseconds, required to
33 transmit the pending frame, plus one CTS frame, plus one Ack or BlockAck frame if required,
34
plus any NDPs required, plus explicit feedback if required, plus applicable IFSs.
35
36 2) In an MU-RTS Trigger frame that is not an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame, the Duration/ID field
37 is set to the estimated time, in microseconds, required to transmit the pending frame(s), plus
38
one CTS frame, plus the time to transmit the solicited HE TB PPDU if required, plus the time
39
to transmit the acknowledgment for the solicited HE TB PPDU if required, plus applicable
40
41 IFSs.

## 第 162 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 The Partial BW Info subfield is defined in Figure 9-82 (Partial BW Info subfield format).
2
3
The RU Start Index subfield in the Partial BW Info subfield indicates the first 26-tone RU for which the HE
4
beamformer is requesting feedback. The RU End Index subfield of the Partial BW Info subfield indicates the
5
6 last 26-tone RU for which the HE beamformer is requesting feedback. The value of the RU Start Index sub-
7 field is less than or equal to the value of the RU End Index subfield. The RU Start Index subfield and RU
8 End Index subfield depends on the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the HE NDP Announcement frame,
9
which is indicated by the TXVECTOR parameter CH_BANDWIDTH if the frame is carried in an HE, VHT,
10
or HT PPDU and by the TXVECTOR parameter CH_BANDWIDTH_IN_NON_HT if the frame is carried
11
12 in a non-HT duplicate PPDU, and is 20 MHz if the frame is carried in non-HT PPDU. The values of the RU
13 Start Index subfield and RU End Index subfield are each selected from the following:
14
— Values 0 to 8 if the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the HE NDP Announcement frame is 20 MHz,
15
where 0 indicates 26-tone RU 1 and 8 indicates 26-tone RU 9. Values 9–127 are reserved. See
16
17 Table 27-7 (Data and pilot subcarrier indices for RUs in a 20 MHz HE PPDU and in a non-OFDMA
18 20 MHz HE PPDU).
19
— Values 0 to 17 if the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the HE NDP Announcement frame is 40 MHz,
20
21 where 0 indicates 26-tone RU 1 and 17 indicates 26-tone RU 18. Values 18–127 are reserved. See
22 Table 27-8 (Data and pilot subcarrier indices for RUs in a 40 MHz HE PPDU and in a non-OFDMA
23 40 MHz HE PPDU).
24
— Values 0 to 36 if the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the HE NDP Announcement frame is 80 MHz,
25
26 where 0 indicates 26-tone RU 1 and 36 indicates 26-tone RU 37. Values 37–127 are reserved. See
27 Table 27-9 (Data and pilot subcarrier indices for RUs in an 80 MHz HE PPDU and in a non-
28 OFDMA 80 MHz HE PPDU).
29
30 — Values 0 to 73 if the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the HE NDP Announcement frame is
31 160 MHz, where 0 indicates 26-tone RU 1 and 73 indicates 26-tone RU 74. Values 74–127 are
32 reserved.
33
— Values 0 to 73 if the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the HE NDP Announcement frame is
34
35 80+80 MHz, where 0 indicates 26-tone RU 1 in the lower 80 MHz frequency segment, 36 indicates
36 26-tone RU 37 in the lower 80 MHz frequency segment, 37 indicates 26-tone RU 1 in the upper
37 80 MHz frequency segment, and 73 indicates 26-tone RU 37 in the upper 80 MHz frequency seg-
38
ment. Values 74–127 are reserved. For 80+80 MHz, feedback is not requested for the gap between
39
the 80 MHz segments. See Table 27-9 (Data and pilot subcarrier indices for RUs in an 80 MHz HE
40
41 PPDU and in a non-OFDMA 80 MHz HE PPDU).

1 — If the Feedback Type subfield indicates CQI, the Nc subfield indicates the number of space-time
2 streams, Nc, in the CQI report and is set to Nc–1

5
6 vidual address, the AID11 field in the STA Info field has a value other than 2047, andthe STA Info field
7 having a value in the AID11 field other than 2047, the Nc subfield is reserved.

The format of the STA Info field in an HE NDP Announcement frame if the AID11 subfield is set to 2047 is
10

11
12 is 2047).
13
14 The Disallowed Subchannel Bitmap subfield indicates the 20 MHz subchannels and the 242-tone RUs that
15
are present in HE sounding NDPs announced by the HE NDP Announcement frame and the 242-tone RUs
16
that are to be included in requested sounding feedback. A 20 MHz subchannel is as defined in Clause 17
17
18 (Orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) PHY specification) for the portions of the PPDU that
19 use a tone plan as specified in Clause 17 (Orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) PHY speci-
20 fication) and a 242-tone RU is as defined in 27.3.2 (Subcarrier and resource allocation)). The lowest num-
21
bered bit of the Disallowed Subchannel Bitmap subfield corresponds to the 20 MHz subchannel that lies
22
within the BSS bandwidth and that has the lowest frequency of the set of all 20 MHz subchannels within the
23
24 BSS bandwidth. Each successive bit in the bitmap corresponds to the next higher frequency 20 MHz sub-
25 channel. A bit in the bitmap is set to 1 to indicate that for the corresponding 20 MHz subchannel, no energy
26 is present in the HE sounding NDP associated with this HE NDP Announcement frame. For each disallowed
27
20 MHz subchannel, the 242-tone RU that is most closely aligned in frequency with the 20 MHz subchannel
28
is disallowed for PPDUs that use a tone plan as specified in Clause 27 (High-efficiency (HE) PHY specifica-
29
30 tion). STAs addressed by the HE NDP Announcement frame do not include tones from disallowed 242-tone
31 RUs when determining the average SNR of space time streams 1 to Nc and when generating requested
32 sounding feedback. If a 20 MHz subchannel and its corresponding 242-tone RU is not disallowed, the corre-
33
sponding bit in the bitmap is set to 0.

The Resolution subfield in the Partial BW Info subfield indicates the resolution bandwidth for each bit in the
31
32 Feedback Bitmap subfield. The Feedback Bitmap subfield indicates the request of each resolution band-
33 width from the lowest frequency to the highest frequency with B1 indicating the lowest resolution band-
34 width. Each bit in the Feedback Bitmap subfield is set to 1 if the feedback is requested on the corresponding
35
resolution bandwidth.

38 When the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the EHT NDP Announcement frame is less than 320 MHz, the
39 Resolution bit B0 is set to 0 to indicate a resolution of 20 MHz.
40
— When the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the EHT NDP Announcement frame is equal to 20 MHz,
41
42 B1 is set to 1 to indicate the request of feedback on the 242-tone RU. B2–B8 are reserved and set to
43 0.
44
— When the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the EHT NDP Announcement frame is equal to 40 MHz,
45
46 B1 and B2 indicate the request of feedback on each of the two 242-tone RUs from lower frequency
47 to higher frequency. B3–B8 are reserved and set to 0.
48
— When the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the EHT NDP Announcement frame is equal to 80 MHz,
49
50 if B1–B4 are all set to 1, it indicates the feedback request on the 996-tone RU, otherwise B1–B4 indi-
51 cate the request of feedback on each of the four 242-tone RUs from lower frequency to higher fre-
52 quency. B5–B8 are reserved and set to 0.
53
54 — When the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the EHT NDP Announcement frame is equal to
55 160 MHz, if B1–B4 are all set to 1, it indicates the feedback request on the lower 996-tone RU, oth-
56 erwise B1–B4 indicate the request of feedback on each of four 242-tone RUs from lower frequency
57
to higher frequency in the lower 80 MHz. If B5–B8 are all set to 1, it indicates the feedback request
58
on the upper 996-tone RU, otherwise B5–B8 indicate the request of feedback on each of the four
59
60 242-tone RUs from lower frequency to higher frequency in the upper 80 MHz.
61
62
When the bandwidth of the PPDU carrying the EHT NDP Announcement frame is equal to 320 MHz, set the
63
Resolution bit B0 to 1 to indicate a resolution of 40 MHz. If B1 and B2 are both set to 1, it indicates the
64
65 feedback request on the lowest 996-tone RU, otherwise B1 and B2 indicate the request of feedback on each

The Disambiguation subfield is set to 1.
39
40
NOTE—Setting the Disambiguation subfield to 1 prevents a non-EHT VHT STA from incorrectly identifying its AID in
41
the EHT NDP Announcement frame. The Disambiguation subfield coincides with the MSB of the AID12 subfield of a
42
VHT NDP Announcement frame if the EHT NDP Announcement field is parsed as VHT NDP Announcement frame by
43
a non-EHT VHT STA. The MSB of the AID12 subfield is always 0 since the maximum AID is 2007.
44
45
In an EHT NDP Announcement frame with more than one STA Info field that contains a value less than
46
2008 in the AID11 subfield, the RA is a broadcast address and the following applies:
47
48 — If the Feedback Type And Ng subfield and the Codebook Size subfield indicate SU or MU, the Nc
49
Index subfield indicates the number of columns in the compressed beamforming feedback matrix
50
minus 1, Nc–1. Nc Index subfield values above 7 are reserved.
51
52 — If the Feedback Type And Ng subfield and the Codebook Size subfield indicate CQI, the Nc Index
53 subfield indicates the number of spatial streams in the CQI report minus 1, Nc–1. Nc Index sub-
54
field values above 7 are reserved.
55
56
57 In an EHT NDP Announcement frame with a single STA Info field that contains a value less than 2008 in
58 the AID11 subfield, the RA is an individual address and the Nc index subfield is reserved.

6 Change the first paragraph as follows:
7
8
A Trigger frame which is not an MU-RTS Trigger frame allocates resources for and solicits one or more HE
9
10 TB PPDU transmissions. An MU-RTS Trigger frame allocates resources for one or more PPDUs that are not
11 TB PPDU (see 26.2.6 (MU-RTS Trigger/CTS frame exchange procedure), 35.2.1.2 (Triggered TXOP shar-
12 ing procedure), and 35.2.2 (MU-RTS trigger/CTS frame exchange procedure for EHT STAs)). The Trigger
13
frame also carries other information required by the responding STA to send an HE TB PPDU (see 26.5.2
14
(UL MU operation)), an EHT TB PPDU (see 35.5.2 (EHT UL MU operation)), a non-HT PPDU or a non-
15
16 HT duplicate PPDU (see 26.2.6 (MU-RTS Trigger/CTS frame exchange procedure), 35.2.1.2 (Triggered
17 TXOP sharing procedure)), and 35.2.2 (MU-RTS trigger/CTS frame exchange procedure for EHT STAs)),
18 HE Ranging NDP (see 11.21.6.1.3 (Passive TB ranging overview)), or HE TB Ranging NDP (see
19
11.21.6.4.3 (TB ranging measurement exchange)) in response to the Trigger frame.

28
AID12 subfield of the User Info field contains the AID of a non-AP STA, the RA field is set to the
29
30 address of that STA

— For a Trigger frame that has at least one User Info field with the AID12 subfield that allocates an
32
RA-RU, the RA field is set to the broadcast address

to the broadcast address

the broadcast address

1 EHT AP does not transmit a Trigger frame that solicits both an HE TB PPDU and an EHT TB PPDU as

If a Trigger frame is generated by an EHT AP, the EHT AP does not set the AID12 subfield in an HE variant
5
6 User Info field to 2007.
7
8
A User Info field that is addressed to a non-AP STA is either an HE variant or an EHT variant. The User
9
Info field is an HE variant addressed to a non-AP EHT STA if the B39 of the User Info field is set to 0 and
10
11 the B54 of the Common Info field is set to 1 in the Trigger frame; otherwise, it is an EHT variant. B39 of an
12 HE variant User Info field is reserved for a non-EHT HE STA. B39 is set to 0 for an HE variant User Info
13 field by an EHT AP, and is the PS160 subfield for an EHT variant User Info field. Table 9-45c (Valid com-
14
binations of B54 and B55 in the Common Info field, B39 in the User Info field, and solicited TB PPDU for-
15
mat) defines valid combinations of the B54 and B55 in the Common Info field, the B39 in the User Info

17 field, the presence of the Special User Info field in the Trigger frame, the variant of a User Info field, and the
18 corresponding TB PPDU type.

Split the last paragraph into two paragraphs and further changes as follows:
51
52
53 The Padding field is optionally present in a Trigger frame to extend the frame length for the following pur-
54
poses:
55
56 a) Toto give the recipient STAs enough time to prepare a response for transmission a SIFS after the
57 frame is received.
58
59 b) To align the end time of simultaneously transmitted PPDUs as described in 35.3.16.5 (PPDU end
60 time alignment on a NSTR link pair).
61
62
The Padding field, if present, is at least two octets in length and is set to all 1s. If the Padding field is present
63
in a Trigger frame, its length is computed as described in 26.5.2.2.3 (Padding for a triggering frame) and
64
65 35.5.2.2.3 (Padding for a triggering frame).

25 The Trigger Dependent Common Info subfield in the Common Info field is optionally present based on the
26
value of the Trigger Type field (see 9.3.1.22.6 (Basic Trigger frame format) to 9.3.1.22.13 (NFRP Trigger
27
frame format)).
28
29
30 9.3.1.22.3 Special User Info field)
31
32
The Special User Info field is a User Info field that does not carry the user specific information but carries
33
the extended common information not provided in the Common Info field.
34
35
36 If the Special User Info field is included in the Trigger frame, then the Special User Info Field Flag subfield
37 of the EHT variant Common Info field is set to 0, otherwise it is set to 1.
38
39
The Special User Info field is identified by an AID12 value of 2007 and is optionally present in a Trigger
40
41 frame that is generated by an EHT AP.
42
NOTE 1—An EHT AP does not use the value 2007 as an AID for any STA associated to it (see 35.15.1 (Basic EHT BSS
43
operation)).
44
45 NOTE 2— The length of the Special User Info field is equal to the length of the other User Info fields present in the
46 same Trigger frame, except when the Trigger frame is an MU-BAR Trigger frame.
47
48 NOTE 3—The Special User Info field is not included in the Trigger frame unless the Trigger frame includes one or more
49 EHT variant User Info fields.
50
51 The Special User Info field, if present, is located immediately after the Common Info field of the Trigger
52 frame and carries information for the U-SIG field of a solicited EHT TB PPDU.

stream minus 1 (see 26.5.2.3.3 (TXVECTOR parameters for HE TB PPDU response to Trigger frame)) with
20
a maximum value of 7 (see 36.1.1 (Introduction to the EHT PHY)). The Starting Spatial Stream subfield val-
21
22 ues above 7 are reserved for a STA. The Starting Spatial Stream subfield is set to 0 if the corresponding RU
23 or MRU is not allocated for MU-MIMO.
24
25
The Number Of Spatial Streams subfield indicates the number of spatial streams, and is set to the number of
26
spatial streams minus 1 with a maximum value of 3 (see 36.1.1 (Introduction to the EHT PHY)).
27
28
29 The RA-RU Information subfield of the User Info field indicates the RA-RU information and the format is
30 defined in Figure 9-92 (RA-RU Information subfield format).
31
32
33
34
B26 B30 B31

Bits: 5 1

41 Figure 9-92—RA-RU Information subfield format
42
43
44
The Number Of RA-RU subfield indicates the number of contiguous RUs allocated for UORA. The value of
45
the Number Of RA-RU subfield is equal to the number of contiguous RA-RUs minus 1. A non-AP STA
46
47 determines whether an RA-RU indicated by the Number Of RA-RU subfield is an eligible RA-RU as
48 defined in 26.5.4.2 (Eligible RA-RUs).
49
50 NOTE—The starting spatial stream and the number of spatial streams of the HE TB PPDU transmitted on each RA-RU
51 are 1.
52
53 The More RA-RU subfield is set to 1 to indicate that RA-RUs of the type indicated by the AID12 subfield in
54 this User Info field (see Table 9-51 (AID12 subfield encoding)) are allocated in subsequent Trigger frames
55
that are sent until the end of the TWT SP in which the Trigger frame carrying this field is sent. Otherwise,
56
the subfield is set to 0. The More RA-RU subfield is reserved if the More TF field in the Common Info field
57
58 is set to 0.

49
Table 9-53b (Lookup table for X1 and N) summarizes how to use Equation (9-0a1) to calculate N for differ-
50
ent configurations. For a bandwidth less than or equal to 80 MHz, PS160, B0, X0, and X1 are set to 0. For a
51
52 bandwidth of 160 MHz, PS160 and X1 are set to 0, while X0 is set to 0 to indicate that the RU or MRU allo-
53 cation applies to the lower 80 MHz frequency subblock and set to 1 to indicate that the RU or MRU alloca-
54 tion applies to the upper 80 MHz frequency subblock. For a bandwidth of 320 MHz, X1 is set to 0 to
55
indicate that the RU or MRU allocation applies to the lower 160 MHz segment and set to 1 to indicate that
56
the RU or MRU allocation applies to the upper 160 MHz segment. Within the indicated 160 MHz segment,
57
58 X0 is set to 0 to indicate that the RU or MRU allocation applies to the lower 80 MHz frequency subblock
59 and set to 1 to indicate that the RU or MRU allocation applies to the upper 80 MHz frequency subblock. The
60 configuration indicates the frequency order of the primary and secondary 80 MHz and 160 MHz channels.
61
The order from left to right indicates the order from lower frequency to higher frequency. The primary
