## 第 366 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 that may be exchanged through affiliated STAs between the pair of MLDs. When the current state is State 3 or
2 State 4, the current state existing between MLDs determines the IEEE 802.11 frame types that may be
3
exchanged on any setup links between that pair of MLDs subject to additional constraints (see 35.3.7 (Link
4
management)). A unique state exists for each pair of transmitter and receiver STAs or each pair of MLDs.
5
6 The allowed frame types are grouped into classes and the classes correspond to the STA state or the MLD
7 state. In State 1, only Class 1 frames are allowed. In State 2, only Class 1 or Class 2 frames are allowed. In
8 State 3 and State 4, all frames are allowed (Classes 1, 2, and 3). In the definition of frame classes, the
9
following terms are used:
10
11 — Within an infrastructure BSS: both the transmitting STA and the recipient STA participate in the
12 same infrastructure BSS
13
— Within a PBSS: both the transmitting STA and the recipient STA participate in the same PBSS
14
15 — Within an IBSS: both the transmitting STA and the recipient STA participate in the same IBSS
16
— dot11RSNAActivated: reference to the setting of dot11RSNAActivated at the STA or the MLD that
17
18 needs to determine whether a transmission or reception is permitted.
19

24 The frame classes are defined as follows:
25 c) Class 3 frames
26
27 1) Data frames
28 i) Data frames between STAs in an infrastructure BSS or in an MBSS
29
30 ii) Data frames between an AP MLD and a non-AP MLD associated with the AP MLD
31 2) Management frames
32
33 i) In an infrastructure BSS, an MBSS, or a PBSS, all Action and Action No Ack frames
34 except those that are declared to be Class 1 or Class 2 frames
35
ii) Between an AP MLD and a non-AP MLD associated with the AP MLD, all Action and

1 Deauthentication notification sets the state for a STA or an MLD to State 1. Deauthentication notification when

26 11.3.5.2 Authentication—originating STA or MLD
27
28
29 Change the second paragraph as follows:
30
31 Upon receipt of an MLME-AUTHENTICATE.request primitive, the originating STA or MLD shall
32
authenticate with the indicated STA or MLD, respectively, using the following procedure:
33
34 a) If the STA is in an IBSS, the SME shall delete any PTKSA, GTKSA, IGTKSA and temporal keys
35 held for communication with the indicated STA by using the MLME-DELETEKEYS.request
36
primitive (see 12.6.18 (RSNA security association termination)).
37
38 b) The STA or the MLD shall execute one of the following:
39
1) For the Open System or Shared Key authentication algorithm, the authentication mechanism
40
described in 12.3.3.2 (Open System authentication) or 12.3.3.3 (Shared Key authentication),
41
42 respectively.
43 2) For the fast BSS transition (FT) authentication algorithm in an ESS, the authentication
44
mechanism described in 13.5 (FT protocol), or, if resource requests are included, 13.6 (FT
45
resource request protocol).
46
47 3) For SAE authentication between an AP MLD and a non-AP MLD or in an infrastructure BSS,
48
IBSS, or MBSS, the authentication mechanism described in 12.4 (Authentication using a
49
password).
50
51 4) For FILS authentication, the authentication mechanism described in 12.11 (Authentication for
52 FILS). An AP or PCP may provide estimated association response latency to a non-AP and
53
non-PCP STA using the Association Delay Info field in the Association Delay Info element
54
(9.4.2.174 (Future Channel Guidance element)). The value of the Association Delay Info field
55
56 shall be larger than dot11HLPWaitTime.
57
c) If the authentication was successful within the AuthenticateFailureTimeout, the state for the
58
indicated STA or MLD shall be set to State 2 if it was State 1; the state shall remain unchanged if it
59
60 was other than State 1.
61 d) The MLME shall issue an MLME-AUTHENTICATE.confirm primitive to inform the SME of the
62
result of the authentication.

1 Change the title of the subclause 11.3.5.3 as follows:

3
11.3.5.3 Authentication—destination STA or MLD
4
5
6 Change the first two paragraphs as follows:
7
8 Upon receipt of an Authentication frame with authentication transaction sequence number equal to 1, the
9
destination STA or MLD shall authenticate with the originating STA or MLD, respectively, using the
10
following procedure:
11
12 a) If Open System or Shared Key authentication algorithm is being used, the STA or the MLD shall
13
execute the procedure described in 12.3.3.2 (Open System authentication) or 12.3.3.3 (Shared Key
14
authentication) respectively. These result in the generation of an MLME-
15
16 AUTHENTICATE.indication primitive to inform the SME of the authentication request.
17 b) If FT authentication is being used, the MLME shall issue an MLME-AUTHENTICATE.indication
18
primitive to inform the SME of the authentication request, including the FT Authentication
19
Elements, and the SME shall execute the procedure as described in 13.5 (FT protocol) or 13.6 (FT
20
21 resource request protocol).
22
c) If SAE authentication is being used between an AP MLD and a non-AP MLD or in an infrastructure
23
BSS, IBSS, or MBSS, the MLME shall issue an MLME-AUTHENTICATE.indication primitive to
24
25 inform the SME of the authentication request, including the SAE authentication elements, and the
26 SME shall execute the procedure as described in 12.4 (Authentication using a password).
27
d) If FILS authentication is being used, the MLME shall issue an MLME-
28
AUTHENTICATE.indication primitive to inform the SME of the authentication request, and the
29
30 SME shall execute the procedure described in 12.11 (Authentication for FILS).
31
e) If the STA is in an IBSS and management frame protection was not negotiated when the PTKSA(s)
32
were created, the SME shall delete any PTKSA, GTKSA, IGTKSA and temporal keys held for
33
34 communication with the originating STA by using the MLME-DELETEKEYS.request primitive
35 (see 12.6.18 (RSNA security association termination)).
36
f) Upon receipt of an MLME-AUTHENTICATE.response primitive, if the ResultCode is not
37
SUCCESS, the MLME shall transmit an Authentication frame with the corresponding status code,
38
39 as defined in 9.4.1.9 (Status Code field), and the state for the originating STA or MLD shall be left
40 unchanged. The Authentication frame is constructed using the appropriate procedure in 12.3.3.2
41 (Open System authentication), 12.3.3.3 (Shared Key authentication), 13.5 (FT protocol) or 13.6 (FT
42
resource request protocol).
43
44 g) Upon receipt of an MLME-AUTHENTICATE.response primitive, if the ResultCode is SUCCESS,
45 the MLME shall transmit an Authentication frame that is constructed using the appropriate
46
procedure in 12.3.3.2 (Open System authentication), 12.3.3.3 (Shared Key authentication), 13.5 (FT
47
protocol) or 13.6 (FT resource request protocol), with a status code of SUCCESS, and the state for
48
49 the originating STA or MLD shall be set to State 2 if it was in State 1; the state shall remain
50 unchanged if it was other than State 1.
51
52 NOTE—If management frame protection was negotiated, the SME does not change the state for the originating STA or
53 originating MLD and does not delete any of the previously created SAs or temporal keys as a part of this authentication
54 procedure.
55
56 Insert the following paragraph at the end of the subclause:

59
complete the authentication procedure shall have the Address 1 field equal to the MAC address of the STA
60
61 affiliated with the originating MLD that sends the Authentication frame with authentication transaction
62 sequence number equal to 1.

11.3.5.4 Deauthentication—originating STA or MLD
4
5
6 Change as follows:
7
8
The originating STA or MLD shall deauthenticate with the indicated STA or MLD, respectively, using the
9
following procedure:
10
11 a) The SME shall generate an MLME-DEAUTHENTICATE.request primitive containing the
12 appropriate reason code for the STA or MLD deauthentication, as defined in Table 9-49 (Reason
13
codes) of 9.4.1.7 (Reason Code field).
14
15 b) On receipt of the MLME-DEAUTHENTICATE.request primitive, if the state for the indicated STA
16 or MLD is State 2, State 3, or State 4, the MLME shall generate a Deauthentication frame to be
17
transmitted to the indicated STA or MLD, respectively.
18
19 NOTE—As the Deauthentication frame is a bufferable MMPDU, the transmission of this frame might be
20 delayed by the operation of a power saving protocol. The AID and the PTKSA are maintained (when
21 applicable) until the frame is acknowledged or attempts to transmit the frame are abandoned.
22
23
c) The state for the indicated STA or MLD shall be set to State 1.
24
25 d) Once the Deauthentication frame is acknowledged or attempts to transmit the frame are abandoned,
26 the MLME shall issue an MLME-DEAUTHENTICATE.confirm primitive to inform the SME of the
27 deauthentication.
28
29 e) The SME, upon receipt of an MLME-DEAUTHENTICATE.confirm primitive, shall delete any
30 PTKSA, GTKSA, IGTKSA, BIGTKSA, WIGTKSA and temporal keys held for communication
31 with the indicated STA or MLD by using the MLME-DELETEKEYS.request primitive (see
32
12.6.18 (RSNA security association termination)) and by generating an MLME-
33
SETPROTECTION.request(None) primitive.
34
35 f) If the STA is contained within an AP or PCP, its SME, upon receipt of an MLME-
36 DEAUTHENTICATE.confirm primitive, shall release the AID assigned for the indicated STA, if
37
the state for the indicated STA was State 3 or State 4.
38
39 f1) If the MLD is an AP MLD, its SME, upon receipt of an MLME-DEAUTHENTICATE.confirm
40 primitive, shall release the AID assigned for the indicated non-AP MLD, if the state for the indicated
41
MLD was State 3 or State 4.
42
43 g) If the STA is contained within an AP, its SME shall inform the DS of the disassociation, if the state
44 for the indicated STA was State 3 or State 4.
45
g1) If the MLD is an AP MLD, its SME shall inform the DS of the disassociation, if the state for the
46
47 indicated non-AP MLD was State 3 or State 4.
48 h) If the STA is a mesh STA, its SME shall inform the mesh peering instance controller (see
49
14.3.4 (Mesh peering instance controller)) of the deauthentication.

11.3.6 Association, reassociation, and disassociation
24
25
26 11.3.6.1 General
27
28 Change the third, fourth, and fifth paragraphs as follows:
29
30
Successful association enables a STA to exchange Class 3 frames. Successful association enables an MLD to
31
32 exchange Class 3 frames on any setup link subject to additional constraints (see 35.3.7 (Link management)).
33 Successful association sets the state for a non-FILS STA or a non-FILS MLD to State 3 or State 4. Successful
34 association sets the state for FILS STAs to State 4.
35
36
Successful reassociation enables a STA or an MLD to exchange Class 3 frames. Unsuccessful reassociation
37
38 when not in State 1 leaves the state for a STA state unchanged (with respect to the AP or PCP that was sent the
39 Reassociation Request (which may be the current STA)) or for a non-AP MLD state unchanged (with respect
40 to the AP MLD that was sent the Reassociation Request). Successful reassociation sets the state for a non-FILS
41
STA to State 3 or State 4 (with respect to the AP or PCP that was sent the Reassociation Request frame) or for
42
a non-FILS non-AP MLD to State 3 or State 4 (with respect to the AP MLD that was sent the Reassociation
43
44 Request frame). Successful reassociation when not in State 1 sets the state for a STA to State 2 (with respect to
45 the current AP or PCP, if this is not the AP or PCP that was sent the Reassociation Request frame) or for a non-
46 AP MLD to State 2 (with respect to the current AP MLD, if this is not the AP MLD that was sent the
47
Reassociation Request frame). Successful reassociation sets the state for a FILS STA to State 4 (with respect to
48
the AP or PCP that was sent the Reassociation Request frame) and enables it to exchange Class 3 frames.
49
50 Reassociation shall be performed only if the originating STA or non-AP MLD is already associated in the same
51 ESS.
52
53
Disassociation notification when not in State 1 sets the state for a non-FILS STA or a non-FILS MLD to State
54
2. Disassociation notification when not in State 1 sets the state for a FILS STA to State 1. The STA or MLD
55
56 shall become associated again prior to sending Class 3 frames. A STA or an MLD may disassociate a peer STA
57 or a peer MLD, respectively, at any time, for any reason.
58
59
Insert the following paragraph after the sixth paragraph (“If non-DMG STA A in an
60
infrastructure BSS receives a Class 3 frame...”):
61
62
63 If any affiliated STA A of an MLD receives a Class 3 frame from STA B that cannot be identified to be
64 affiliated with an associated MLD, the MLD shall discard the frame. If the frame has an individual address in
65

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 the Address 1 field, the MLME of the MLD shall send a Disassociation frame through the affiliated STA A of
2 the MLD to STA B.

4
Change the last paragraph as follows:
5
6
7 Association is not applicable in an IBSS. In an infrastructure BSS, association is required. Between an AP
8 MLD and a non-AP MLD, association is required. In a PBSS, association is optional. APs, AP MLDs, and
9
PCPs do not initiate association.

16

19 The SME shall delete any PTKSA, GTKSA, IGTKSA, BIGTKSA and temporal keys held for

23
24 Insert the following two paragraphs after the now-shifted fifth paragraph (“Upon receipt of an
25 MLME-ASSOCIATE.request primitive that is ...”):
26
27
For a non-AP MLD associated with an AP MLD, a non-AP STA affiliated with the non-AP MLD shall not
28
29 send an Association Request frame without Basic Multi-Link element.
30
31 NOTE—A non-AP MLD can disassociate from the associated AP MLD to allow a non-AP STA that was affiliated with
32 the non-AP MLD to send an Association Request frame without a Basic Multi-Link element to perform association with
33 an AP.
34

36

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 2) dot11MinBHIDuration from the MinBHIDuration subfield

3) dot11BroadcastSTAInfoDuration from the BroadcastSTAInfoDuration subfield

4 4) dot11AssocRespConfirmTime from the AssocRespConfirmTime subfield

5) dot11MinPPDuration from the MinPPDuration subfield
6
7 6) dot11SPIdleTimeout from the SPIdleTimeout subfield
8
7) dot11MaxLostBeacons from the MaxLostBeacons subfield
9
10 e) If an Association Response frame is received with a status code of SUCCESS, the state for the AP,
11 AP MLD, or PCP shall be set to State 4 or, if dot11RSNAActivated is true, State 3. The state for any
12
other AP, AP MLD, or PCP which is State 3 or State 4 prior to the association request shall be set to
13
State 2, and the MLME shall issue an MLME-ASSOCIATE.confirm primitive to inform the SME of
14
15 the successful completion of the association.
16 f) If an Association Response frame is received with a status code of SUCCESS at an MM-SME
17
coordinated STA and the Single AID field within the MMS element is equal to 1, then
18
19 — For each of its MAC entities advertised within the MMS element and for which
20 dot11RSNAActivated is true, the state is set to State 3. Progress from State 3 to State 4 occurs
21
independently in each such MAC entity.
22
23 — For each of its MAC entities advertised within the MMS element and for which
24 dot11RSNAActivated is false, the state is set to State 4.
25
— For each of its MAC entities advertised within the MMS element the state for any other AP or
26
27 PCP which is State 3 or State 4 prior to the association request shall be set to State 2.
28 g) If an Association Response frame is received with a status code other than SUCCESS or the
29
association fails to complete within dot11AssociationResponseTimeout the state for the AP, AP
30
MLD, or PCP shall be set to State 2, and the MLME shall issue an MLME-ASSOCIATE.confirm
31
32 primitive to inform the SME of the failure of the association. The status code returned in the
33 Association Response frame indicates the cause of the failed association attempt. Any
34 misconfiguration or parameter mismatch, e.g., data rates required as basic rates that the STA or a
35
non-AP STA affiliated with the non-AP MLD did not indicate as supported in the STA’s Supported
36
Rates and BSS Membership Selectors element, shall be corrected before the SME issues an MLME-
37
38 ASSOCIATE.request primitive for the same AP, AP MLD, or PCP. If the status code indicates the
39 association failed because of a reason that is not related to configuration (e.g., the AP, AP MLD, or
40 PCP is unable to support additional associations) and the Association Response frame does not
41
include a Timeout Interval element with Timeout Interval Type equal to 3 the SME shall not issue
42
an MLME-ASSOCIATE.request primitive for the same AP, AP MLD, or PCP until a period of at
43
44 least 2 s has elapsed. If the status code indicates the association failed and the Association Response
45 frame contains a Timeout Interval element with Timeout Interval Type equal to 3, the SME shall not
46 issue an MLME-ASSOCIATE.request primitive for the same AP, AP MLD, or PCP until the period
47
specified in the Timeout Interval element has elapsed.
48
49 h) If an MLME-ASSOCIATE.confirm primitive is received with a ResultCode of SUCCESS, and
50 RSNA is required, and FILS authentication was not used, then the SME shall perform a 4-way
51
handshake to establish an RSNA with the STA or the AP MLD. As a part of a successful 4-way
52
handshake, the SME shall enable protection by generating an MLME-
53
54 SETPROTECTION.request(Rx_Tx) primitive. If an MLME-ASSOCIATE.confirm primitive is
55 received with a ResultCode of SUCCESS, and FILS authentication was used, then the SME shall
56 enable protection by generating an MLME-SETPROTECTION.request(Rx_Tx) primitive.
57
i) Upon receipt of the MLME-SETPROTECTION.request(Rx_Tx) primitive, the MLME shall set the
58
59 state of the STA or the AP MLD to State 4.

16
17 The following procedure shall be used by an AP or PCP Uupon receipt of an Association Request frame from a
18 STA the AP or PCP shall use the following procedure or by an AP MLD after an AP affiliated with the AP
19
MLD receives an Association Request frame with Basic Multi-Link element from a non-AP STA affiliated
20
with a non-AP MLD:
21
22 a) The MLME shall issue an MLME-ASSOCIATE.indication primitive to inform the SME of the
23
association request. The SME shall issue an MLME-ASSOCIATE.response primitive addressed to
24
the STA or MLD identified by the PeerSTAAddress parameter of the MLME-
25
26 ASSOCIATE.indication primitive. If the association is not successful, the SME shall indicate a
27 specific reason for the failure to associate in the ResultCode parameter. Upon receipt of the MLME-
28 ASSOCIATE.response primitive, the MLME shall transmit an Association Response frame.
29
b) If the state for the STA is 1 and the STA is a non-DMG STA or the state of the non-AP MLD is 1,
30
31 the SME shall refuse the association request by issuing an MLME-ASSOCIATE.response primitive
32 with ResultCode NOT_AUTHENTICATED.
33
c) AP with dot11InterworkingServiceActivated true only: If the MLME-ASSOCIATE.indication
34
35 primitive has the EmergencyServices parameter set to true and the RSN parameter does not include
36 an RSNE, the SME shall not reject the association request on the basis that dot11RSNAActivated is
37 true, thereby granting access, using unprotected frames (see 9.2.4.1.9 (Protected Frame subfield)), to
38
the network for emergency services purposes.
39
40 d) Otherwise, in an RSNA the SME shall check the values received in the RSN parameter to see
41 whether the values received match the security policy. If they do not, the SME shall refuse the
42 association by issuing an MLME-ASSOCIATE.response primitive with a ResultCode indicating the
43
security policy mismatch.
44
45 e) Otherwise, if the state for the STA or the non-AP MLD is 4, the STA or the non-AP MLD has a
46 valid security association, the STA or the non-AP MLD has negotiated management frame
47

48

49

53
primitive with ResultCode REFUSED_TEMPORARILY and TimeoutInterval containing a
54

56 time). If the SME is in an ongoing SA Query with the STA or the non-AP MLD, the Timeout
57 Interval Value field shall be set to the remaining SA Query period, otherwise it shall be set to
58
dot11AssociationSAQueryMaximumTimeout or dot11MLDAssociationSAQueryMaximum-

63
64 the SME shall issue one MLME-SA-QUERY.request primitive addressed to the STA or the

52 dot11MLDAssociationSAQueryMaximumTimeout period), the SME shall issue an MLME-

56

57
association request was genuine, the STA has deleted the PTKSA by this point and so the protected
58

1 REASSOCIATE.confirm primitive indicates the cause of the failed reassociation attempt. Any

16
17 g) If an MLME-REASSOCIATE.confirm primitive is received with a ResultCode of SUCCESS, and
18 RSNA is required, and FILS authentication was not used, and the STA or the non-AP MLD is in
19 State 3, then the SME shall perform a 4-way handshake to establish an RSNA with the STA or the
20
AP MLD. As a part of a successful 4-way handshake, the SME shall enable protection by generating
21
an MLME-SETPROTECTION.request(Rx_Tx) primitive. If an MLME-REASSOCIATE.confirm
22
23 primitive is received with a ResultCode of SUCCESS, and FILS authentication was used, and the
24 STA is in State 3, then the SME shall enable protection by generating an MLME-
25 SETPROTECTION.request(Rx_Tx) primitive.
26
27 h) Upon receipt of the MLME-SETPROTECTION.request(Rx_Tx) primitive, the MLME shall set the
28 state of the STA or of the AP MLD to State 4.
29
30
Change the title of the subclause 11.3.6.5 as follows:
31

33 11.3.6.5 AP, AP MLD, or PCP reassociation receipt procedures

The following procedure shall be used by an AP or PCP uUpon receipt of a Reassociation Request frame from
48

51 MLD:

53 a) The MLME shall issue an MLME-REASSOCIATE.indication primitive to inform the SME of the

1 c) AP with dot11InterworkingServiceActivated true only: If the MLME-REASSOCIATE.indication
2 primitive has the EmergencyServices parameter set to true and the RSN parameter does not include
3
an RSNE, the SME shall not reject the reassociation request on the basis that dot11RSNAActivated
4
is true and dot11PrivacyInvoked is true thereby granting access, using unprotected frames (see
5
6 9.2.4.1.9 (Protected Frame subfield)), to the network for emergency services purposes.
7 d) Otherwise, in an RSNA the SME shall check the values received in the RSN parameter to see
8
whether the values received match the security policy. If they do not, SME shall refuse the
9

10

12

13

14
15 protection, the reassociation is not a part of a fast BSS transition, the STA or the non-AP MLD has
16 not performed a successful SAE authentication after the current association was established, and
17 there has been no earlier, timed out SA Query procedure with the STA or the non-AP MLD (which
18
would have allowed a new reassociation process to be started, without an additional SA Query
19
procedure):
20
21 1) The SME shall refuse the reassociation request by issuing an MLME-REASSOCI-
22 ATE.response primitive with ResultCode REFUSED_TEMPORARILY and TimeoutInterval
23
containing a Timeout Interval element with the Timeout Interval Type field set to 3 (Associa-
24

25
26 MLD, the Timeout Interval Value field shall be set to the remaining SA Query period, other-
27 wise it shall be set to dot11AssociationSAQueryMaximumTimeout or dot11MLDAssociation-
28 SAQueryMaximumTimeout.
29

31 3) Following this, if the SME is not in an ongoing SA Query with the STA or the non-AP MLD,
32
the SME shall issue one MLME-SA-QUERY.request primitive addressed to the STA or the
33
non-AP MLD every dot11AssociationSAQueryRetryTimeout TUs until an MLME-SA-
34
35 QUERY.confirm primitive for the STA or the non-AP MLD is received or
36 dot11AssociationSAQueryMaximumTimeout TUs or
37 dot11MLDAssociationSAQueryMaximumTimeout TUs from the beginning of the SA Query
38
procedure have passed. The SME shall increment the TransactionIdentifier by 1 for each
39
MLME-SA-QUERY.request primitive, rolling it over to 0 after the maximum allowed value is
40
41 reached.
42
4) If no MLME-SA-QUERY.confirm primitive for a STA or a non-AP MLD is received within
43
the dot11AssociationSAQueryMaximumTimeout period or the
44
45 dot11MLDAssociationSAQueryMaximumTimeout period, the SME shall allow a subsequent
46 reassociation process to be started without starting an additional SA Query procedure, except
47 that the SME may deny a subsequent reassociation process with the STA or the non-AP MLD
48
if an MSDU was received from the STA or any non-AP STA affiliated with the non-AP MLD
49
within this period.
50
51
NOTE 1—Reception of an MSDU implies reception of a valid protected frame, which obviates the need
52
for the SA Query procedure.
53
54

55
56 BSSBasicRateSet parameter and all of the membership selectors in the BSSMembershipSelectorSet

58

1 h1) The SME shall refuse a reassociation request from a HE STA that does not support all of the <HE-
2 MCS, NSS> tuples indicated by the Basic HE-MCS And NSS Set field of the HE Operation
3
parameter in the MLME-START.request primitive.
4
5 i) If the ResultCode in the MLME-REASSOCIATE.response primitive is SUCCESS, the SME has an
6 existing SA with the STA or the non-AP MLD, and an SA Query procedure with that STA or the
7 non-AP MLD has failed to receive a valid response (i.e., has not received an MLME-SA-
8
QUERY.confirm primitive within the dot11AssociationSAQueryMaximumTimeout period or the
9
dot11MLDAssociationSAQueryMaximumTimeout period), the SME shall issue an MLME-
10
11 DISASSOCIATE.request primitive addressed to the STA or the non-AP MLD with ReasonCode
12 INVALID_AUTHENTICATION.
13
14 NOTE 2—This MLME-DISASSOCIATE.request primitive generates a protected Disassociation frame. If the
15 reassociation request was genuine, the STA or the non-AP MLD has deleted the PTKSA by this point and so
16 the protected Disassociation frame is ignored. The purpose is to inform a STA which has for some reason failed
17 to respond to an SA Query procedure triggered by a forged reassociation request.
18
19 j) If the ResultCode in the MLME-REASSOCIATE.response primitive is SUCCESS and the
20 reassociation is not part of a fast BSS transition, the SME shall delete any PTKSA, GTKSA,
21
IGTKSA, BIGTKSA, WIGTKSA and temporal keys held for communication with the STA or the
22
non-AP MLD by using the MLME-DELETEKEYS.request primitive (see 12.5.18 (RSNA security
23
24 association termination)).
25
k) If the MLME-REASSOCIATE.indication primitive includes an MMS parameter, the AP or PCP
26
shall take the following additional action, as appropriate:
27
28 1) If the Single AID field in the MMS parameter of the MLME-REASSOCIATE.indication prim-
29 itive is equal to 1, the AP or PCP may allocate a single AID for all of the STAs included in the
30
MMS element. If the AP or PCP allocates the same AID to all STAs whose MAC address was
31
included in the MMS element, it shall include the MMS element received from the MM-SME
32
33 coordinated STA in the MLME-REASSOCIATE.response primitive.
34
2) If the Single AID field is 0, the AP or PCP shall allocate a distinct AID for each STA specified
35
in the MMS element.
36
37
NOTE 3—When the Single AID field is 0, a separate reassociation request/response exchange is performed for
38
each STA specified in the MMS element, and this assigns the multiple AIDs for the STAs.
39
40
l) If a Reassociation Response frame with a status code of SUCCESS is acknowledged by the STA or
41
42 a non-AP STA affiliated with the non-AP MLD, the state for the STA or the non-AP MLD shall be
43 set to State 4, or to State 3 if dot11RSNAActivated is true and the reassociation is not part of a fast
44 BSS transition.
45
m) If the ResultCode in the MLME-REASSOCIATE.response primitive is not SUCCESS and
46
47 management frame protection is in use the state for the STA or the non-AP MLD shall be left
48 unchanged. If the ResultCode is not SUCCESS, management frame protection is not in use, and the
49 reassociation is part of a fast BSS transition, the state for the STA or the non-AP MLD shall be left
50
unchanged. If the ResultCode is not SUCCESS, management frame protection is not in use, and the
51
reassociation is not part of a fast BSS transition, the state for the STA or the non-AP MLD shall be
52
53 set to State 3 if it was State 4.
54
n) If the ResultCode in the MLME-REASSOCIATE.response primitive is SUCCESS, RSNA
55
establishment is required, and the reassociation is not part of a fast BSS transition, and FILS is not in
56
57 use, the SME shall attempt a 4-way handshake with the STA or with the non-AP MLD. Upon a
58 successful completion of a 4-way handshake, the SME shall enable protection by issuing an MLME-
59 SETPROTECTION.request(Rx_Tx) primitive. If FILS authentication was used, the SME shall
60
enable protection by generating an MLME-SETPROTECTION.request(Rx_Tx) primitive. In either
61
case, upon receipt of the MLME-SETPROTECTION.request(Rx_Tx) primitive, the MLME shall
62
63 set the state for the STA or the non-AP MLD to State 4.

42

11.3.6.8 AP, AP MLD, or PCP disassociation initiation procedure

54
Upon receipt of an MLME-DISASSOCIATE.request primitive, an AP, AP MLD, or PCP shall disassociate a
55
56 STA (with respect to the AP or PCP) or a non-AP MLD (with respect to the AP MLD) using the following

58
a) If the state for the STA or the non-AP MLD is State 3 or State 4, the AP or PCP (with respect to the
59
STA) or AP MLD (with respect to the non-AP MLD) shall generate a Disassociation frame to be
60
61 transmitted to the indicated STA or an non-AP STA affiliated with the non-AP MLD.
62
63 NOTE—As the Disassociation frame is a bufferable MMPDU, the transmission of this frame might be delayed
64 by the operation of a power saving protocol. The AID and the PTKSA are maintained (when applicable) until
65 the frame is acknowledged or attempts to transmit the frame are abandoned.

1 11.8 DFS procedures

11.8.3 Quieting channels for testing
4
5
6 Insert the following paragraph and note at the end of the subclause:
7
8
An EHT AP shall not schedule quiet intervals that would require a value higher than 127 in the Quiet Count
9
field of the Quiet element and the Quiet Channel element.
10
11
NOTE—The Quiet element or the Quiet Channel element carried in a per-STA profile of the Basic Multi-Link element
12
corresponding to a reported AP can have the Quiet Count field set to a value higher than 127 to indicate a quiet interval
13
that the reported AP has started in the past on the link on which the reported AP operates. The number of TBTTs in the
14
past is computed as defined in 9.4.2.22 (Quiet element).
15
16
11.8.10 Future Channel Guidance operation
17
18
19 Insert the following paragraphs after the seventh paragraph (“A STA receiving the Future
20
Channel Guidance element...”):
21
22
23 If the Future Channel Guidance element is used to indicate a switch to an EHT BSS operating channel width
24 wider than 160 MHz or to an EHT BSS operating channel width including at least one punctured 20 MHz
25 subchannel, then the Bandwidth Indication element shall be present in this element. If an EHT STA
26
determines the EHT BSS operating channel bandwidth based on the Bandwidth Indication element in a
27
Future Channel Guidance element, then the STA shall ignore the Wide Bandwidth Channel Switch element
28
29 in the Future Channel Guidance element for determining the EHT BSS operating channel bandwidth.
30
31 When the Bandwidth Indication element is present along with the Wide Bandwidth Channel Switch element,
32
33 — the announced BSS bandwidth in the Wide Bandwidth Channel Switch element is the maximum
34 bandwidth including the primary channel without covering any punctured 20 MHz subchannel
35 indicated in the Disabled Subchannel Bitmap subfield in the Bandwidth Indication element as
36
defined in 35.15.2 (Preamble puncturing operation), and
37
38 — the announced BSS bandwidth in the Wide Bandwidth Channel Switch element is less than the BSS
39 bandwidth in the Bandwidth Indication element and the corresponding BSS shall not operate as an
40 80+80 MHz BSS.

48 11.10.9.1 Beacon report

50
11.10.9.1.1 General
51
52
53 Change the 16th paragraph as follows:
54
55
A STA that is not extended spectrum management capable shall not include a Wide Bandwidth Channel
56
Switch subelement or Bandwidth Indication subelement in a Beacon request or Beacon report. A STA shall
57
58 not include a Wide Bandwidth Channel Switch subelement or Bandwidth Indication subelement in a Beacon

1 To send an SA Query Request or SA Query Response frame to a peer STA or a peer MLD, the SME shall issue

Query Request or SA Query Response frame is signaled to the SME with an MLME-SA-QUERY.indication or

30 indication that there might be a mismatch in the association state between itself and the AP or PCP. In such a
31
case, the non-AP and non-PCP STA’s SME may initiate the SA Query procedure with the AP or PCP to
32
verify the validity of the SAsecurity association by issuing one MLME-SA-QUERY.request primitive every
33
34 dot11AssociationSAQueryRetryTimeout TUs until a matching MLME-SA-QUERY.confirm primitive is
35 received or dot11AssociationSAQueryMaximumTimeout TUs from the beginning of the SA Query
36 procedure has passed. If the AP or PCP responds to the SA Query request with a valid SA Query response,
37
the non-AP STA should continue to use the SAsecurity association. If no valid SA Query response is
38
received, the non-AP and non-PCP STA’s SME may delete the SAsecurity association (and temporal keys)
39
40 held for communication with the STA by issuing an MLME-DELETEKEYS.request primitive and the non-

30
11.21.14 Proxy ARP service
31
32
33 Change as follows:
34
35
Implementation of the proxy ARP service is optional for a WNM STA. A STA that implements the proxy ARP
36
37 service has dot11ProxyARPImplemented equal to true. When dot11ProxyARPImplemented is true,
38 dot11WirelessManagementImplemented shall be true. When dot11ProxyARPActivated is true, the Proxy ARP
39 Service bit in the Extended Capabilities field shall be set to 1 to indicate that the AP supports the proxy ARP
40
service. When dot11ProxyARPActivated is false, the Proxy ARP Service bit shall be set to 0 to indicate that
41
the AP does not support the proxy ARP service.
42
43
44 When the AP sets the Proxy ARP field to 1 in the Extended Capabilities element, the AP shall maintain a
45 Hardware Address to Internet Address mapping for each associated STA and for each IPv4 and IPv6 address of
46
the STA, and shall update the mapping when one of the addresses of the associated STA changes. When an AP
47
MLD supports proxy ARP (see 35.3.22 (Proxy ARP service in AP MLDs)), the AP MLD shall maintain an
48
49 MLD MAC address to Internet address mapping for each associated non-AP MLD and for each IPv4 and IPv6
50 address of the non-AP MLD, and shall update the mapping when one of the addresses of the associated non-AP
51 MLD changes. A Proxy ARP service receives and processes three types of messages: IPv4 ARP requests, IPv6
52
ND address lookups, and IPv6 ND duplicate address detection (DAD) messages. These messages are all
53
received as group addressed. If the target address is not known, the Proxy ARP service does not forward the
54
55 request to the BSS. If the target address is known, the Proxy ARP service can either respond directly on behalf
56 of a STA or forward the request as a unicast frame to the intended STA. For fixed devices in doze state, a direct
57 response is preferable. Otherwise, forwarding as unicast is recommended, to avoid responding with misleading
58
information.
59
60
61 For IPv4, when the address being resolved in the ARP request (IETF RFC 826) is used by a non-AP STA
62 currently associated to the BSS, the proxy ARP service shall either respond on behalf of the STA to an ARP
63 request or an ARP probe (IETF RFC 5227) or preferably turn the ARP request into a unicast frame sent to that
64
STA. When the IPv4 address being resolved in the ARP request (IETF RFC 826) is used by a non-AP MLD
65
Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved. 389
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

## 第 390 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 currently associated with the AP MLD, the proxy ARP service shall either respond on behalf of the non-AP
2 MLD to an ARP request or ARP probe (IETF RFC 5227) or preferably turn the ARP request into a unicast
3
frame sent to that non-AP MLD.
4
5
6 When an AP receives an IPv4 ARP request from one associated STA or from the DS with a target IPv4 address
7 that corresponds to a second associated STA, the AP that decides to form a proxy ARP response frame shall
8 insert the second STA MAC address as the sender’s MAC address in the ARP response. When an AP affiliated
9
with an AP MLD receives an IPv4 ARP request from one associated STA, or from a STA affiliated with a non-
10
AP MLD that is associated with the AP MLD, or from the DS, with a target IPv4 address that corresponds to a
11
12 second associated STA, the AP shall insert the second STA MAC address as the Sender’s MAC Address in the
13 ARP response packet. When an AP MLD receives an IPv4 ARP request from a STA associated with an
14 affiliated AP, or from one associated non-AP MLD via any affiliated AP, or from the DS, with a target IPv4
15
address that corresponds to a second associated non-AP MLD, the AP MLD that decides to form a proxy ARP
16
response shall insert the MLD MAC address of the second non-AP MLD as the Sender’s MAC Address in the
17
18 ARP response packet.
19
20 In contrast to IPv4, Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC), which is part of IPv6 Neighbor Discovery
21
(ND), enables a node to form multiple addresses, some of them temporary and with a particular attention paid
22
to privacy. SLAAC addresses may be formed and deprecated asynchronously to the association. Even if the
23
24 knowledge of IPv6 addresses used by a STA (or a non-AP MLD) can be obtained by snooping protocols such
25 as IPv6 ND and DHCPv6, or by observing data traffic sourced at the STA, such methods provide only an
26 imperfect knowledge of the state of the STA at the AP (or of the non-AP MD at the AP MLD), in particular
27
when SLAAC is enabled. Running a Proxy ARP service on an incomplete set of addresses may result in a loss
28
of connectivity, in particular for addresses rarely used and in situations of mobility.
29
30
31 This nondeterministic representation of IPv6 address location and binding may also result in undesirable state
32 persistence in the AP when a STA (or in the AP MLD when a non-AP MLD) ceases to use an IPv6 address. It
33
follows that protocol snooping is not a recommended technique and that snooping should only be used as last
34
resort. IETF RFC 8505 defines an address registration mechanism that enables the AP to maintain a
35
36 deterministic knowledge of all the IPv6 addresses of all the associated STAs. IETF RFC 8929 defines a proxy
37 ND service that leverages the address registration to maintain an accurate proxy state that follows the
38 movements of the STAs (or the non-AP MLDs), while IETF RFC 8928 protects the address ownership against
39
impersonation attacks and address spoofing.
40
41
42 The proxy ARP function for IPv4 and IPv6 shall support snooping of DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and IPv6 ND to
43 discover the IPv4 and IPv6 addresses owned by the STA (or the non-AP MLDs).
44
45
For IPv6, since the state obtained by snooping SLAAC is unreliable, the proxy ARP function shall support the
46
backbone router function defined in IETF RFC 8929, which creates a binding state based on an IETF RFC
47
48 8505 registration by the STA (or the non-AP MLDs). The proxy ARP function should support IETF RFC 8928
49 to protect the ownership of the addresses. The non-AP STA and the non-AP MLD shall support the address
50 registration mechanism defined in IETF RFC 8505 and should support the address protection mechanism
51
defined in IETF RFC 8928.
52
53
54 IPv6 ND uses IP layer multicast Internet Control Message Protocol version 6 (ICMPv6) Neighbor Solicitation
55 (NS) messages (section 4.3 of IETF RFC 4861) for address resolution (section 7.2 of IETF RFC 4861), which
56 is the equivalent of ARP request, and for duplicate address detection (DAD). The proxy ARP function shall
57
drop those messages if the target IP address does not correspond to an associated STA (or an associated non-
58
AP MLD). NS messages are sent as IP layer unicast for neighbor unreachability detection (NUD) (section 7 of
59
60 IETF RFC 4861). The proxy ARP function shall not operate on IP layer unicast NS messages.
61
62 With the IPv6 ND proxy operation defined in IETF RFC 8929, the backbone router function at the AP typically
63
operates as a bridging proxy though operation as a routing proxy is also possible. As a bridging proxy, the NS
64
lookups are replied with the MAC address of the STA (or the non-AP MLD), and the packets to the STA (or
65
390 Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved.
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

## 第 391 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 the non-AP MLD) are bridged normally; as a routing proxy, the backbone router function replies to lookups
2 from the wired backbone with its own MAC address and then routes to the STA (or the non-AP MLD) at the IP
3
layer. The routing proxy isolates the layer-2 domains and hides the MAC address of the STA (or the non-AP
4
MLD) in the wired backbone, for a better stability and scalability of the bridged domain. The Proxy ARP
5
6 function shall support the bridging proxy and may support the routing proxy operation.
7
8 When the target IPv6 address of a IP layer multicast NS message corresponds to an associated STA, the Proxy
9
ARP service may respond on behalf of an associated low-power STA with a neighbor advertisement (NA)
10
message (section 4.4 of IETF RFC 4861) with the override flag set to zero, to conserve energy. Preferably,
11
12 though, the Proxy ARP service should transmit the IP layer multicast NS message as a unicast frame to the
13 STA and let the STA respond, as recommended in IETF RFC 8929. When MAC address mappings change, the
14 AP may send unsolicited Neighbor Advertisement messages on behalf of a STA if the IPv6 Neighbor
15
Discovery function at the STA failed to do so. When the target IPv6 address of an IP layer multicast NS
16
message corresponds to an associated non-AP MLD, the Proxy ARP service may respond on behalf of the non-
17
18 AP MLD with a neighbor advertisement (NA) message (section 4.4 of IETF RFC 4861) with the override flag
19 set to zero. Preferably, though, the Proxy ARP service should transmit the IP layer multicast NS message as a
20 unicast frame to the non-AP MLD and let the non-AP MLD respond, as recommended in IETF RFC 8929.
21
When MAC address mappings change, the AP MLD may send unsolicited Neighbor Advertisement messages
22
on behalf of a non-AP MLD if the IPv6 Neighbor Discovery function at the non-AP MLD failed to do so.
23
24
25 The IPv6 ND function at the STA and the non-AP MLD shall register all of the IPv6 addresses on the interface
26 (see section 10 of IETF RFC 8929) to the proxy ARP service at the AP (or the AP MLD) to ensure that the
27
proxy ARP service is aware of all those addresses and will proxy for them. The proxy ND operation may
28
support address mobility (section 6 of IETF RFC 8929) to transfer a role of ND proxy for this STA to the AP
29
30 with which the STA is associated (or for the non-AP MLD to the AP MLD with which the non-AP MLD is
31 associated) following a mobility event
32
33

11.25 Robust AV streaming

27 11.25.2 SCS procedures
28
29
Change the fifth and sixth paragraphs as follows:
30
31
32 Each SCS stream is identified by an SCSID. ThisThe SCSID is used by a non-AP STA to request creation,
33 modification, or deletion of an SCS stream. The SCSID is used by an AP to identify an SCS stream in SCS
34 responses.
35
36

12

35 12.3.3 Pre-RSNA authentication
36
37
12.3.3.1 Overview
38
39
40 Change the now-shifted third and fourth paragraphs as follows:
41
42
In an infrastructure BSS, a non-DMG STA shall complete an IEEE 802.11 authentication exchange prior to
43
44 association. For MLO a non-AP MLD and AP MLD shall complete IEEE 802.11 authentication exchange
45 prior to association. The Authentication frames for MLO are transmitted between the non-AP MLD and AP
46 MLD through an affiliated STA and affiliated AP, respectively. A DMG STA not in an IBSS shall complete
47
an IEEE 802.11 authentication exchange prior to association when an authentication algorithm other than
48
the Open System authentication algorithm is requested. A DMG STA shall not perform an IEEE 802.11
49
50 authentication exchange using the Open System authentication algorithm. An IEEE 802.11 authentication
51 exchange is optional in an IBSS.
52
53
All Authentication frames shall be individually addressed, as IEEE 802.11 authentication is performed
54
between pairs of STAs or MLDs, i.e., group addressed authentication is not allowed. Deauthentication frames
55
56 are advisory and may be sent as group addressed frames.

1 12.3.3.2 Open System authentication
2

12.3.3.2.1 General

8 Any non-DMG STA or MLD requesting Open System authentication can be authenticated if
9
dot11AuthenticationAlgorithmsTable at the peer STA or MLD, respectively, includes an entry with
10
dot11AuthenticationAlgorithm equal to openSystem and dot11AuthenticationAlgorithmActivated equal to
11
12 true.
13
14 A STA or an MLD may decline to authenticate with another requesting STA or requesting MLD, respectively.
15
Open System authentication is the default authentication algorithm for a pre-RSNA STA or MLD.
16
17
18 Open System authentication utilizes a two-message authentication transaction sequence. The first message
19 asserts identity and requests authentication. The second message returns the authentication result. If the result is
20 “successful,” the STAs or MLDs shall be declared mutually authenticated.
21
22

28
requester, and the STA or the MLD, respectively, to which the initial frame in the exchange is addressed is
29
30 referred to as the responder.

58

59
60
61 Authentication protocols that employ passwords need to be resistant to off-line dictionary attacks.

63
64

396 Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved.

## 第 397 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 Change the now-shifted fifth paragraph as follows:
2
3
Simultaneous authentication of equals (SAE) is a variant of Dragonfly, a password-authenticated key exchange
4
based on a zero-knowledge proof. SAE is used by STAs SAE entities to authenticate with a password; it has
5
6 the following security properties:
7
— The successful termination of the protocol results in a PMK shared between the two SAE
8
entitiesSTAs.
9
10 — An attacker is unable to determine either the password or the resulting PMK by passively observing
11 an exchange or by interposing itself into the exchange by faithfully relaying messages between the
12
two SAE entitiesSTAs.
13
14 — An attacker is unable to determine either the password or the resulting shared key by modifying,
15 forging, or replaying frames to an honest, uncorrupted SAE entitySTA.
16
— An attacker is unable to make more than one guess at the password per attack. This implies that the
17
18 attacker cannot make one attack and then go offline and make repeated guesses at the password until
19 successful. In other words, SAE is resistant to dictionary attack.
20
— Compromise of a PMK from a previous run of the protocol does not provide any advantage to an
21
22 adversary attempting to determine the password or the shared key from any other instance.
23 — Compromise of the password does not provide any advantage to an adversary in attempting to
24
determine the PMK from the previous instance.
25
26
27 Change the now-shifted seventh paragraph as follows:
28
29 The parties involved are called Entity-A and Entity-B STA-A and STA-B. They are identified by their MAC
30
addresses, denoted A-MAC and B-MAC. Between two MLDs, the MAC addresses of Entity-A and Entity-B
31
are their MLD MAC addresses. Between the two STAs, the MAC addresses of Entity-A and Entity-B are their
32
33 STA MAC addresses. STA-A-MAC and STA-B-MAC, respectively. Two SAE entities STAs begin the
34 protocol when they discover a peer by receiving Beacon or Probe Response frame(s), or when they receive an
35 Authentication frame indicating SAE authentication from a peer.
36
37
12.4.3 Representation of a password
38
39
40 Change as follows:
41
42
Passwords are used in SAE to deterministically compute a secret element in the negotiated group, called a
43
44 password element. The input to this process needs to be in the form of a binary string. For the protocol to
45 successfully terminate, it is necessary for each side to produce identical binary strings for a given password,
46 even if that password is in character format. There is no canonical binary representation of a character and
47
ambiguity exists when the password is a character string. To eliminate this ambiguity, an SAE entity a STA
48
shall represent a character-based password as a UTF-8 string that is processed according to the OpaqueString
49
50 profile of IETF RFC 8265, the output of which is an octet string. The octet string representation of the
51 password, after being processed, is stored in the dot11RSNAConfigPasswordValueTable. When a “password”
52 is called for in the description of SAE that follows the credential from the
53
dot11RSNAConfigPasswordValueTable is used.
54
55
56 Similarly, to address ambiguity when identifying passwords, an SAE entity a STA shall represent a password
57 identifier as a UTF-8 string that is processed according to the UsernameCasePreserved profile of
58 IETF RFC 8265, the output of which is an octet string that is stored in the
59
dot11RSNAConfigPasswordValueTable. When a “password identifier” is called for in the description of SAE
60
that follows, the identifier from the dot11RSNAConfigPasswordValueTable is used.
61
62
63 In an infrastructure BSS for which an SAE AKM is indicated, the AP shall set the SAE Password Identifiers In
64 Use subfield of the Extended Capabilities field of the Extended Capabilities element to 1 if any entry in the
65
Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved. 397
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

## 第 398 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 dot11RSNAConfigPasswordValueTable has a non-NULL dot11RSNAConfigPasswordIdentifier, and shall set
2 it to 0 otherwise. Similarly, an AP shall set the SAE Password Identifiers Used Exclusively subfield of the
3
Extended Capabilities field of the Extended Capabilities element to 1 if every entry in the
4
dot11RSNAConfigPasswordValueTable has a non-NULL dot11RSNAConfigPasswordIdentifier and shall set
5
6 it to 0 otherwise.
7
8 For an AP MLD, the dot11RSNAConfigPasswordValueTable for all affiliated APs of the AP MLD shall be
9

12.4.4 Finite cyclic groups
16
17
18 12.4.4.1 General
19
20 Change the first two paragraphs as follows:
21
22
SAE uses discrete logarithm cryptography to achieve authentication and key agreement. Each party to the
23
24 exchange derives ephemeral public and private keys with respect to a particular set of domain parameters
25 that define a finite cyclic group. Groups may be based on either finite field cryptography (FFC) or on elliptic
26 curve cryptography (ECC). Each component of a group is referred to as an element. Groups are negotiated
27
using an identifying number from a repository maintained by IANA as “Group Description” attributes for
28
IETF RFC 2409 (IKE) [B14][B28]. The repository maps an identifying number to a complete set of domain
29
30 parameters for the particular group. Not all groups defined in this repository are suitable. Only FFC groups
31 whose prime is at least 3072 bits and ECC groups defined over a prime field whose prime is at least 256 bits
32 are suitable for use with SAE. ECC groups defined over a characteristic 2 finite field or ECC groups with a
33
co-factor greater than 1 shall not be used with SAE (see NIST Special Publication 800-57). For the purpose
34
of interoperability, an SAE entity a STA shall implement support for group 19, an ECC group defined over a
35
36 256-bit prime order field.
37
38 More than one group may be configured on an SAE entity a STA for use with SAE by using the
39
dot11RSNAConfigDLCGroupTable. Configured groups are prioritized in ascending order of preference. If
40
only one group is configured, it is, by definition, the most preferred group.
41
42
43 12.4.4.2 Elliptic curve cryptography (ECC) groups
44
45
12.4.4.2.2 Generation of the password element with ECC groups by looping
46
47
48 Change the fourth paragraph as follows:
49
50
In order to minimize the possibility of side-channel attacks that attempt to determine the number of
51
interactions of the “hunting-and-pecking” loop required for a given <password, STA-A-MAC, STA-B-
52
53 MAC> tuple, implementations should perform at least k iterations regardless of whether PWE is discovered
54 or not. The value k may be set to any non-negative value and should be set to a sufficiently large number to
55 effectively guarantee the discovery of PWE in less than k iterations. If PWE is discovered in less than k
56
iterations a random “password” can be used in subsequent iterations to further obfuscate the true cost of
57
discovering PWE.
58
59
60 Change the sixth paragraph as follows (not all texts are shown):
61
62
Algorithmically this process is described as follows:
63
64 found = 0;
65
398 Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved.
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

1 counter = 1
2
Length = len(p)
3
4 base = password
5
do {
6
7 pwd-seed = H(MAX(STA-A-MAC, STA-B-MAC) || MIN(STA-A-MAC, STA-B-MAC),
8 base || counter)
9
…

17 An SAE entity peer, e.g. a mesh STA or an AP, indicates support for direct hashing to obtain an ECC password
18 element by setting the SAE hash-to-element bit in the Extended RSN Capabilities field in all Beacon and Probe
19
Response frames. An SAE entity A STA that uses a password identifier shall use the hash-to-curve method. An
20

22 Response frames) shall derive a secret element, PT, according to the following technique and indicate this by

24 not indicate support for this form of element derivation unless its peer has already signalled support for this
25
method. If an SAE Commit message is received with status code equal to SAE_HASH_TO_ELEMENT the
26

27

Change the first paragraph as follows:

55 An SAE peer indicates support for direct hashing to obtain the FFC password element by setting the SAE hash-
56 to-element bit in the Extended RSN Capabilities field in all Beacon and Probe Response frames. An SAE entity
57

## 第 400 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 12.4.5 SAE protocol
2
3
12.4.5.2 PWE and secret generation
4
5
6 Change the second paragraph as follows:
7
8 When an SAE entity a STA supports directly hashing to a group element (according to 12.4.4.2.3 (Hash-to-
9
element generation of the password element with ECC groups) or 12.4.4.3.3 (Direct Generation of the
10
password element with FFC groups)) it computes a secret element, PT, offline at provisioning time for all
11
12 groups it wishes to support with that password. Prior to initiating SAE to an SAE entity a STA which also
13 supports the direct form of hashing to a group element, or upon receipt of an SAE Commit message indicating
14 it was generated using a direct form of hashing to a group element, it shall generate the PWE by hashing the
15
two peer MAC addresses to produce a digest, reducing the digest modulo the order of the particular group, r,
16
interpreting the reduced digest as an integer and using it with the secret element to generate the PWE:
17
18 val = H(0n, MAX(STA-A-MAC, STA-B-MAC) || MIN(STA-A-MAC, STA-B-MAC))
19
val = val modulo (r – 1) + 1
20
21 PWE = scalar-op(val, PT)
22 where 0n is a salt of all zeros whose length equals the length of the digest from the hash function used to
23
instantiate H() (see Table 12-1 (Hash algorithm based on length of prime)).
24
25
26 Change the fourth paragraph as follows:
27
28 After generation of the PWE, each SAE entity STA shall generate a secret value, rand, and a temporary secret
29
value, mask, each of which shall be chosen randomly such that 1 < rand < r and 1 < mask < r and (rand +
30
mask) mod r is greater than 1, where r is the (prime) order of the group. If their sum modulo r is not greater than
31
32 1, they shall both be irretrievably deleted and new values shall be randomly generated. The values rand and
33 mask shall be random numbers produced from a quality random number drawn from a uniform distribution
34 generator. These values shall never be reused on distinct protocol runs.
35
36
12.4.5.4 Processing of a peer’s SAE Commit message
37
38
39 Change the first two paragraphs as follows:
40
41
If the peer’s SAE Commit message contains a password identifier, the value of that identifier shall be used in
42
construction of the password element (PWE) for this exchange. If a password identifier is present in the peer’s
43
44 SAE Commit message and there is no password with the given identifier an SAE entity a STA shall fail
45 authentication.
46
47
If the peer’s SAE Commit message contains a Rejected Groups element, the list of rejected groups shall be
48
checked to ensure that all of the groups in the list are groups that would be rejected. If any groups in the list
49
50 would not be rejected then processing of the SAE Commit message terminates and the SAE entity STA shall
51 reject the peer’s authentication. While the rejected groups are appended to the Rejected Groups element as they
52
are rejected (see 12.4.7.4 (Encoding and decoding of SAE Commit messages)) there is no inherent order to the
53
groups in the list. The order in which they are sent and received shall be retained when deriving keys.
54
55
56 Change the fifth and sixth paragraphs as follows:
57
58
If either scalar validation or element validation fails, the SAE entity STA shall reject the peer’s authentication.
59
If both the scalar and element from the peer’s SAE Commit message are successfully validated, a shared secret
60
61 element, K, shall be derived using the scalar and element (peer-commit-scalar and PEER-COMMIT-
62 ELEMENT, respectively) from the peer’s SAE Commit message and the SAE entity’s STA’s secret value.
63
64
65

1 K = scalar-op(rand, (elem-op(scalar-op(peer-commit-scalar, PWE), (cid:3)
2 PEER-COMMIT-ELEMENT)))
3
4
If the shared secret element, K, is the identity element for the negotiated group (the value one for an FFC group
5
6 or the point-at-infinity for an ECC group) the SAE entity STA shall reject the peer’s authentication. Otherwise,
7 a secret value, k, shall be computed as:
8
9
k = F(K)

12 12.4.6 Anti-clogging tokens
13
14 Change the first paragraph as follows:
15
16
An SAE entity A STA is required to do a considerable amount of work upon receipt of an SAE Commit
17
18 message. This opens up the possibility of a distributed denial-of-service attack by flooding an SAE entity a
19 STA with bogus SAE Commit messages from forged MAC addresses. To prevent this from happening, an
20 SAE entity a STA shall maintain an Open counter in its SAE state machine indicating the number of open and
21
unfinished protocol instances (see 12.4.5.1 (Message exchanges)). When that counter hits or exceeds
22
dot11RSNASAEAntiCloggingThreshold, the SAE entity STA shall respond to each SAE Commit message
23
24 with a rejection that includes an Anti-Clogging Token field statelessly bound to the sender of the SAE Commit
25 message. The sender of the SAE Commit message shall then include the Anti-Clogging Token field in a
26 subsequent SAE Commit message.
27
28
12.4.8 SAE finite state machine

12.4.8.3.1 Parent process events and output

36 Change the fourth paragraph as follows:

38
Receipt of frames containing SAE messages signals the following events to the SAE parent process:
39
40 — Authentication frame with Transaction Sequence number 1—This event indicates that an SAE
41 Commit message has been received from a peer STA.
42
— Authentication frame with Transaction Sequence number 2—This event indicates that an SAE
43
44 Confirm message has been received from a peer STA.

23 1) Increment the PN, to obtain a fresh PN for each MPDU, so that the PN never repeats for the
24
same temporal key.
25
26 NOTE 1—Retransmitted MPDUs are not modified on retransmission. For MLO, MPDUs are not
27 encapsulated with a new PN when retransmitted on another link.
28
29 2) Use the fields in the MPDU header to construct the additional authentication data (AAD) for
30 CCM. The CCM algorithm provides integrity protection for the fields included in the AAD.
31
MPDU header fields that may change when retransmitted are muted by being masked to 0 or
32
being set to a known value when calculating the AAD as described in 12.5.2.3.3 (Construct
33
34 AAD).
35

36

37

41
Type field of the Frame Control field is 10 (Data frame) and there is a QoS Control field
42
present in the MPDU header, the priority value of the MPDU is equal to the value of the TID
43
44 subfield of the QoS Control field (bits 0 to 3 of the QoS Control field). If the Type field of the
45 Frame Control field is 00 (Management frame) and the frame is a QMF, the priority value of
46 the MPDU is equal to the value in the ACI subfield of the Sequence Number field. Otherwise,
47
the priority value of the MPDU is equal to the fixed value 0.
48
49 NOTE 2—For MLO, AAD and CCM Nonce construction for Management frames follows 12.5.2.3.4
50 (Construct CCM nonce) and uses the MPDU header fields to be transmitted over the affiliated STA link.
51
4) Construct the CCMP header as defined in 12.5.3.3.5 (Construct CCMP header for PV0
52
MPDUs).
53
54 5) Use the temporal key, AAD, nonce, and MPDU data to form the cipher text and the encrypted
55 MIC. This step is known as CCM originator processing.
56
57 6) Form the encrypted MPDU by combining the original MPDU header, the CCMP header, the
58 encrypted data and the encrypted MIC, as described in 12.5.3.2 (CCMP MPDU format)

12.5.2.3.3 Construct AAD
10
11
12 Change the first paragraph as follows:
13
a) For PV0 MPDUs, the format of the AAD is shown in Figure 12-19 (AAD construction for PV0
14
MPDUs). The length of the AAD for PV0 varies depending on the presence or absence of the QC
15
16 and A4 fields and is shown in Table 12-3 (AAD length for PV0 MPDUs).
17
The AAD is constructed from the MPDU header. The AAD includes neither the Duration/ID field
18
nor the HT Control field because the contents of these fields might change during normal operation
19
20 (e.g., due to a rate change preceding retransmission). The HT Control field might also be inserted or
21 removed during normal operation (e.g., retransmission of an A-MPDU where the original A-MPDU
22 included an MRQ that has already generated a response). For similar reasons, several subfields in
23
the Frame Control field are masked to 0. For PV0 MPDUs, AAD construction is performed as fol-
24
lows:
25
26 1) FC – MPDU Frame Control field, with
27
i) Subtype subfield (bits 4 5 6) in a Data frame masked to 0
28
29 ii) Retry subfield (bit 11) masked to 0
30
iii) Power Management subfield (bit 12) masked to 0
31
32 iv) More Data subfield (bit 13) masked to 0
33
v) Protected Frame subfield (bit 14) always set to 1
34
35 vi) +HTC subfield (bit 15) as follows:
36
— Masked to 0 in all Data frames containing a QoS Control field
37
38 — Unmasked otherwise
39 vii) Other subfields are not modified
40
41 2) A1 – MPDU Address 1 field.A1 is set as follows:
42 — If the To DS or From DS subfields in the MAC header of the MPDU are not both equal to
43
0, and the MPDU is an individually addressed Data frame between an AP MLD and a non-
44
AP MLD associated with the AP MLD, then A1 is set to the MLD MAC address of the
45
46 intended receiver.
47
— otherwise, Al is set to the MPDU Address 1 field.
48
49 3) A2 – MPDU Address 2 field.A2 is set as follows:
50
— If the To DS or From DS subfields in the MAC header of the MPDU are not both equal to
51
0, and the MPDU is an individually addressed Data frame between an AP MLD and a non-
52
53 AP MLD associated with the AP MLD, then A2 is set to the MLD MAC address of the
54 transmitting MLD..
55
— otherwise, A2 is set to the MPDU Address 2 field.
56
57 4) A3 – MPDU Address 3 field.If dot11MultiLinkActivated is true, the MPDU Address 3 field is
58 the BSSID and the MPDU is an individually addressed Data frame between an AP MLD and a
59
non-AP MLD associated with the AP MLD, then:
60
61 — A3 is set to the MLD MAC address of the AP MLD.
62
— Otherwise, A3 is set to the MPDU Address 3 field.
63
64

1 5) SC – MPDU Sequence Control field, with the Sequence Number subfield (bits 4–15 of the
2 Sequence Control field) masked to 0. The Fragment Number subfield is not modified.
3
6) A4 – MPDU Address field, if present.A4, if present, is set as follows:
4
5 — if dot11MultiLinkActivated is true, MPDU Address 4 field is a BSSID, and the MPDU is
6
an individually addressed Data frame between an AP MLD and a non-AP MLD associated
7

11
12 construction of the AAD. When in a non-DMG BSS and both the STA and its peer have their
13 SPP A-MSDU Capable fields equal to 1, bit 7 (the A-MSDU Present field) is used in the
14 construction of the AAD. The remaining QC fields are masked to 0 for the AAD calculation
15
(bits 4 to 6, bits 8 to 15, and bit 7 when either the STA or its peer has the SPP A-MSDU
16
Capable field equal to 0). When in a DMG BSS, the A-MSDU Present bit 7 and A-MSDU
17
18 Type bit 8 are used in the construction of the AAD, and the remaining QC fields are masked to
19 0 for the AAD calculation (bits 4 to 6, bits 9 to 15).

12.5.2.3.7 CCM originator processing
35
36
37 Change the fifth paragraph as follows:
38
39
The PN values sequentially number each MPDU. Each transmitter STA that is not affiliated with an MLD shall
40
maintain a single PN (48-bit counter) for each PTKSA and GTKSA. Each transmitter STA that is affiliated
41
42 with an MLD shall use the PN (48-bit counter) that is maintained by the MLD for the PTKSA, or by the STA
43 for GTKSA. The PN shall be implemented as a 48-bit strictly increasing integer, initialized to 1 when the
44 corresponding temporal key is initialized or refreshed.

12.5.4.1 GCMP overview
50
51
52 Change the first paragraph as follows:
53
54
Subclause 12.5.4 (GCM protocol (GCMP)) specifies the GCMP, which provides data confidentiality,
55
authentication, integrity, and replay protection. A DMG RSNA STA shall support GCMP-128. An EHT RSNA
56
57 STA shall support GCMP-256.

25
26
Change the second paragraph as follows:
27
28
29 GCMP encrypts the Frame Body field of a plaintext MPDU and encapsulates the resulting cipher text using the
30
following steps:
31
32 a) Increment the PN, to obtain a fresh PN for each MPDU, so that the PN never repeats for the same
33 temporal key.
34
35 NOTE—Retransmitted MPDUs are not modified on retransmission. For MLO, MPDUs are not encapsulated
with a new PN when retransmitted on another link.
36
37
b) Use the fields in the MPDU header to construct the additional authentication data (AAD) for GCM.
38
39 The GCM algorithm provides integrity protection for the fields included in the AAD. MPDU header
40 fields that may change when retransmitted are muted by being masked to 0 or being set to a known
41 value when calculating the AAD as described in 12.5.5.3.3 (Construct AAD).
42
43 c) Construct the GCM nonce block as defined in 12.5.2.3.4 (Construct CCM nonce) from the PN and
44 A2, where A2 is MPDU Address 2.
45
d) Construct the GCMP header as defined in 12.5.5.3.5 (Construct GCMP header).
46
47 e) Use the temporal key, AAD, nonce, and MPDU data to form the cipher text and the MIC. This step
48 is known as GCM originator processing.
49
f) Form the encrypted MPDU by combining the original MPDU header, the GCMP header, the
50
51 encrypted data and the MIC, as described in 12.5.5.2 (GCMP MPDU format).
52
53 12.5.4.3.4 Construct GCM nonce
54
55
Change the second paragraph as follows:
56
57
58 If the To DS or From DS subfields in the MAC header of the MPDU are not both equal to 0, and the MPDU is
59
an individually addressed Data frame between an AP MLD and a non-AP MLD associated with the AP MLD,
60
then the A2 subfield shall contain the MLD MAC address of the transmitting MLD. Otherwise, theThe A2
61
62 subfield shall contain the Address 2 field from the MAC header.
63
NOTE—For MLO, AAD and GCM Nonce construction for Management frames uses the MPDU header fields to be
64
transmitted over the affiliated STA link.

15 12.6 RSNA security association management
16
17 12.6.1 Security associations
18
19
12.6.1.1 Security association definitions
20
21
22 12.6.1.1.2 PMKSA
23
24
Change the third paragraph as follows:
25
26
27 A PMKSA association is bidirectional. In other words, both parties use the information in the security
28 association for both sending and receiving. The PMKSA is used to create the PTKSA. PMKSAs have a certain
29
lifetime. For a non-AP MLD that is associated with an AP MLD, the PMKSA association is between the AP
30
MLD and the non-AP MLD. The PMKSA consists of the following:
31
32 — PMKID, as defined in 12.7.1.3 (Pairwise key hierarchy) or 12.7.1.6.3 (PMK-R0). The PMKID
33 identifies the security association.
34
35 — Authenticator’s or peer’s MAC address. For multi-band RSNA, the MAC address is associated with
36 the operating band in use when the PMKSA is established. For MLO, the Authenticator’s MAC
37 address is the MLD MAC address of the AP MLD.
38
— PMK; or if the PMKSA was established with an AKM suite type for which the Authentication type
39
40 column includes FT authentication (see Table 9-151 (AKM suite selectors)), MPMK (see
41 12.7.1.6.3 (PMK-R0)).
42
— Lifetime, as defined in 12.7.1.3 (Pairwise key hierarchy) or 12.7.1.6 (FT key hierarchy).
43
44 — AKMP.
45
— All authorization parameters specified by the AS or local configuration. This might include
46
47 parameters such as the STA’s authorized SSID.
48 — Cache Identifier, if advertised by the AP in FILS Indication element.
49
50
12.6.1.1.6 PTKSA
51
52
53 Change the first paragraph as follows:
54
55
The PTKSA results from a successful 4-way handshake, FT 4-way handshake, FT protocol, FT resource
56
57 request protocol, or FILS authentication. This security association is also bidirectional. PTKSAs have the same
58 lifetime as the PMKSA or PMK-R1 security Association, whichever comes first. Because the PTKSA is tied to
59 the PMKSA or to a PMK-R1 security association, it only has the additional information from the 4-way
60
handshake, or FT Protocol authentication, or FILS authentication. For the PTKSA derived as a result of the 4-
61
way handshake, there shall be only one PTKSA per band (see 12.6.19 (Protection of robust Management
62
63 frames)) or per MLD setup (see 35.3.5 (Multi-link (re)setup)) with the same Supplicant and Authenticator
64
65

This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

1 MAC addresses. For the PTKSA derived as a result of an initial mobility domain association or fast BSS
2 transition, there shall be only one PTKSA with the same STA’s MAC address and BSSID.

12.6.1.1.8 GTKSA
30
31
32 Change the first paragraph as follows:
33
34
The GTKSA results from a successful 4-way handshake, FT 4-way handshake, FT protocol, FT resource
35
request protocol, group key handshake, or FILS authentication, and is unidirectional. In an infrastructure
36
37 BSS, there is one GTKSA, used exclusively for encrypting group addressed MPDUs that are transmitted by
38 the AP and for decrypting group addressed transmissions that are received by the STAs. Between an AP
39 MLD and a non-AP MLD that have completed a successful multi-link (re)setup, for each setup link there is
40
one GTKSA used exclusively for encrypting group addressed MPDUs that are transmitted by the affiliated
41
AP operating on the link and for decrypting group addressed transmissions that are received by the affiliated
42
43 non-AP STA operating on the link. In an IBSS or in a PBSS, each STA defines its own GTKSA, which is
44 used to encrypt its group addressed transmissions, and stores a separate GTKSA for each peer STA so that
45 encrypted group addressed traffic received from other STAs may be decrypted. A GTKSA is created by the
46
Supplicant’s SME when message 3 of the 4-way handshake is received, when message 1 of the group key
47
handshake is received, when the Reassociation Response frame of the FT handshake is received, or when the
48
49 FILS authentication with a status code indicating success is received. The GTKSA is created by the
50 Authenticator’s SME when the SME changes the GTK and has sent the GTK to all STAs with which it has a
51 PTKSA. It has the same lifetime as the BSS, unless superseded. A GTKSA consists of the following:
52
— Direction vector (whether the GTK is used for transmit or receive).
53
54 — Group cipher suite selector.
55
— GTK.
56
57 — For non-MLO, Authenticator MAC address. For MLO, the MAC address of the AP operating on the
58
link corresponding to the GTKSA.
59
60 — Key ID.
61
— All authorization parameters specified by local configuration. This might include parameters such as
62
the STA’s authorized SSID.
63
64
65
Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved. 409
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 12.6.1.1.9 IGTKSA
2
3
Change as follows:
4
5
6 When management frame protection is enabled, a non-AP STA’s SME creates an IGTKSA when it receives
7 a valid message 3 of the 4-way handshake or FT 4-way handshake, the Reassociation Response frame of the
8
fast BSS transition protocol with a status code indicating success, a Mesh Peering Open Message of the
9
Authenticated Mesh Peering Exchange (AMPE) protocol, a valid message 1 of the group key handshake, or
10
11 the (Re)Association Response frame of FILS authentication with a status code indicating success. When
12 management frame protection is enabled, a non-AP MLD’s SME creates an IGTKSA for each of the setup
13 links when it receives a valid message 3 of the 4-way handshake or FT 4-way handshake, or the
14
Reassociation Response frame of the fast BSS transition protocol with a status code indicating success, or a
15
valid message 1 of the group key handshake. The Authenticator’s SME creates an IGTKSA when it
16
17 establishes or changes the IGTK with all STAs to which it has a valid PTKSA or mesh PTKSA. An AP
18 MLD’s SME creates an IGTKSA for each of its links when it establishes or changes the IGTK with all non-
19 AP STAs that operate on the link and are affiliated with the non-AP MLDs to which it has a valid PTKSA.
20
An IGTKSA has the same lifetime as the BSS, unless superseded.
21
22
23 An IGTKSA consists of the following:
24 — Direction vector (whether the IGTK is used for transmit or receive)
25
26 — Key ID
27 — IGTK
28
29 — For non-MLO, Authenticator MAC address. For MLO, the MAC address of the AP operating on the
30 link corresponding to the IGTKSA.
31

35 Change as follows:

38

39
has the same lifetime as the BSS, unless superseded.
40
41
42 A Supplicant’s SME creates a BIGTKSA when dot11BeaconProtectionEnabled is true, upon receiving a
43 BIGTK from its Authenticator. A non-AP MLD’s SME creates a BIGTKSA for each of its setup link when
44
dot11BeaconProtectionEnabled is true, upon receiving a BIGTK for the link from its Authenticator.
45

48

49
50 — Key ID
51
— BIGTK

13 12.6.3 RSNA policy selection in an infrastructure BSS
14
15
16 Insert a new subclause General before the beginning of the first paragraph (“The requirements
17 in this subclause”):
18
19
12.6.3.1 General
20
21
22 Change the fourth paragraph as follows:
23
24
An SME initiating an association shall insert an RSNE into its (Re)Association Request frame via the MLME-
25
26 ASSOCIATE.request or MLME-REASSOCIATE.request primitive, when the targeted AP indicates RSNA
27 support. The initiating STA’s RSNE shall include one authentication and pairwise cipher suite from among
28 those advertised by the targeted AP in its Beacon and Probe Response frames. It shall also specify the group
29
cipher suite specified by the targeted AP.
30
31
32 For MLO, the initiating non-AP MLD shall include only one RSNE, and RSNXE if present, in the
33 (Re)Association Request frame. The RSNE shall include one AKM suite selector, one pairwise cipher suite
34 selector, and one group cipher suite selector that are common among those advertised by the APs affiliated
35
with the targeted AP MLD. A non-AP MLD determines the appropriate AKM suite selector and pairwise
36
cipher suite selector during MLO discovery by passively or actively scanning for APs affiliated with the AP
37
38 MLD or by an exchange of Multi-Link Probe Request and Multi-Link Probe Response frames with one or
39 more APs affiliated with the AP MLD. If at least one RSNE field from the AP’s RSNE fails to overlap with
40 any value the STA supports, the STA shall decline to associate with that AP. An HT STA shall eliminate TKIP
41
as a choice for the pairwise cipher suite if CCMP-128 or CCMP-256 is advertised by the AP or if the AP
42
included an HT Capabilities element in its Beacon and Probe Response frames. The elimination of TKIP as a
43
44 choice for the pairwise cipher suite may result in a lack of overlap of the remaining pairwise cipher suite
45 choices, in which case the STA shall decline to create an RSN association with that AP.
46

39 12.6.21 RSNA rekeying
40
41 Change the first paragraph as follows:
42
43
44 When a PTKSA is deleted, a non-AP and non-PCP STA may reassociate with the same AP or PCP and/or
45 establish a new RSNA with the AP or PCP. When a PTKSA is deleted, a non-AP MLD may reassociate with
46 the same AP MLD and/or establish a new RSNA with the AP MLD. If the non-AP and non-PCP STA has
47
cached one or more PMKSAs, it may skip the PMKSA establishment and proceed with the creation of a new
48
PTKSA by using 4-way handshake, FT 4-way handshake, or FILS authentication using the procedures
49
50 defined in 12.6.10.3 (Cached PMKSAs and RSNA key management). When a GTKSA is deleted, an
51 originating STA may create a new GTKSA by using 4-way handshake or group key handshake.
52
53
