54
55 MAC shall issue an...”):
56
57 For MLO, the AP MLD’s Authenticator manages packet number assignment for the PTKSA with a non-AP
58
MLD. For a given link, the affiliated AP’s Authenticator manages packet number assignment for the
59
IGTKSA, GTKSA, or BIGTKSA. If an IGTKSA, GTKSA, or BIGTKSA update is triggered, the affiliated
60
61 AP updates group keys for the given link through a group key handshake between the AP MLD and non-AP
62 MLD.

1 Change the now-shifted fifth paragraph as follows:
2
3
A PTKSA has a limited lifetime, either in absolute time or due to exhausting the PN space. To maintain an
4
uninterrupted security association, a STA or MLD should establish a new PTKSA prior to the expiration of
5
6 the old PTKSA.

9 12.7 Keys and key distribution
10
11
12.7.1 Key hierarchy
12
13
14 12.7.1.1 General
15
16 Change the third paragraph as follows:
17
18
In an infrastructure BSS, the IEEE 802.1X Authenticator MAC address (AA) and the AP’s MAC address are
19
20 the same, and the Supplicant’s MAC address (SPA) and the STA’s MAC address are equal. Between an AP
21 MLD and a non-AP MLD, the IEEE 802.1X Authenticator MAC address (AA) shall be set to the MLD MAC
22 address of the AP MLD, and the Supplicant’s MAC address (SPA) shall be set to the MLD MAC address of
23
the non-AP MLD. For the purposes of comparison in this standard, the MAC address is encoded as 6 octets,
24
taken to represent an unsigned integer. The first octet of the MAC address shall be used as the most significant
25
26 octet. The bit numbering conventions in 9.2.2 (Conventions) shall be used within each octet. This results in a
27 sequence of 48 bits represented such that bit 0 is the first transmitted bit (Individual/Group bit) and bit 47 is the
28 last transmitted bit.
29
30
Insert the following paragraph after the fourth paragraph (“An RSNA STA shall support...”):
31
32
33 An MLD shall support at least one pairwise key for any <transmitter_MLD MAC address, receiver_MLD
34
MAC address> pair for use with enhanced data cryptographic encapsulation mechanisms. The
35
<transmitter_MLD MAC address, receiver_MLD MAC address> pair identifies the pairwise key.
36

43 In this example, the following apply:
44
a) Group nonce (GNonce) is a random or pseudorandom value contributed by the IEEE 802.1X
45
Authenticator.

47 b) The GTK is derived from the GMK by GTK = PRF-Length(GMK, “Group key expansion”, AA ||
48 GNonce)
49
50 NOTE—For MLO, AA is replaced with the MAC address of the AP operating on the link.

52 c) Length = TK_bits. The value of TK_bits is cipher-suite dependent and is defined in Table 12-
53
8 (Cipher suite key lengths). AA is represented as an IEEE 802 address and GNonce as a bit string
54
as defined in 9.2.2 (Conventions).

— 4-way handshake message 1 is an EAPOL-Key frame with the Key Type subfield equal to 1. Use
23

25 derivation is defined in 12.6.10.3 (Cached PMKSAs and RSNA key management). When a cached
26 PMKSA is not being used, inclusion of the PMKID (if derived) is optional. For MLO, the Key Data
27
field shall include the MAC Address KDE set to the MLD MAC address of the Authenticator. The
28
Key Data field need not be encrypted.

31 Key Data field shall contain an RSNE, may contain an RSNXE, and need not be encrypted. For
32

Supplicant.

35 An ESS Supplicant’s SME shall insert the RSNE it sent in its (Re)Association Request frame, and
36

37
(Re)Association Request frame it sent. The RSNE and the RSNXE are included as transmitted in the

41 affiliated STA MAC address corresponding to each link included in the basic Multi-Link element.

against the RSNE received in the (Re)Association Request frame, and shall validate the RSNXE

52
Key Data field shall contain one or two RSNEs, and may contain an RSNXE. If a group cipher has
53
54 been negotiated, this field shall also include a GTK. This field shall be encrypted if a GTK is
55 included. For MLO, the Key Data field shall include the MAC Address KDE set to the MLD MAC
56 address of the Authenticator. When the Authenticator is an AP MLD and the Supplicant is a non-AP
57
MLD, this field shall include one MLO GTK for each setup link (see 35.3.5 (Multi-link (re)setup)).

62
mobility domain association or an association started through the FT protocol, the PMKR1Name is
63
added in the PMKID List field of the RSNE. For MLO, for each affiliated AP, the Authenticator’s

1 SME shall insert a MLO Link KDE that includes the LinkID field, affiliated AP MAC address,

14
15 NOTE 3—A non-AP MLD obtains the Link ID, AP MAC address, RSNE, and RSNXE (if present) for an AP affiliated
16 with the AP MLD when it receives a Beacon or Probe Response frame from that AP or when it receives a multi-link
17 probe response transmitted by another AP affiliated with the same AP MLD carrying a Basic Multi-Link element
18 containing a complete profile of that AP (see 35.3.4 (Discovery of an AP MLD)).
19
20 It may happen, for example, that a Supplicant selects a pairwise cipher suite which is advertised by
21 an AP, but which policy disallows for this particular STA. An Authenticator may, therefore, insert a
22
second RSNE to overrule the STA’s selection. An Authenticator’s SME shall insert the second
23
RSNE, after the first RSNE, only for this purpose. The pairwise cipher suite in the second RSNE
24
25 included shall be one of the ciphers advertised by the Authenticator. All other fields in the second

GTK KDE in octets. The entire Key Data field shall be encrypted as specified by the Key Descriptor

30 Version.

25
26
The RSNXEInfo field indicates that the RSNXE is present in the MLO Link KDE when its value is set to 1.
27
28
29 The MAC Address field contains the MAC address of the affiliated STA for the link specified in the Link
30
Information field.
31
32
33 The RSNE field contains the RSNE of the affiliated STA for the link specified in the Link Information field.
34 The RSNE is described in 9.4.2.24 (RSNE).
35
36
The RSNXE field contains the RSNE of the affiliated STA for the link specified in the Link Information
37
38 field. The RSNXE is described in 9.4.2.241 (RSNXE).
39
40 12.7.4 EAPOL-Key frame notation
41
42
Change as follows:
43
44
45 The following notation is used throughout the remainder of 12.7 (Keys and key distribution) and 13.4 (FT
46
initial mobility domain association) to represent EAPOL-Key frames:
47
48
49 EAPOL-Key(S, M, A, I, K, Reserved, KeyRSC, ANonce/SNonce, MIC, {Key Data})
50
51 where
52
53
S means the initial key exchange is complete. This is the Secure bit of the Key Informa-
54
55 tion field.
56 M means the MIC is available in message. This should be set in all messages except
57 message 1 of a 4-way handshake. This is the Key MIC bit of the Key Information
58
field. When the negotiated AKM is 00-0F-AC:14, 00-0F-AC:15, 00-0F-AC:16, or
59
00-0F-AC:17, this Key MIC bit is set to 0 regardless of the M parameter value.
60
61 A means a response is required to this message. This is used when the receiver should
62 respond to this message. This is the Key Ack bit of the Key Information field.
63 I is the Install bit: indicates whether to install (1) or not install (0) for the pairwise key.
64
This is the Install bit of the Key Information field.
65
Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved. 417
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

## 第 418 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 K is the key type: P (Pairwise), G (Group). This is the Key Type bit of the Key Informa-
2 tion field.
3
Reserved reserved.
4
KeyRSC is the key RSC. This is the Key RSC field.
5
6 ANonce/SNonce is the Authenticator or Supplicant nonce, respectively. This is the Key Nonce field.
7 MIC is the integrity check, which is generated using the KCK. This is the Key MIC field.
8 When the negotiated AKM is 00-0F-AC:14, 00-0F-AC:15, 00-0F-AC:16, or 00-0F-
9
AC:17, this Key MIC field is not included regardless of the MIC parameter value.
10
{Key Data} is a sequence of zero or more elements and KDEs, concatenated and contained in the
11
12 Key Data field, where:
13 RSNE is described in 9.4.2.24 (RSNE).
14 RSNE[KeyName]is the RSNE, with the PMKID List field set to KeyName.
15
GTK[N] is the GTK, with the key identifier field set to N. The key identifier specifies
16
which index is used for this GTK. Index 0 shall not be used for GTKs,
17
18 except in mixed environments, as described in 12.7.1 (Key hierarchy).
19 MAC Address is the MLD MAC address of the MLD with which the transmitting STA is affili-
20 ated
21
FTE is the Fast BSS Transition element, described in 9.4.2.47 (Fast BSS Transition
22
element (FTE))
23
24 MDE is the Mobility Domain element, described in 9.4.2.46 (Mobility Domain element
25 (MDE))
26 TIE[IntervalType]is a Timeout Interval element of type IntervalType, as described in
27
9.4.2.48 (Timeout Interval element (TIE)), containing e.g., for type Key-
28
Lifetime, the lifetime of the FT key hierarchy.
29
30 IGTK[M] is the IGTK, with key identifier field set to M.
31 IPN is the current IGTK replay counter value provided by the IGTK KDE
32 BIGTK[Q] is the BIGTK, with key identifier field set to Q
33
BIPN is the current BIGTK replay counter value provided by the BIGTK KDE
34
PMKID is of type PMKID KDE and is the key identifier used during 4-way PTK hand-
35
36 shake for PMK identification.
37 OCI KDE is a KDE containing operating channel information.
38 RSNXE is described in 9.4.2.241 (RSN Extension element (RSNXE)).
39
PMKID identifies the PMKSA selected by the Authenticator.
40
MLO GTK is the GTK for the AP affiliated with the AP MLD for the link specified by the
41
42 value in the LinkID field.
43 MLO IGTK is the IGTK for the AP affiliated with the AP MLD for the link specified by the
44 value in the LinkID field.
45
MLO BIGTK is the BIGTK for the AP affiliated with the AP MLD for the link specified by the
46
value in the LinkID field.
47
48 MLO Link is the MAC address, RSNE, and RSNEX, if advertised, for the STA affiliated
49 with the MLD specified by the value in the LinkID field.
50 “{a} or {b}” means that exactly one of either {a} or {b} is present as the {Key Data}.
51
“a ” means that the KDE could occur multiple times in the field for n links.
52 n
53
54 12.7.5 Nonce generation
55
56
Change the third paragraph as follows:

58
59 The local MAC address should be AA on the Authenticator and SPA on the Supplicant. When the
60 Authenticator is an AP MLD and the Supplicant is a non-AP MLD, the AA shall be the MLD MAC address
61 of the AP MLD and the SPA shall be the MLD address of the non-AP MLD.

1 12.7.6 4-way handshake

12.7.6.1 General
4
5
6 Change the first paragraph as follows:
7
8 RSNA defines a protocol using EAPOL-Key frames called the 4-way handshake. The handshake completes the
9
IEEE 802.1X authentication process. The information flow of the 4-way handshake is as follows:
10
11 Message 1: Authenticator (cid:111) Supplicant: EAPOL-Key(0,0,1,0,P,0,0,ANonce,0,{} or {PMKID} or
12 {MAC Address})
13
Message 2: Supplicant (cid:111) Authenticator: EAPOL-Key(0,1,0,0,P,0,0,SNonce,MIC,{RSNE} or
14
15 {RSNE, OCI KDE} or {RSNE, RSNXE} or {RSNE, OCI KDE, RSNXE} or {RSNE,
16 MAC Address} or {RSNE, RSNXE, MAC Address} or {RSNE, OCI KDE, RSNXE,
17 MAC Address} or {RSNE, MAC Address, MLO Link } or {RSNE, RSNXE, MAC
n
18
Address, MLO Link }or {RSNE, OCI KDE, RSNXE, MAC Address, MLO Link })
19 n n
20 Message 3: Authenticator(cid:111)Supplicant: EAPOL-
21 Key(1,1,1,1,P,0,KeyRSC,ANonce,MIC,{RSNE,GTK[N]} or {RSNE, GTK[N], OCI
22 KDE} or {RSNE, GTK[N], RSNXE} or {RSNE, GTK[N], OCI KDE, RSNXE} or
23
{MAC Address, MLO Link , MLO GTK , MLO IGTK , MLO BIGTK } or {OCI
24 m n n n
KDE, MAC Address, MLO Link , MLO GTK , MLO IGTK , MLO BIGTK })
25 m n n n
26 Message 4: Supplicant (cid:111) Authenticator: EAPOL-Key(1,1,0,0,P,0,0,0,MIC,{} or {MAC
27
Address}).
28
29
30 Change the third paragraph as follows:
31
32
The following apply:
33
34 — EAPOL-Key((cid:152)) denotes an EAPOL-Key frame conveying the specified argument list, using the
35 notation introduced in 12.7.4 (EAPOL-Key frame notation).
36
— ANonce is a nonce that the Authenticator contributes for PTK generation. ANonce has the same
37
38 value in message 1 and message 3.
39 — SNonce is a nonce from the Supplicant for PTK generation.
40
41 — P means the pairwise bit is set.
42 — The MIC is computed over the body of the EAPOL-Key frame (with the Key MIC field first zeroed
43
before the computation) using the KCK defined in 12.7.1.3 (Pairwise key hierarchy) for PTK
44
generation.
45
46 — RSNE represents the appropriate RSNEs. For AP MLD, the RSNE is present in the MLO Link
47
KDE.
48
49 — GTK[N] represents the GTK with its key identifier.
50
— OCI KDE contains the current operating channel information for the operating channel in which the
51
EAPOL-Key frame is sent. OCI KDE is present when
52
53 dot11RSNAOperatingChannelValidationActivated is true on the Supplicant in Message 2 and
54 Authenticator in Message 3. Otherwise it is absent.
55
— RSNXE, when included in message 2, contains the RSNXE that the Supplicant sent in its
56
(Re)Association Request frame, and when included in message 3, contains the RSNXE that the
57
58 Authenticator sent in its Beacon or Probe Response frame. RSNXE is present in message 2 if this
59 element is present in the (Re)Association Request frame that the Supplicant sent, and is present in
60 message 3 if this element is present in the Beacon or Probe Response frame that the Authenticator
61
sent. For AP MLD, the RSNXE is present in the MLO Link KDE.
62
63 — For MLO, each message of the 4-way handshake contains an MAC Address KDE containing the
64 MLD MAC address of the Authenticator or Supplicant that is sending the message.
65

42 The Supplicant also:
43
44 a) Verifies the RSNE and, if present, the RSNXE. If this message 3 is part of a fast BSS transition
45 initial mobility domain association or an association started through the FT protocol, the Supplicant
46 verifies that the PMKR1Name in the PMKID List field of the RSNE is identical to the value it sent
47
in message 2 and verifies that all other fields of the RSNE are identical to the fields in the RSNE
48
present in the Beacon or Probe Response frames and verifies that the FTE and MDE are the same as
49
50 in the (Re)Association Response frame. Otherwise, the Supplicant verifies that the RSNE is
51 identical to that the STA received in the Beacon or Probe Response frame. If the RSNXE is present,
52 the Supplicant verifies that the RSNXE is identical to that the STA received in the Beacon or Probe
53
Response frame. If any of these verification steps indicates a mismatch, the STA shall disassociate
54
or deauthenticate. If a second RSNE is provided in the message, the Supplicant uses the pairwise
55
56 cipher suite specified in the second RSNE or deauthenticates.
57 b) For MLO, verifies the following:

60 requested link are identical to those received for the corresponding affiliated APs of the AP
61 MLD.
62
• The affiliated AP MAC address and all fields in the RSNE and the RSNXE (if present), of other
63
discovered links, if information is available are identical to those received for the affiliated APs
64
65 of the AP MLD.

## 第 432 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 13. Fast BSS transition
2
3
4
13.1 Overview
5
6
7 Change the first four paragraphs as follows:
8
9 Fast BSS transition seeks to reduce the length of time that connectivity is lost between a STA and the DS or
10
between a non-AP MLD and the DS during a BSS transition. The FT protocols are part of the reassociation
11
service and only apply to a STA or an MLD transition to an AP or an AP MLDSTA transitions between APs
12
13 within the same mobility domain within the same ESS (see 4.5.3.2 (Mobility types)).
14
15 The FT protocols require information to be exchanged during the initial association (or a later reassociation)
16
between a STA [known as the FT Originator (FTO)] and AP [known as the FT Responder (FTR)] or
17
between a non-AP MLD [known as the FT Originator (FTO)] and AP MLD [known as the FT Responder
18
19 (FTR)]. The initial exchange is referred to as the FT initial mobility domain association. Subsequent
20 reassociations to APsFTRs within the same mobility domain may make use of the FT protocols.
21
22
Two FT protocols are defined:
23
24 — FT protocol. This protocol is executed when an FTO makes a transition to a target APFTR and does
25 not require a resource request prior to its transition.
26
— FT resource request protocol. This protocol is executed when an FTO requires a resource request
27
28 prior to its transition.
29
30 For an FTO to move from its current AP to a target APFTR utilizing the FT protocols, the message
31
exchanges are performed using one of two methods:
32
33 — Over-the-Air. The FTO communicates directly with the target APFTR using IEEE 802.11
34 authentication with the FT authentication algorithm.
35
— Over-the-DS. The FTO communicates with the target APFTR via the current APFTR. The
36
37 communication between the FTO and the target APFTR is carried in FT Action frames between the
38 FTO and the current APFTR. Between the current APFTR and target APFTR, communication is via
39 an encapsulation method described in 13.10.3 (Remote Request/Response frame definition). The
40
current APFTR converts between the two encapsulations.
41
42
43
13.2 Key holders
44
45
46 13.2.1 Introduction
47
48 Change the second paragraph as follows:
49
50
51 The R0KH and R1KH are part of AP’s or AP MLD’s SME RSNA key management. The computation of
52 PMK-R0 and PMK-R1, and all of the intermediate results in the computations, shall be restricted to the
53 R0KH. The computation of PTK, and all intermediate results in its computation, shall be restricted to the
54
R1KH.
55
56
57
58
59
60
61
62
63
64
65
432 Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved.
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

## 第 433 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 13.2.2 Authenticator key holders
2
3
Change the seventh paragraph as follows:
4
5
6 The R1KH shall meet the following requirements:
7
— The R1KH-ID shall be set to a MAC address of the physical entity that stores the PMK-R1 and uses
8
9 it to generate the PTK. That same MAC address shall be used to advertise the PMK-R1 identity to
10 the STA or non-AP MLD and the R0KH.
11
— The R1KH shall derive and distribute the GTK and IGTK to all connected STAs. If the R1KH
12
13 identifies an AP MLD, the R1KH shall distribute the GTKs and IGTKs for setup links to all
14 connected non-AP MLDs.
15
— If beacon protection is enabled, the R1KH shall derive and distribute the BIGTK and BIPN to all
16
connected STAs. If an R1KH identifies an AP MLD, the R1KH shall derive and distribute the
17
18 BIGTKs and BIPNs for setup links to all connected non-AP MLDs.
19
— When the PMK-R1 lifetime expires, the R1KH shall delete the PMK-R1 PMKSA and shall revoke
20
all PTKSAs derived from the PMK-R1 using the MLME-DELETEKEYS primitive.
21
22 — The R1KH shall not expose the PMK-R1 to other parties.
23
24
13.2.3 Supplicant key holders
25
26
27 Change the second paragraph as follows:
28
29 The S0KH interacts with the IEEE 802.1X functional block (see Figure 4-24 (Portion of the ISO/IEC basic
30
reference model covered in this standard) in 4.9 (Reference model)) to receive the MSK resulting from an
31
EAP authentication or the FILS-FT resulting from a FILS authentication. The S1KH interacts with the IEEE
32
33 802.1X entity to open the Controlled Port. Both the S0KH and S1KH interactions with the IEEE 802.1X
34 entity occur within the SME of a STA or a non-AP MLD.

Insert the following paragraph after the first paragraph (“The FT capability is advertised in...”)

44
the Authentication type column indicates FT authentication.

13.4 FT initial mobility domain association
48
49
50 13.4.1 Overview
51
52 Change as follows:
53
54
The FT initial mobility domain association is the first (re)association in the mobility domain, where the
55
56 SME of the STA or non-AP MLD enables its future use of the FT procedures.
57
58 FT initial mobility domain association is typically the first association within the ESS. In addition to
59
Association Request and Response frames, Reassociation Request and Response frames are supported in the
60
initial mobility domain association to enable both FT and non-FT APs or AP MLDs to be present in a single
61
62 ESS.
63
64

1 NOTE—For MLO, the non-AP MLD and AP MLD include the Basic Multi-Link element in all Authentication and

9
10 A STA or a non-AP MLD indicates its support for the FT procedures by including the MDE in the
11
(Re)Association Request frame and indicates its support of security by including the RSNE. The AP or AP
12

13
14 successful IEEE 802.1X authentication (if needed) or SAE authentication, the STA and AP or the non-AP
15 MLD and the AP MLD perform an FT 4-way handshake. At the end of the sequence, the IEEE 802.1X
16 Controlled Port is opened, and the FT key hierarchy has been established. The message flow between a STA
17
and an AP is shown in Figure 13-2 (FT initial mobility domain association in an RSN).

## 第 435 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 If the contents of the MDE received by the AP do not match the contents advertised in the Beacon and Probe
2
Response frames, the AP shall reject the (Re)Association Request frame with status code
3
STATUS_INVALID_MDE. If an MDE is present in the (Re)Association Request frame and the contents of
4
5 the RSNE do not indicate a negotiated AKM for which the Authentication type column indicates FT
6 authentication (see Table 9-151 (AKM suite selectors)), the AP shall reject the (Re)Association Request
7 frame with status code STATUS_INVALID_AKMP.
8
9
For MLO, if the contents of the MDE received by the AP MLD do not match the contents advertised in the
10
11 Beacon and Probe Response frames of APs affiliated with the AP MLD, the AP MLD shall reject the
12 (Re)Association Request frame with status code STATUS_INVALID_MDE. If an MDE is present in the
13 (Re)Association Request frame and the contents of the RSNE do not indicate a negotiated AKM for which
14
the Authentication type column indicates FT authentication (see Table 9-151 (AKM suite selectors)), the AP
15
MLD shall reject the (Re)Association Request frame with status code STATUS_INVALID_AKMP.
16
17
18 The (Re)Association Response frame from the AP shall contain an MDE, with contents as presented in
19 Beacon and Probe Response frames. The (Re)Association Response frame from the AP MLD shall contain
20
an MDE, with contents as presented in Beacon and Probe Response frames of APs affiliated with the AP
21
MLD. The FTE shall include the key holder identities of the AP or the AP MLD, the R0KH-ID and R1KH-
22
23 ID, set to the values of dot11FTR0KeyHolderID and dot11FTR1KeyHolderID, respectively. The FTE shall
24 have a MIC element count of zero (i.e., no MIC present) and have ANonce, SNonce, and MIC fields set to 0.
25 The RSNXE Used subfield of the MIC Control field shall be set to 0.
26
27
On successful (re)association, the S0KH on the STA or the non-AP MLD and the R0KH on the AP or the AP
28
29 MLD, respectively, then proceed with an IEEE 802.1X authentication using EAPOL PDUs carried in IEEE
30 802.11 Data frames if SAE authentication was not performed (i.e., if the suite type is not 00-0F-AC:9). The
31 S0KH shall use the value of R0KH-ID as the endpoint identifier of the NAS Client (NAS-Identifier if
32
RADIUS is used) in the exchange as defined in IETF RFC 3748 [B37].
33
34
35 If IEEE 802.1X authentication was performed, then upon successful completion of authentication, the
36 R0KH receives the MSK and authorization attributes. If SAE authentication was performed, the R0KH
37 receives the PMK, resulting in the successful completion of SAE. If a key hierarchy already exists for this
38
STA or non-AP MLD belonging to the same mobility domain (i.e., having the same MDID), the R0KH shall
39
delete the existing PMK-R0 security association and PMK-R1 security associations. It then calculates the
40
41 PMK-R0, PMKR0Name, and PMK-R1 and makes the PMK-R1 available to the R1KH of the AP with
42 which the STA is associated or the AP MLD with which the non-AP MLD is associated.
43
44
If the SME of the STA or the non-AP MLD cannot authenticate the AS, then it shall disassociate with an
45
MLME-DISASSOCIATE.request primitive. If the AS signals the Authenticator that the STA or the non-AP
46
47 MLD cannot be authenticated, then the SME of the AP or the AP MLD, respectively, shall disassociate with
48 an MLME-DISASSOCIATE.request primitive.
49
50
If the MSK lifetime attribute is provided by the AS, the lifetime of the PMK-R0 shall not be more than the
51
lifetime of the MSK. If the MSK lifetime attribute is not provided, the PMK-R0 lifetime shall be
52
53 dot11FTR0KeyLifetime. For PSK, the PMK-R0 lifetime shall be dot11FTR0KeyLifetime. The lifetime of
54 the PMK-R1s and PTK shall be the same as the lifetime of PMK-R0. When the key lifetime expires, each
55 key holder shall delete its respective PMK-R0, PMK-R1, and PTK SAs.
56
57
The R1KH and S1KH then perform an FT 4-way handshake. The EAPOL-Key frame notation is defined in
58
59 12.7.4 (EAPOL-Key frame notation).

n
17

20 Deadline], TIE[KeyLifetime]})

n n n

27 12.7.4 (EAPOL-Key frame notation).
28
29
Between a non-AP MLD and an AP MLD, if RSNA has not been established, each message of the FT 4-way
30
handshake shall be sent on the same link used by the latest exchange of successful (Re)Association Request/
31
32 Response frame.
33
34 The message sequence is described in 12.7.6 (4-way handshake).
35
36
It is assumed by this standard that the reassociation deadline is administered consistently across the mobility
37
38 domain. The mechanism for such consistent administration is outside the scope of this standard.
39
40 The PTK shall be calculated by the R1KH and S1KH according to the procedures given in 12.7.1.6.5 (PTK).
41
42
Upon completion of a successful FT 4-way handshake, the IEEE 802.1X Controlled Port shall be opened on
43
44 both the non-AP STA and the AP or the non-AP MLD and the AP MLD. Subsequent EAPOL-Key frames
45 shall use the key replay counter to detect replayed messages.
46
47
Upon completion of a successful FT 4-way handshake, the PTK lifetime timer is initiated. The operation of
48
this timer prevents the PTKSA being used for longer than the value provided in the TIE[KeyLifetime] sent
49
50 in message 3.
51
52 Once the PTKSA key lifetime expires, as indicated by the TIE[KeyLifetime], to continue its association in
53
the mobility domain the STA or the non-AP MLD shall perform the FT initial mobility domain association
54
procedures. If the AP or the AP MLD sends a Deauthentication or Disassociation frame to the STA or the
55
56 non-AP MLD, respectively, with reason code INVALID_AUTHENTICATION, then to continue its
57 association in the mobility domain, the STA or the non-AP MLD shall perform the FT initial mobility
58 domain association procedures with any AP or any AP MLD, respectively, in the mobility domain. If the
59
Supplicant EAPOL state machines are triggered to send an EAPOL-Start frame after a successful initial
60
mobility domain association, the STA or the non-AP MLD shall perform the FT initial mobility domain
61
62 association procedures.

13.5 FT protocol
7
8
9 13.5.1 Overview
10
11
Change as follows:
12
13
14 STAs and MLDs with dot11FastBSSTransitionActivated equal to true shall support the FT protocol.
15
16 The FT protocol supports resource requests as part of the reassociation. The optional FT resource request
17
protocol (see 13.6 (FT resource request protocol)) supports resource requests prior to reassociation.
18
19
20 A STAFTO shall not use any authentication algorithm except the FT authentication algorithm when using
21 the FT protocol.
22
23
To prevent key reinstallation attacks, the non-AP STA shall maintain a copy of the most recent GTK, IGTK,
24
and BIGTK when present installed as part of the FT protocol as if they were installed as a result of receipt of
25
26 EAPOL-Key frames (see 12.7.7.4 (Group key handshake implementation considerations)) and shall refuse
27 to update a GTK, IGTK, or a BIGTK when the key to be set matches anyeither one of these two keys (see
28 6.3.19 (SetKeys)).

## 第 439 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 The FTO and APFTR use the FT authentication sequence to specify the PMK-R1 security association and to
2
provide values of SNonce and ANonce that enable a liveness proof, replay protection, and PTK separation.
3
This exchange enables a fresh PTK to be computed in advance of reassociation. The PTKSA is used to
4
5 protect the subsequent reassociation transaction, including the optional RIC-Request.
6
7 To perform an over-the-air fast BSS transition to a target APFTR, the FTO and target APFTR shall perform
8
the following exchange:
9
10 FTO(cid:111)Target APFTR: Authentication-Request (FTAA, 0, RSNE[PMKR0Name], MDE,
11 FTE[SNonce, R0KH-ID], Basic Multi-Link element)
12
Target APFTR(cid:111)FTO: Authentication-Response (FTAA, Status, RSNE[PMKR0Name], MDE,
13
14 FTE[ANonce, SNonce, R1KH-ID, R0KH-ID], Basic Multi-Link element)
15
16 where the Basic Multi-Link element is included when the target FTR is an AP MLD.
17
18
The SME of the FTO initiates the authentication exchange, through the use of the MLME-
19
20 AUTHENTICATE.request primitive, and the SME of the APFTR responds with an MLME-
21 AUTHENTICATE.response primitive. See 11.3.5 (Authentication and deauthentication). The MLME
22 primitives for Authentication when the FT authentication algorithm is selected use only authentication
23
transaction sequence number values 1 and 2.
24
25
26 In the Authentication-Request frame that does not include the Basic Multi-Link element, the SA field of the
27 message header shall be set to the MAC address of the FTO, and the DA field of the message header shall be
28 set to the BSSID of the target AP’s BSS. In the Authentication-Request frame that includes the Basic Multi-
29
Link element, the Address 1 (RA) field and the Address 2 (TA) field of the message header shall be set as
30
defined in 35.3.2 (Multi-link device addressing).
31
32
33 The elements in the frame, and their required contents, shall be as given in 13.8.2 (FT authentication
34 sequence: contents of first message).
35
36
If the contents of the MDE received by the APFTR do not match the contents advertised in the Beacon and
37
38 Probe Response frames if the FTR is an AP or in the Beacon and Probe Response frames of any AP
39 affiliated with the FTR if the FTR is an AP MLD, the APFTR shall reject the authentication request with
40 status code STATUS_INVALID_MDE. If the Authentication-Request frame contains an authentication
41
algorithm equal to FT authentication and the contents of the RSNE do not indicate a negotiated AKM for
42
which the Authentication type column indicates FT authentication (see Table 9-151 (AKM suite selectors)),
43
44 the APFTR shall reject the authentication request with status code STATUS_INVALID_AKMP. If the FTE
45 in the FT Request frame contains an invalid R0KH-ID, the APFTR shall reject the FT Request frame with
46 status code STATUS_INVALID_FTE. If the RSNE in the Authentication-Request frame contains an invalid
47
PMKR0Name and the APFTR has determined that it is an invalid PMKR0Name, the APFTR shall reject the
48
authentication request with status code STATUS_INVALID_PMKID. If the requested R0KH is not
49
50 reachable, the APFTR shall respond to the authentication request with status code
51 R0KH_UNREACHABLE. If the FTO selects a pairwise cipher suite in the RSNE that is different from the
52 ones used in the Initial mobility domain association, then the APFTR shall reject the authentication request
53
with status code STATUS_INVALID_PAIRWISE_CIPHER. Subsequent to a rejection of an authentication
54
request, the FTO may retry the authentication request.
55
56
57 In the Authentication-Response frame that does not include the Basic Multi-Link element, the SA field of
58 the message header shall be set to the BSSID of the target AP’s BSS, and the DA field of the message header
59
shall be set to the MAC address of the FTO. In the Authentication-Response frame that includes the Basic
60
Multi-Link element, the Address 1 (RA) field and the Address 2 (TA) field of the message header shall be
61
62 set as defined in 35.3.2 (Multi-link device addressing).

6 The R1KH of the target APFTR uses the value of PMKR0Name and other information in the frame to

17 value.

21
association). If the Status Code field value returned by the target APFTR is SUCCESS, the FTO and target
22
23 APFTR transition to State 2 (as defined in 11.3 (STA authenticationAuthentication and association)); the
24 FTO may continue with reassociation (13.7.1 (FT reassociation in an RSN)). Handling of errors returned in
25 the Status Code field shall be as specified in 11.3 (STA authenticationAuthentication and association).

50 If the contents of the MDE received by the target APFTR do not match the contents advertised in the Beacon

54

59

60

61

64 reassociation to this APtarget FTR by using the status code REQUEST_DECLINED. If the FTO selects a

13.6 FT resource request protocol
37
38
39 13.6.1 Introduction
40
41
Change the second and third paragraphs as follows:
42
43
44 APsFTRs capable of fast BSS transition may allow FTOs to request resources prior to reassociation.
45 Availability of the FT resource request protocol is advertised by the target APFTR in the MDE. If the
46 Resource Request Protocol Capability subfield is 0, then the FTO shall not send an Authentication-Confirm
47
nor FT Confirm frame to the APFTR. An APFTR that receives an Authentication-Confirm or FT Confirm
48
frame from a STAFTO and does not support the FT resource request protocol shall respond with status code
49
50 INVALID_PARAMETERS.
51
52 The additional message exchange for the FT resource request protocol shall be performed using the same
53
method (over-the-air or over-the-DS) as was used for the Authentication-Request/Response frame or FT
54
Request/Response frame. An APA FTR that receives an FT Confirm frame that did not previously receive
55
56 an FT Request frame from the same STAFTO shall reject the request with status code
57 STATUS_INVALID_FT_ACTION_FRAME_COUNT. An APA FTR that receives an Authentication-
58 Confirm frame that did not previously receive an Authentication-Request frame from the same STAFTO
59
shall reject the request with status code TRANSACTION_SEQUENCE_ERROR.
60

50
the PTKSA has been established and proven live. The key replay counter shall be initialized to 0, and the

10 deadline interval received during the FT initial mobility domain association, the target APFTR may delete
11 the PTKSA, and the FTO shall abandon this transition attempt.
12
13
The FTO shall perform a reassociation directly with the target APFTR via the following exchange:
14
15 FTO(cid:111)Target APFTR: Reassociation Request(RSNE[PMKR1Name], MDE, FTE[MIC, ANonce,
16 SNonce, R1KH-ID, R0KH-ID], RIC-Request, RSNXE, Basic Multi-Link element)
17
Target APFTR(cid:111)FTO: Reassociation Response(RSNE[PMKR1Name], MDE, FTE[MIC, ANonce,
18

20 BIGTK ], RIC-Response, RSNXE, Basic Multi-Link element)
n
21

where
23

25 fied by the value in the Link ID field,

34 n n n

38

primitive. See 11.3.6 (Association, reassociation, and disassociation).

44

54 discard the request if the MIC is incorrect.

1 Otherwise, the FTO rejects the Reassociation Response frame by discarding the frame.
2
3
If an FTO is performing a reassociation exchange as part of the FT resource request protocol, then the FTO
4
5 shall not include the RIC-Request in the Reassociation Request frame, and the AP shall not include the RIC-
6 Response in the Reassociation Response frame. If the reassociation exchange is part of the FT resource
7 request protocol and the AP is unable to honor the resources that have been placed in the accepted state for
8
that FTO, then the AP shall reject the Reassociation Request frame and may use status code
9

10
11
12 If the FTO did not utilize the FT resource request protocol, the FTO may make a request for resources by
13 including a RIC-Request (see 13.11 (Resource request procedures)) in the Reassociation Request frame. The
14
RIC-Request is generated by the procedures of 13.11.3.1 (FTO procedures), and the RIC-Response is
15

19 REFUSED_REASON_UNSPECIFIED, TRANSACTION_SEQUENCE_ERROR, or
20
REJECTED_SEQUENCE_TIMEOUT, then the FTO shall abandon this transition attempt. Handling of
21
other errors returned in the Status Code field shall be as specified in 11.3 (STA authenticationAuthentication
22
23 and association).
24
25 Upon a successful reassociation, the PTKSA has been established and proven live. The SME of the APFTR
26
shall open the IEEE 802.1X Controlled Port. The FTO shall transition to State 4 (as defined in 11.3 (STA
27
authenticationAuthentication and association)). If the target APFTR is distinct from the previous APFTR,
28
29 the FTO shall enter State 1 with respect to the previous APFTR.
30
31 Upon a successful reassociation, the FTO shall delete any corresponding PTKSA with its previous APFTR.
32
The SME of the FTO shall issue an MLME-DELETEKEYS.request primitive to delete the pairwise keys
33
with the previous APFTR, and the FTO and the APFTR shall issue an MLME-SETKEYS.request primitive
34
35 and MLME-SETPROTECTION.request primitive to install the pairwise keys. The PTK lifetime timer shall
36 be initialized with the value calculated as the difference between the TIE[KeyLifetime] sent in message 3 of
37 the FT initial mobility domain association and the time since the completion of the FT 4-way handshake
38
during the FT initial mobility domain association.
39
40
41 When the IEEE 802.1X Controlled Port is opened, the EAPOL-Key frame replay counter shall be initialized
42 to 0. The R1KH shall increment the key replay counter on each successive EAPOL-Key frame that it
43 transmits.
44
45
46 13.8 FT authentication sequence
47
48
13.8.1 Overview
49
50
51 Change (including the last entry of Table 13-1 (not all lines shown)) as follows:
52
53
The FT authentication sequence comprises four sets of FT elements. Each set of FT elements is referred to in
54
13.8 (FT authentication sequence) as a message. These messages are included in the FT Protocol frames or
55
56 FT Resource Request Protocol frames to initiate a fast BSS transition. The FT authentication sequence is
57 always initiated by the FTO and responded to by the target APFTR.
58
59
In an RSN, the first two messages in the sequence allow the FTO and target APFTR to provide association
60
instance identifiers, SNonce and ANonce, respectively. SNonce and ANonce are chosen randomly or
61
62 pseudorandomly and are used to generate a fresh PTK. The first two messages also enable the target APFTR
63 to provision the PMK-R1 and the FTO and target APFTR to compute the PTK. The third and fourth
64
65
450 Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved.
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

## 第 451 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 messages demonstrate liveness of the peer, authenticate the elements, and enable an authenticated resource
2
request.
3
4
5 When an FTO invokes the FT protocol, then the first two messages of the sequence are both carried in
6 Authentication frames or both carried in Action frames, and these messages are described in 13.8.2 (FT
7 authentication sequence: contents of first message) and 13.8.3 (FT authentication sequence: contents of
8
second message). The third and fourth messages in the sequence are carried in the Reassociation Request
9
and Reassociation Response frames and are described in 13.8.4 (FT authentication sequence: contents of
10
11 third message) and 13.8.5 (FT authentication sequence: contents of fourth message).
12
13 When the FTO invokes the FT resource request protocol, then the first four messages of the sequence are all
14
carried in Authentication frames or all carried in Action frames, and these messages are described in 13.8.2
15
(FT authentication sequence: contents of first message) to 13.8.5 (FT authentication sequence: contents of
16
17 fourth message). The fifth and sixth frames of the FT resource request protocol are carried in the
18 Reassociation Request frame and Reassociation Response frame and are described in 13.8.4 (FT
19 authentication sequence: contents of third message) and 13.8.5 (FT authentication sequence: contents of
20
fourth message).
21
22
23 Regardless of the transport mechanism, the information contained in the FT authentication sequence
24 consists of the set of elements shown in Table 13-1 (FT authentication elements).
25
26
27
Table 13-1—FT authentication elements
28
29
30
Information Presence in Authentication Sequence messages Description
31
32
RSNXE The RSNXE is present in the third message if an RSNXE is 9.4.2.241 (RSN
33
present in a Beacon or Probe Response frame that the FTO has Extension element
34
received from the target AP or an AP affiliated with the target (RSNXE))
35
AP MLD and the FTO set to 1 any subfield, except the Field
36

37
this element, and is present in the fourth message if an
38
RSNXE was present in the third message and the target AP or
39
an AP affiliated with the target AP MLD set to 1 any subfield,
40
except the Field Length subfield, of the Extended RSN
41
Capabilities field in this element.
42
43
44
45 The first message is used by the FTO to initiate a fast BSS transition. When RSNA is enabled, the FTO shall
46
include the R0KH-ID and the SNonce in the FTE and the PMKR0Name in the RSNE. The target APFTR
47
can use the PMKR0Name to derive the PMKR1Name, and if the target APFTR does not have the PMK-R1
48
49 identified by PMKR1Name, it may attempt to retrieve that key from the R0KH identified by R0KH-ID. See
50 13.2 (Key holders). The FTO includes a fresh SNonce as its contribution to the association instance
51 identifier and to provide key separation of the derived PTK; it is selected randomly to serve as a challenge
52
that demonstrates the liveness of the peer in the fourth message.
53
54
55 The second message is used by the target APFTR to respond to the requesting FTO. The target APFTR
56 provides the key holder identifiers and key names used to generate the PTK. The target APFTR also includes
57 a fresh ANonce as its contribution to the association instance identifier and to provide key separation of the
58
derived PTK. The response includes a status code.
59
60
61 In an RSN, the third message is used by the FTO to assert to the target APFTR that it has a valid PTK. If no
62 resources are required, then the FTO omits inclusion of the RIC.
63
64
65

1 wrapped using KEK or KEK2 and the appropriate key wrap algorithm, as specified in Table 12-
2
11 (Integrity and key wrap algorithms) and 12.7.2 (EAPOL-Key frames). The padding consists of
3
appending a single octet 0xdd followed by zero or more 0x00 octets. When processing a received
4
5 message, the receiver shall ignore this trailing padding. Addition of padding does not change the

HMAC-SHA-384 algorithm. The output of the HMAC-SHA-384 shall be truncated to 192 bits.
21
22 — The MIC shall be calculated on the concatenation of the following data, in the order given here:
23
— FTO’s MAC address (6 octets)
24
25 — Target AP’sFTR’s MAC address (6 octets)
26
— Transaction sequence number (1 octet), which shall be set to the value 6 if this is a
27
Reassociation Response frame or, otherwise, set to the value 4
28
29 — RSNE if Basic Multi-Link element is not included in the Reassociation Response frame
30
— RSNEs corresponding to all requested links in increasing order of link ID if Basic Multi-Link
31
32 element is included in the Reassociation Response frame
33 — MDE
34
35 — FTE and corresponding Fragment element(s) (if FTE is fragmented), with the MIC field of the
36 FTE set to 0
37
— Contents of the RIC-Response (if present)
38
39 — RSNXE (if present) if Basic Multi-Link element is not included in the Reassociation Response
40 frame
41
— RSNXEs (if present) corresponding to all requested links in increasing order of link ID if Basic
42
43 Multi-Link element is included in the Reassociation Response frame
44
— AP MAC address corresponding to all requested links in increasing order of link ID if Basic
45
Multi-Link element is included in the Reassociation Response frame
46
47 — All other fields shall be set to 0.
48
49
If this message is other than a Reassociation Response frame and dot11RSNAActivated is false, a TIE may
50
appear. If this message is other than a Reassociation Response frame, includes a RIC-Response, and
51
52 dot11RSNAActivated is false, then a timeout interval shall appear. If it appears, it shall be set as follows:
53
— Timeout Interval Type field shall be set to 1 (reassociation deadline)
54
55 — Timeout Interval Value field shall be set to the reassociation deadline time.
56
57 If resources were requested by the FTO, then a RIC-Response shall be included.
58
59
The RSNXE shall be present if an RSNXE was present in the third message and the target APFTR if the
60
61 FTR is an AP or an AP affiliated with the target FTR if the FTR is an AP MLD set to 1 any subfield, except
62 the Field Length subfield, of the Extended RSN Capabilities field in this element.

## 第 456 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 13.9 FT security architecture state machines
2
3
13.9.1 Introduction
4
5
6 Change as follows:
7
8
The interactions between the R0KH and IEEE Std 802.1X, between the R1KH and IEEE Std 802.1X, and
9
between the S1KH and IEEE Std 802.1X occur within the SME. At both the target APFTR and at the FTO,
10
11 the R1KH and S1KH initialize the IEEE 802.1X EAPOL state machines in the respective SMEs. The
12 Controlled Port is opened without an EAP exchange when the reassociation completes.
13
14
13.9.5 S1KH state machine
15
16
17 13.9.5.3 S1KH state machine variables
18
19 Change the third item of the first paragraph as follows (not all items shown):
20
21
The following list summarizes the variables used by the S1KH state machine:
22
23 — …
24
— Init – This variable is set to true to initialize the S1KH state machine. In addition, this variable is
25
26 used to restart the state machine when transitioning to a new APFTR.
27
28
29 13.10 Remote request broker (RRB) communication
30
31
13.10.1 Overview
32
33
Change as follows:
34
35
36 The RRB mechanism allows the FTO to communicate with a target APFTR through the FTO’s existing
37
association (with the current APFTR). The FTO transmits an FT Action frame (including the address of the
38
FTO and the BSSID of the target AP’s BSSMAC address of the target FTR) to the current APFTR. The
39
40 current APFTR includes the contents of the FT Action frame (Request or Confirm) inside a Remote Request
41 frame and transmits it to the target APFTR over the DS. The target APFTR processes the remote request and
42 responds to the FTO by sending an FT Action frame (Response or Acknowledgment) through the current
43
APFTR.
44
45
46 The SME of the FTO initiates an exchange with a target APFTR by issuing an MLME-
47 REMOTEREQUEST. request primitive with parameters including the contents of the FT Action frame to be
48 sent. The MAC of the FTO transmits this Action frame. When the MAC of the current APFTR receives an
49
FT Action frame, it passes it to the RRB by use of an MLME-REMOTE-REQUEST.indication primitive,
50
with parameters including the contents of the received Action frame.
51
52
53 When the RRB of the current APFTR has received a response from the target APFTR, it uses the MLME-
54 REMOTEREQUEST.request primitive to send the response, as an FT Action frame, to the requesting FTO.
55
The MAC of the current APFTR transmits this Action frame. When the MAC of the FTO receives an FT
56
Action frame, the MAC passes the Action frame to the SME by use of an MLME-REMOTE-
57
58 REQUEST.indication primitive, with parameters including the contents of the received Action frame.
59
60
61
62
63
64
65
456 Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved.
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

## 第 457 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 13.10.2 Remote request broker (RRB)
2
3
Change (including Figure 13-20) as follows:
4
5
6 The RRB resides in the SME on the APsFTRs and acts as a forwarding agent (at the current APFTR and
7 termination point (at the target APFTR) for protocol messages over the DS.
8
9
The RRB allows APsFTRs that are part of the same mobility domain to exchange information over the DS.
10
11 APsFTRs that advertise the same MDID shall be reachable over the DS and support the over-the-DS
12 communication.
13
14
As a termination point, when the RRB at the target APFTR receives a request frame from the current
15
APFTR, it interacts with the MAC and other parts of the SME to process the request and respond with a
16
17 Remote Response frame, through the RRB on the current APFTR, back to the requesting FTO.
18
19 As a forwarding agent, when the RRB at the current APFTR receives a request from an FTO directed to
20
another APFTR in the same mobility domain, the current APFTR forwards the request to that target APFTR.
21
The RRB on the current APFTR converts Action frames into Remote Request frames and converts Remote
22
23 Response frames into Action frames.
24
25 The target APFTR and the current APFTR shall reside in the same mobility domain. The RRB on the current
26
APFTR shall transmit Remote Request frames to the target APFTR based on the BSSID of the target AP’s
27
BSSMAC address of the target FTR (supplied in the FT Action frames) using the same procedures as
28
29 preauthentication, as described in 12.6.10.2 (Preauthentication and RSNA key management).
30
31 The message flow for a resource request over the DS is given in Figure 13-20 (Sample message flow for
32
over-the-DS resource request). The FTO indicates the destination target AP BSSIDFTR as part of the FT
33

13.10.3 Remote Request/Response frame definition
39
40
41 Change the first two paragraphs as follows:
42
43 This subclause defines a mechanism to transport the remote request and remote response between the
44
current APFTR and the target APFTR. Any other mechanism may be used.
45
46
47 The Remote Request frame is transmitted over the DS from the current APFTR to the target APFTR. The
48 Payload for the Remote Request/Response frame is given in Table 13-2 (Remote Request/Response Payload
49 format). Remote Request/Response frames shall use an EtherType of 89-0d, as specified in Annex H. The
50
Remote Request/Response frame contains Version, Type, and Length fields, along with the AP Address.
51
52
53 Change the fifth paragraph as follows:
54
55 The AP Address field shall be set to the BSSID of the current AP’s BSSMAC address of the current FTR.
56
The target APFTR shall use this address as the destination address when sending the Remote Response
57
frame as a response to the Remote Request.

## 第 459 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 13.11 Resource request procedures
2
3
13.11.1 General
4
5
6 Change the first two paragraphs as follows:
7
8
When using the resource request procedure, the FTO has the option to request a resource allocation at the
9
target APFTR. To request resources, the FTO creates a resource information container (RIC) and inserts it in
10
11 an appropriate request message to the target APFTR. The request message is sent to the target APFTR either
12 directly (over the air), or via the current APFTR (over the DS), according to the FT procedures described in
13 13.5 (FT protocol) and 13.6 (FT resource request protocol). In an RSNA, resource requests and responses
14
are exchanged only after the establishment of the PTK and are protected by MICs.
15
16
17 The RIC contains a complete list of resources requested by the FTO. An APA FTR that receives a resource
18 request from an FTO shall discard any previous resource request from that FTO. In an RSN, this resource
19 request shall first be authenticated by the APFTR through checking of the MIC before the APFTR discards
20
any previous resource request.
21
22
23 13.11.2 Resource information container (RIC)
24
25 Change the second entry of Table 13-3 as follows (not all lines shown):
26
27
28
Table 13-3—Resource types and resource descriptor definitions
29
30
31

32
33

34
Parameters (RIC Descriptor element)), containing a in 11.5 (Block ack operation) or 35.3.8 (Block
35
Resource Type field identifying Block Ack. ack procedures in Multi-link operation).
36
37

38

39

Change the 11th paragraph as follows (Figure 13-28 not shown):
44
45
46 When sent by an APa target TR in response to a RIC-Request, the RIC-Response consists of a list of one or
47 more Resource Responses including one response for each of the Resource Requests that was contained in
48 the RIC-Request. The basic format of a RIC-Response is shown in Figure 13-28 (RIC-Response format).
49
50
Change the 12th paragraph as follows:
51
52
53 Each Resource Response consists of an RDE with the RDE identifier matching the RDE identifier in the
54 request, in the same order as the RDEs appeared in the request. The RDE is followed by zero or one
55
Resource Descriptors. If the request was not successful (as indicated in the RDE status), then the APtarget
56
FTR may include a suggestion that could have been successful. If the resource request was successful, then
57
58 the particular Resource Descriptor (of the alternatives given by the FTO) is included in the response, as
59 modified by the APtarget FTR during the processing of the resource request. For example, when the
60 resource being requested is QoS for upstream traffic, the TSPEC element may be followed by a Schedule
61
element.

## 第 460 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 13.11.3 Creation and handling of a resource request
2
3
13.11.3.1 FTO procedures
4
5
6 Change the first paragraph as follows:
7
8
The resource request enables an FTO to request resources based on specified Resource Descriptors (e.g.,
9
TSPECs) before or at the time the FTO associates with the target APFTR. In using TSPECs for requesting
10
11 QoS resources, the TSPECs in the request need not belong to only active TSs; the FTO can send TSPECs for
12 any TS that it intends to use after the transition and request the same resources that would be requested by a
13 later ADDTS exchange. For each resource, the FTO may provide the APFTR with a choice of Resource
14
Descriptors in order of preference, any one of which meets the needs of the application.
15
16
Change the third paragraph as follows:
17
18
19 The FTO shall indicate the resources required at the target APFTR. For QoS resources, each TS shall be
20
requested by a separate RDE and associated TSPEC(s). The RDE Identifier field in the RDE shall be an
21
arbitrary value chosen by the FTO that uniquely identifies the RDE within the RIC. The Status Code field
22
23 shall be set to SUCCESS, and the Resource Count field shall be set to the number of alternative Resource
24 Descriptors that follow.
25
26 Change the seventh and eighth paragraphs as follows:
27
28
29 If the frame containing the response to the resource request contains a status code other than SUCCESS, the
30 FTO considers that the request has failed and that no resources are being held at the target APFTR.
31
32
The response from the target APFTR contains a RIC-Response, with the RDEs in the response indicating
33
which resources were considered by the target APFTR and the setting of the status code indicating which
34
35 Resource Descriptors were accepted by the APtarget FTR.
36
37 Change the last paragraph as follows:
38
39
A response to a successful resource request (other than in a Reassociation Request frame) may contain a
40
41 reassociation deadline. If the FTO does not initiate a Reassociation Request frame with the target APFTR
42 within the reassociation deadline (if appropriate), then the APtarget FTR releases resources held for that
43 FTO.
44
45
13.11.3.2 APFTR procedures
46
47
48 Change the first paragraph as follows:
49
50
When a RIC appears in a request message, the APFTR shall check its ability to allocate one resource for
51
each RDE in the RIC in the order appearing in the RIC. In a Reassociation Request frame, the QoS
52
53 Capability element shall be processed prior to the QoS resource requests in the RIC.
54
55 Change the second paragraph (including the figure caption of Figure 13-30) as follows (Figure
56 13-30 not shown):
57
58
59 The behavior of the APFTR shall be identical to that described in the flowchart in Figure 13-30 (Overview
60 of RIC processing at an APa FTR).
61

## 第 461 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 Change the third, fourth, and fifth paragraphs as follows:
2
3
As shown in Figure 13-30 (Overview of RIC processing at an APa FTR), the Resource Descriptors are
4
5 examined by the APFTR in the order presented, and the first that could have been allocated is accepted.
6 Thus the preference ordering by the FTO is honored.
7
8
The target APFTR’s SME examines the resource requests in the RIC. For requests that require processing by
9
the MAC sublayer, the SME generates an MLME-RESOURCE-REQUEST-LOCAL.request primitive. The
10
11 MAC shall respond with MLME-RESOURCE-REQUEST-LOCAL.confirm primitive that indicates
12
13 whether the MAC has accepted the resource request. The SME may also send these resource requests to an
14
external entity such as a backend QoS module for its consideration; these procedures are beyond the scope
15
of this standard. The acceptance of a TSPEC by the target AP results in the resource allocation for a TS at
16
17 the target AP.
18
19 In response to a RIC-Request, the APFTR shall construct a RIC-Response. The RIC-Response shall contain
20
one RDE for each RDE in the RIC-Request. The RDEs shall be in the same order as in the request, and the
21
RDE Identifier field in each RDE shall be the value of the RDE Identifier field in the corresponding RDE in
22
23 the request. The Status Code field in the RDE shall be set according to the result of the allocation request as
24 follows:
25
— Status code = SUCCESS indicates that the resource request has been accepted. The RDE shall also
26
be followed by the Resource Descriptor that was accepted.
27
28 — Status code = not SUCCESS indicates that the resources could not be accepted. The Status Code
29
field contains a value from 9.4.1.9 (Status Code field) indicating the reason for the failure. In this
30
case, the APFTR may include a single Resource Descriptor following the RDE indicating a sug-
31
32 gested resource that could have been accepted. The Resource Count field shall be set to 0 or 1
33 depending whether the suggested Resource Descriptor is attached. A not SUCCESS status code in an
34 RDE shall not cause a not SUCCESS status code in the frame containing the RIC.
35

49 17.3.5.2 SERVICE field
50
51
Change the paragraph and replace Figure 17-6 (SERVICE field bit assignment) as follows:
52
53
54 The SERVICE field has 16 bits, which shall be denoted as bits 0–15. The bit 0 shall be transmitted first in
55 time. The bits from 0–6 of the SERVICE field, which are transmitted first, are set to 0s and are used to
56 synchronize the descrambler in the receiver. If the CH_BANDWIDTH_IN_NON_HT parameter in the
57
TXVECTOR primitive is not present or is present and is equal to CBW20, CBW40, CBW80, CBW160, or
58
CBW80+80, then bit 7 of the SERVICE field is set to 0. If the CH_BANDWIDTH_IN_NON_HT parameter
59
60 in the TXVECTOR primitive is present and is equal to CBW320, then bit 7 of the SERVICE field is set to 1.
61 The remaining 98 bits (78–15) of the SERVICE field shall be reserved for future use. All reserved bits shall
62 be set to 0 on transmission and ignored on reception. Refer to Figure 17-6 (SERVICE field bit assignment).

20

If the non-AP STA is NSTR limited and the conditions above are met, then the non-AP STA may commence
30
31 transmission of a CTS frame response at the SIFS time boundary after the end of the received PPDU.

6
7
8 35.1 Introduction
9
10 An EHT STA has dot11EHTOptionImplemented equal to true.
11
12
An EHT STA supports the MAC and MLME functions defined in Clause 35 (Extremely high throughput
13
14 (EHT) MAC specification) in addition to the MAC functions defined in Clause 26 (High efficiency (HE)
15 MAC specification) and Clause 10 (MAC sublayer functional description), the MLME functions defined in
16 Clause 11 (MLME), and the security functions defined in Clause 12 (Security) except when the functions in
17
Clause 35 (Extremely high throughput (EHT) MAC specification) supersede the functions in Clause 10
18
(MAC sublayer functional description), Clause 11 (MLME), Clause 12 (Security), or Clause 26 (High
19
20 efficiency (HE) MAC specification).
21
22 An EHT STA supports multi-link operation defined in 35.3 (Multi-link operation). MLO allows an AP MLD
23
and a non-AP MLD to set up multiple links between them. A reference model for MLO is described in 4.9.6
24
(Reference model for multi-link operation (MLO)).
25
26
27
35.2 EHT channel access
28
29
30 35.2.1 TXOP
31
32
35.2.1.1 Bandwidth signaling
33
34
35 An EHT STA that is a STA 6G with 320 MHz bandwidth supports transmitting a Control frame in non-HT
36 duplicate format with a bandwidth signaling TA addressed to an EHT STA shall set the TXVECTOR
37
parameter CH_BANDWIDTH_IN_NON_HT signaled via the scrambling sequence and SERVICE
38
field.according to Table 36-1 (TXVECTOR and RXVECTOR parameters)
39
40
41 An EHT STA that is a STA 6G with 320 MHz bandwidth supports transmitting a CTS frame in non-HT
42 duplicate format in response to an RTS frame in non-HT duplicate format with a bandwidth signaling TA
43
addressed to the EHT STA shall set the TXVECTOR parameter CH_BANDWIDTH_IN_NON_HT signaled
44
via the scrambling sequence and SERVICE field according to Table 36-1 (TXVECTOR and RXVECTOR
45
46 parameters).
47
NOTE—In an EHT BSS set up by an EHT AP that has included the Disabled Subchannel Bitmap subfield in the EHT
48
Operation element, both an EHT STA transmitting a Control frame in non-HT duplicate format with a bandwidth
49
signaling TA and an EHT STA responding a Control frame in non-HT duplicate format set the TXVECTOR parameter
50
INACTIVE_SUBCHANNELS of a non-HT duplicate PPDU based on the value indicated in the most recently
51
exchanged Disabled Subchannel Bitmap subfield in the EHT Operation element for that BSS.
52
53
54 35.2.1.2 Triggered TXOP sharing procedure
55
56 35.2.1.2.1 General
57
58
The Triggered TXOP sharing procedure allows an AP to allocate a portion of an obtained TXOP to one
59
60 associated non-AP EHT STA for transmitting one or more non-TB PPDUs.
61
62 An EHT STA with dot11EHTTXOPSharingTFOptionImplemented equal to true shall set one or both of the
63
following subfields in the EHT Capabilities element to 1: the Triggered TXOP Sharing Mode 1 Support
64
65
Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved. 473
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

## 第 474 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 subfield or the Triggered TXOP Sharing Mode 2 Support subfield (see Table 9-401l (Subfields of the EHT
2 MAC Capabilities Information field)).
3
4
An EHT STA with dot11EHTTXOPSharingTFOptionImplemented equal to true shall follow the rules
5
6 defined in 35.2.2 (MU-RTS trigger/CTS frame exchange procedure for EHT STAs) when transmitting or
7 responding to an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame and the additional rules defined in 35.2.1.2.2 (AP behavior)
8 and 35.2.1.2.3 (Non-AP STA behavior).
9
10
An EHT STA that uses information from a received MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame as the most recent basis to
11
12 update its NAV should not reset its NAV after the NAVTimeout has expired (see 10.3.2.4 (Setting and
13 resetting the NAV)) unless the STA receives a CF-End frame that satisfies the conditions in
14 26.2.5 (Truncation of TXOP) and 10.23.2.10 (Truncation of TXOP).
15
16
35.2.1.2.2 AP behavior
17
18
19 An EHT AP may allocate time within an obtained TXOP (see 10.23.2.4 (Obtaining an EDCA TXOP)) to an
20 associated non-AP EHT STA by transmitting an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame as defined in 9.3.1.22.9 (MU-
21
RTS Trigger frame format) parameterized as follows:
22
23 — The MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame, if transmitted by an AP, shall have only one User Info field that is
24 not a Special User Info field.
25
— The User Info field shall be addressed to an associated non-AP STA (i.e., AID12 subfield is set to a
26
27 value in the range of 1 to 2006).
28 — The MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame may contain a Special User Info field as defined in 9.3.1.22.9
29
(MU-RTS Trigger frame format) and 9.3.1.22.3 (Special User Info field)).
30
31
32 The time allocated to the associated non-AP EHT STA is specified in the Allocation Duration subfield in the
33 MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame.
34
35
An EHT AP shall not send an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame with Triggered TXOP Sharing Mode subfield
36
equal to 1 and with the User Info field that is addressed to an associated non-AP STA from which it has not
37
38 received an EHT Capabilities element with the Triggered TXOP Sharing Mode 1 Support subfield equal to
39 1.
40
41
An EHT AP shall not send an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame with Triggered TXOP Sharing Mode subfield
42
equal to 2 and with the User Info field that is addressed to an associated non-AP STA from which it has not
43
44 received an EHT Capabilities element with the Triggered TXOP Sharing Mode 2 Support subfield equal to
45 1.
46
47
If the EHT AP determines that its transmission of an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame to a non-AP EHT STA
48
with the Triggered TXOP Sharing Mode subfield equal to 1 is successful (see 26.2.6.2 (MU-RTS Trigger
49
50 frame transmission)), then the AP shall not transmit any PPDU within the allocated time specified in the
51 MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame unless one of the following conditions are true:
52
— The PPDU carries an immediate response that is solicited by the non-AP STA.
53
54 — The CS mechanism indicates that the medium is idle at the TxPIFS slot boundary after the end of
55 either the transmission of an immediate response frame sent to that STA or the reception of a frame
56
from that STA that did not require an immediate response.
57
58
59 If the EHT AP determines that its transmission of an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame to a non-AP EHT STA
60 with the Triggered TXOP Sharing Mode subfield equal to 2 is successful, then the AP shall not transmit any
61 PPDU within the allocated time specified in the MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame unless one of the following
62
conditions are true:
63
64 — The PPDU carries an immediate response that is solicited by the non-AP STA.
65
474 Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved.

## 第 475 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 — The AP with the TXOP Return Support In TXOP Sharing Mode 2 subfield equal to 1 received a
2 frame from the non-AP STA containing a CAS Control field with the RDG/More PPDU subfield
3
equal to 0.
4
5
6 If the EHT AP determines that the transmission of an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame is successful and the
7 T (see 9.2.5.2 (Setting for single and multiple protection under enhanced distributed channel
TXOP-REMAINING
8
access (EDCA))) after the end of the allocated time is not zero, then the AP may transmit a PPDU after the
9
end of the allocated time if any of the following conditions are satisfied:
10
11 — The medium is determined to be idle by the CS mechanism at the end of the allocated time in which
12 case it may transmit PIFS after the end of the allocated time.
13
14 — The last PPDU transmitted by the AP ended less than PIFS before the end of the allocated time in
15 which case it may transmit SIFS after the end of the last PPDU transmission.
16
— The last PPDU transmitted by the allocated STA to its associated AP did not contain any MPDU
17
soliciting immediate acknowledgement from the AP and ended less than PIFS before the end of the
18
19 allocated time in which case it may transmit SIFS after the end of the last PPDU transmission.
20
21 If the EHT AP determines that the transmission of the MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame is successful and the CS
22
mechanism indicates that the medium is busy at the end of the allocated time, then the AP might transmit
23
after the CS mechanism indicates that the medium is idle at the TxPIFS slot boundary or invoke the backoff
24
25 procedure as described in 10.23.2.2 (EDCA backoff procedure).
26

32 figure shows the case where the AP transmits to another non-AP STA within the allocated time in MU-RTS
33 TXS Trigger frame and within TxPIFS boundary, since the CS mechanism indicates that the medium is idle
34
after the transmission of the last BlockAck frame to STA 1.

35.2.1.2.3 Non-AP STA behavior
29
30
31 After a non-AP EHT STA receives an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame from its associated AP that contains a
32 User Info field that is addressed to it, the STA may transmit one or more non-TB PPDUs within the time
33 allocation signaled in the MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame. The first PPDU of the exchange shall carry a CTS
34
frame transmitted per the rules defined in 26.2.6.3 (CTS frame response to an MU-RTS Trigger frame).
35
36
37 The time allocation shall start when the PHY-RXEND.indication primitive of the PPDU that contains the
38 MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame has occurred.
39
40
The non-AP EHT STA may use the time allocated by the associated AP in an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame,
41
which is addressed to the STA and that has the Triggered TXOP Sharing Mode subfield equal to 2, for the
42
43 transmission of one or more non-TB PPDUs that are addressed to the AP or to another STA. The non-AP
44 EHT STA that received a MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame with TXOP Sharing Mode subfield equal to 2 may
45 transmit a QoS Data or QoS Null frame containing a CAS Control subfield with the RDG/More PPDU
46
subfield equal to 0 to an associated AP from which it has received EHT Capabilities element with the TXOP
47
Return Support In TXOP Sharing Mode 2 subfield set to 1. Otherwise, the STA shall not transmit such frame
48
49 to its associated AP within the allocated time.
50
NOTE 1—For example, the STA might use the allocated time to transmit to a peer STA of a peer-to-peer link or might
51
use the allocated time for noninfrastructure network communication.
52
53
54 The non-AP EHT STA may use the time allocated by the associated AP in the MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame
55 with the Triggered TXOP Sharing Mode subfield value equal to 1 only for the transmission of one or more
56 non-TB PPDUs that are addressed to the AP.
57
58
A non-AP EHT STA addressed by a User Info field in the MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame shall ensure that its
59
60 PPDU transmission(s) and any expected responses fit entirely within the allocated time.
61
62 A non-AP EHT STA that receives a MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame from its associated AP that contains a User
63
Info field addressed to the STA shall update its CWmin[AC], CWmax[AC], AIFSN[AC], and
64
MUEDCATimer[AC] state variables to the values contained in the dot11MUEDCATable, for all the ACs
65
476 Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved.
This is an unapproved IEEE Standards Draft, subject to change.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Georgia Institute of Technology. Downloaded on July 28,2023 at 09:06:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

## 第 477 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 from which at least one QoS Data frame was transmitted successfully in a non-TB PPDU to the AP within
2 the time allocated in the Trigger frame. A QoS Data frame is transmitted successfully by the STA for an AC
3
if it requires immediate acknowledgment and the STA receives an immediate acknowledgment for that
4
frame, or if the QoS Data frame does not require immediate acknowledgment.
5
6
7 If the last non-TB PPDU transmitted to its associated AP within the time allocated in an MU-RTS TXS
8 Trigger frame contains at least one QoS Data frame for an AC that requires immediate acknowledgment, the
9
updated MUEDCATimer[AC] for that AC shall start at the end of the immediate response. If the last
10
transmitted non-TB PPDU to its associated AP does not contain any QoS Data frames for an AC that
11
12 requires immediate acknowledgment, the updated MUEDCATimer[AC] for that AC shall start at the end of
13 the non-TB PPDU.
14
15
After sending the CTS solicited by MU-RTS TXS frame from the associated AP, the STA that sends the
16
responding CTS shall ignore the NAV that is set by the AP within the time allocation signaled in the MU-
17
18 RTS TXS Trigger frame.
19
20 After sending the CTS solicited by the MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame, the STA shall set the Duration/ID field
21
of its frame(s) to a STA that is not the associated AP with a value that indicates a time no later than the
22
ending time of the PPDU carrying the MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame plus the allocated time duration in the
23
24 Allocation Duration field of the soliciting MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame. Within the allocated time by an
25 MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame with Triggered TXOP Sharing Mode subfield equal to 2, the addressed STA by
26 the MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame may transmit QoS Data frames, Management frames and the frames that
27
assists the transmission of QoS Data frames and Management frames, e.g., RTS frame, the frames for
28
sounding.
29
30
NOTE 2—With the Duration rule defined here, the basic NAV of any STA in the same BSS that receives these frames
31
might become zero only at the end of the allocated time if the basic NAV timer is set per the P2P transmission frames
32
during the allocated time period. Hence, these STAs can transmit in the remaining TXOP after the allocated time period
33
due to a zero basic NAV value.
34
35
A non-AP STA addressed by an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame with the triggered TXOP sharing mode equal
36
37 to 2 may either perform PIFS recovery within the allocated time or perform a backoff within the allocated
38 time when the non-TB PPDU transmission is not successful. How it chooses among these options is
39 implementation dependent.
40
41
During the time allocated by an associated AP using an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame, a non-AP STA
42
43 addressed by the MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame shall not transmit non-TB PPDUs occupying subchannels
44 that are not used when sending the CTS frame in response to the MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame.
45
46
A non-AP STA addressed by an MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame shall set the TXVECTOR parameter
47
CH_BANDWIDTH or CH_BANDWIDTH_IN_NON_HT of a non-TB PPDU to be the same or narrower
48
49 than the TXVECTOR parameter CH_BANDWIDTH_IN_NON_HT of the CTS frame that it has responded
50 to the MU-RTS TXS Trigger frame.
51
52
If a 20 MHz subchannel is indicated as a punctured subchannel in the most recently exchanged Disabled
53
Subchannel Bitmap subfield in the EHT Operation element, the corresponding bit in the TXVECTOR
54
55 parameter INACTIVE_SUBCHANNELS shall be set to 1 and the punctured 20 MHz subchannel shall not
56 be used by the non-TB PPDU(s) that is transmitted during the time allocated by the associated AP.
57
58
35.2.2 MU-RTS trigger/CTS frame exchange procedure for EHT STAs
59
60
61 35.2.2.1 MU-RTS Trigger frame transmission
62
63 An EHT AP shall follow the rules defined in 26.2.6.2 (MU-RTS Trigger frame transmission) and the
64
following additional rules to transmit an MU-RTS Trigger frame.
65

## 第 478 页

IEEE P802.11be/D3.0, January 2023
1 If a non-AP EHT STA is addressed in an MU-RTS Trigger frame from an EHT AP and any of the following
2 conditions is met, the User Info field addressed to an EHT STA in the MU-RTS Trigger frame shall be an
3
EHT variant User Info field:
4
5 — The bandwidth of the EHT MU PPDU or non-HT duplicate PPDU carrying the MU-RTS Trigger
6 frame is 320 MHz.
7
— The EHT MU PPDU or non-HT duplicate PPDU carrying the MU-RTS Trigger frame is punctured.
8
9
10 Otherwise, the EHT AP may decide whether the User Info field in the MU-RTS Trigger frame is an HE
11 variant User Info field or an EHT variant User Info field.
12
13
If the B55 in the Common Info field is equal to 0 in an MU-RTS Trigger frame, an EHT AP shall not set the
14
15 B54 in the Common Info field to 1.
16
NOTE—Refer to Table 9-45c (Valid combinations of B54 and B55 in the Common Info field, B39 in the User Info field,
17
and solicited TB PPDU format) on valid combinations of B54 and B55 in the Common Info field, B39 in the User Info
18
field, and User Info field variant.
19
20
21 An MU-RTS Trigger frame shall not solicit a CTS frame from an HE STA within a bandwidth that is
22 indicated by UL BW field in the Common Info field of the MU-RTS Trigger frame and that contains any
23 punctured 20 MHz subchannel. If all the User Info fields in the MU-RTS Trigger frame are HE variant, the
24
PPDU carrying the MU-RTS Trigger frame or any responding CTS frame shall not be punctured.
25
26
27 An MU-RTS Trigger frame may be carried in an EHT MU PPDU if all intended recipients are non-AP EHT
28 STAs. If the MU-RTS Trigger frame is carried in an EHT MU PPDU, then the EHT AP shall set the
29 TXVECTOR parameter EHT_PPDU_TYPE of the EHT MU PPDU to 1.
30
31
An EHT AP that transmits a PPDU carrying an MU-RTS Trigger frame shall not puncture other subchannels
32
33 in addition to those indicated in the Disabled Subchannel Bitmap subfield in the EHT Operation element.
34
35 35.2.2.2 CTS frame response to an MU-RTS Trigger frame
36
37
A non-AP EHT STA shall follow the rules defined in 35.5.2.3 (Non-AP STA behavior for UL MU
38
39 operation) to determine whether the EHT STA is addressed by the HE variant User Info field or an EHT
40 variant User Info field in an MU-RTS Trigger frame.
41
42
If an EHT STA is addressed by an HE variant User Info field in an MU-RTS Trigger frame, the EHT STA
43
shall follow the rules defined in 26.2.6 (MU-RTS Trigger/CTS frame exchange procedure) in transmitting a
44
45 response.
46
47 If the EHT STA is addressed by an EHT variant User Info field in the MU-RTS Trigger frame, the EHT STA
48
shall follow the rules defined in 26.2.6 (MU-RTS Trigger/CTS frame exchange procedure) in transmitting a
49
response, except that UL MU CS condition shall be determined based on the rules defined in 35.5.2.4 (UL
50
51 MU CS mechanism for EHT STAs). The CTS frame in response to the MU-RTS Trigger frame shall be sent
52 in the RU indicated by the EHT variant User Info field, excluding any punctured 20 MHz subchannel
53 indicated in the Disabled Subchannel Bitmap subfield in the EHT Operation element.
54
55
35.2.3 Intra-BSS and inter-BSS PPDU classification for EHT STA
56
57
58 An EHT STA shall follow the rules defined in 26.2.2 (Intra-BSS and inter-BSS PPDU classification) to
59 classify intra-BSS and inter-BSS PPDU, except that a classifying STA shall classify a received PPDU as an
60
inter-BSS PPDU if the PPDU is an EHT MU PPDU that is not in an EHT SU transmission with the
61
RXVECTOR parameter UPLINK_FLAG equal to 0, and the classifying STA is an AP.
62
63
64
65
478 Copyright © 2023 IEEE. All rights reserved.
